# Any comments?

_916 messages · 26 participants · 2006-03 → 2006-05_

---

### Any comments?

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-17T01:53:43+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net>`

```
I thought the group might find the following interesting:

PUBLISHER SAVES A MILLION MIGRATING OFF MAINFRAME
By Joe Spurr, News Writer

Simon & Schuster Inc. plans to save $1 million a year on hardware costs and 
licensing fees as a result of migrating off its old IBM mainframes.
The New York City-based publishing house is in the middle of a three-part 
changeover from its Cobol-crunching IBM 9672-RB5 to an Intel-based Unisys 
ES7000 running Microsoft SQL Server.

The decision to switch came about two years ago when officials, already 
cognizant of high mainframe costs, began to realize the growth of their 
company was beginning to conflict with the size of its shoes, so to speak. 
But with stability a concern and the looming task of converting five million 
lines of code, the pressure was on to make the right move.

"The challenge of rewriting everything -- it was a daunting task," said Mike 
Grant, Simon & Schuster vice president of application development. "But we 
needed to do something . We are running flat out on our machine right now. 
We're almost 90% to 100% capacity all the time."

Mainframes have been around forever and know how to get things done their 
own way, but, especially for smaller companies, the elegance that makes them 
useful can also be unwieldy to upkeep.

More on mainframes
Integration specialists ride mainframe migration wave

Graying workforce endangers your mainframes

Advantages of the data center's elder statesman -- stability, the ability to 
scale and flex in the face of server sprawl and new workloads -- can be 
offset by premium hardware costs. Complicated architecture that goes back 20 
years, combined with a skills base in decline, also means potentially high 
labor expenses.

Grant said he was surfing for answers when he stumbled across 
Tokyo-headquartered Fujitsu Software Corp., a specialist in assisting 
migrations like CICS applications to .NET and mainframe batch applications 
to Windows.

The latter was a crucial difference when proofs of concept were drawn and 
discussed with both Fujitsu and U.K.-based Micro Focus Ltd., a similar 
migration outfit, Grant said.

"We were very impressed with both, but the problem we saw with Micro 
Focus -- which may have since changed -- was a lot of emulation software," 
Grant said. "We wanted to standardize our .NET environment, and the Fujitsu 
model more closely aligned with our vision, in terms of running Visual 
Studio and having it play well with VB [Visual Basic] and C#."

After recently transferring over its royalty system -- and working with 
India-based Tata Consultancy Services Ltd. to convert its DB2 to SQL 
server -- the final hulking shift comes soon for Simon & Schuster -- moving 
its main order processing system. But the true test will come late summer, a 
traditional season of frenzy that will test both nerves and networks as 
publishers push to move product in time for schools reopening.

"Until then, we'll still be kind of nervous -- last year our mainframe was 
so backed up that often systems weren't coming up until 10 the next 
morning," Grant said. "But right now we're seeing jobs perform so much 
better. We are really seeing some performance gains, we've been pleased, and 
all the vendors are very confident."

Indeed, Andrew Mackenzie, strategic alliance manager for Fujitsu Software, 
sees no gray in the environment.

"The thing about migrations is you're either a hero or a goat," Mackenzie 
said. "With mission-critical apps, it can be very scary to turn off the 
mainframe. But five years ago there was a lot more risk. There's been 
relentless performance gains in microprocessors. It comes down to if I'm the 
CIO who's got this thing that's sucking up 60% of my budget -- you're either 
going to migrate or drown."

IT research firm Gartner Inc. recently predicted 80% of today's smaller 
mainframe environments will move away by 2010. And though the sentiment of 
that forecast is nothing new -- bashing mainframes is practically a pastime 
in some circles. Detractors today predict doom in the face of the system's 
recent resurgence, exemplified by double-digit revenue gains since 2003 of 
the IBM's zSeries.

"If someone hasn't looked at a mainframe in a while, they should look 
again," said zSeries product director Collette Martin. "There's an awful lot 
of flexibility. And for the customers who are very small, with older 
mainframes, those who are struggling in that respect but looking for a value 
proposition to move forward, it is considerably less expensive to run 
today's mainframes."


No, I didn't write it... :-) (The Author's name is on the byline)

I'm still backing 2015 for the end of COBOL... :-)

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> I thought the group might find the following interesting:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
[...]

Well, we are seeing a lot of interest in our Java-based client-server 
app from traditional mainframe shops.  Many of these shops want to 
minimize any further COBOL development, or control it in a manner more 
conducive to implementation of Sarbanes-Oxley.

However, the usual telcos and banks we talk with are not planning this 
sort of drastic change.  They would rather find solutions that bridge 
the mainframe and client-server world.
```

#### ↳ Re: Any comments?

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-03-16T20:02:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> Simon & Schuster Inc. plans to save $1 million a year on hardware costs and 
> licensing fees as a result of migrating off its old IBM mainframes.
> The New York City-based publishing house is in the middle of a three-part 
> changeover from its Cobol-crunching IBM 9672-RB5 to an Intel-based Unisys 
> ES7000 running Microsoft SQL Server.

Those ES7000 servers rock!  It's basically their mainframe-sized Intel 
platform.  I believe they support up to 16 processors in one box (it may 
be 32 processors by now).

They're very cool - and, I believe they also run Unisys's A-series (LX) 
operating system.  For those folks, they can get cheaper hardware *and* 
no conversion...  :)

(Us 9-bit 2200 (IX) folks are out of luck on those - although the new 
Dorado series boxes are very quick.  I saw our production machine 
running at 1,600 MIPS this week!  (Of course, I also saw them adjust the 
configuration down to 1,200...))

They haven't left us out in the cold, though.  Now, instead of paying 
for the capability of the hardware, we pay for the MIPS we actually use. 
  It's significantly cheaper, while allowing us to have the power on 
demand if we need it.  I'm waiting for us to get that on our development 
boxes, so we can do system-wide rebuilds in an hour.  (We did it in 
about an hour and a half on a 150 MIP machine - but on our normal 
development machine, it takes about 8 hours, usually done overnight.)

More than you ever wanted to know about Unisys, probably...  :)  I just 
had to give props to the ES7000's - I wish I could get one for my house! 
  (although I have no idea what I'd do with it...  They run Linux too - 
maybe that would be my terminal server!)
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't want to get into that all over again do we :-)

It was a recent addition - don't sweat your recent observation.  :)

IMO, though, that's the beauty of that quote.  It's doesn't even attempt 
to get into "all that" - it only questions the rationality of two 
comparative viewpoints.  And, there's not an answer - some might feel 
that the first man is less rational.

Kind of like that Robert Frost poem - "I took the road less traveled by, 
and that has made all the difference."  Most everyone thinks that this 
means that he's happy with that decision - but, that's not what it says. 
  It only says that it changed his life.

(And note, too - it's not ridiculing the lack of belief in (a) God, just 
inquiring as to the rationality of being *offended* by a deity that 
someone doesn't believe exists.)

> Patience this end - let's see what observations you come back with about 
> the Islamic world in terms of their concept of justice, democracy and 
> religion when you have finished your tour of duty. I'm going to forecast 
> you will probably be cynical and might use words like "Bewilderment", 
> "Exasperation" etc.

Well, seeing as I'm pretty cynical of Islam from the get-go, I doubt my 
time there will change that.  :)  But, I shall report back here.

(heh - this'll teach folks to post a thread with the subject "*Any* 
comments?"...  ;>  )
```

```
Richard wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
>>> "Who is more irrational?  A man who believes in a God he doesn't see, or
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> discussing being offended or not by _your_ actions.

But why would worshipping a non-existent deity be offensive?  (IMO, 
there are lots of religions that do this.  :>  In your opinion, they all 
do!)

>>> Are you offended by the hundreds of deities that you don't believe in ?
>>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> what books you could read or what TV programs could be shown ?

Depends on how it's done.  First off, I don't think government should be 
in the business of education, and I don't send my children to government 
schools.  But, I wouldn't have a problem with them learning *about* 
other religions.

Books/TV are a different matter.  Again, "control" is a broad term.  I 
don't think that the government should control those sorts of things 
with an iron fist.  However, the need does exist (as has been 
demonstrated) for some protection for children.  I'm against any books 
or films being outright banned; but, reasonable controls (such as the 
movie rating system we have in place here, though it's becoming useless 
(every movie *can't* be PG-13)) are needed.

(I'd rather not get more detailed in that, because it would only divert 
this thread into a long, long, long social issue discussion, which has 
occurred here before.  Of course I think my own feelings are reasonable; 
but, if you don't, we'll just have to agree to disagree.)
```

```
Interesting that you think that you can state my opinion for me. In
fact I see that Rastas was a real person that was deified and
worshipped (did I mention that Ras Tafari presented my Grandfather with
a lion skin cloak in Aden in 1922), Hirihito was officially a 'god'
until the allies made him not be in 1945, Chin was a real person and a
deity.

In fact I see no reason at all why all the deities were not real
people: warlords, chiefs, ancestors, charasmatic leaders. Jehovah even
specified that the Jews must build him a house that he may dwell
amongst them.  Sounds like a real person to me.  Dead now of course,
just like all the others. The stories are greatly exagerated, but if
you want to believe some fantasy mythology, that's OK.

At the time, of course, they were worshipping someone who was real and
alive, or perhaps Jehovah was more of a dynasty, a succession of
'lords' (cf the House of Lords in the UK - territorial leaders with
inheretable titles).

No, I don't think that you are worshipping something that _never_
existed, just as the Rastafarians worshipped a real person, but he
doesn't exist any more, their church keeps up the pretense. The Jews,
of course, have a real reason to maintain the covenant, and thus keep
up the idea that the other party to the contract is still around: their
very nationhood and land depends on it.


> (I'd rather not get more detailed in that, because it would only divert
> this thread into a long, long, long social issue discussion, which has

Well I wasn't actually interested in your answer, only in having you
think about what would happen if, say, the muslims had a say in what
the laws were.
```

- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142751252.183031.290780@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <tpht12tf11uedt22ev27un42vd44mqamf4@4ax.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> We do see examples of made-up religions.

Depending on what you actually mean by 'made-up', they are _all_
made-up.

Some may have been made-up over thousands of years, others much more
recently.

>  We also see examples of
> made-up stories about gods.   Without anything to go on, I would bet
> half of the gods started off as fiction.

Ceratinly they are fictionalised, but even characters in novels are
often based on real people. There are many examples where the link
between the fictional character and the real original are established,
such as Santa Claus is based on St. Nicolas the Bishop of Myra. People
a few thousand years ago had the same abilities, thoughts, intelligence
as us today so I don't see why they didn't have the same sort of story
telling.

Children are indoctrinated with the Tooth Fairy, Santa Claus, Wombles,
King Arthur, Superman, or many other myths, sometimes they work out
what is fiction and sometimes they continue to believe it because it is
a 'really nice idea'.
```

```
On 20 Mar 2006 14:09:30 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> We do see examples of made-up religions.
>
>Depending on what you actually mean by 'made-up', they are _all_
>made-up.

There is disagreement here.   Many people believe all but one religion
is made-up.   But even those who believe most religions are true
(which is hard to understand), believe that some are made-up.

>Some may have been made-up over thousands of years, others much more
>recently.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>often based on real people. 

Still, I would bet that half of the gods started off not based upon
real people.    Something like the Coyote god would be an avatar of
coyotes.

>There are many examples where the link
>between the fictional character and the real original are established,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>telling.

That happens too.

>Children are indoctrinated with the Tooth Fairy, Santa Claus, Wombles,
>King Arthur, Superman, or many other myths, sometimes they work out
>what is fiction and sometimes they continue to believe it because it is
>a 'really nice idea'.

I don't believe Superman is based upon a real person.

I suspect fiction and movies are a big factor in secularizing the
world.    Movies that tell kids how important Belief in Santa is
backfire when they learn that they have been fooled.

When I saw _The Right Stuff_, my son sat in the front of the theatre.
Driving home, talking about it we discovered that he assumed it was
all fiction.
```

```

Howard Brazee wrote:

> There is disagreement here.   Many people believe all but one religion
> is made-up.   But even those who believe most religions are true
> (which is hard to understand), believe that some are made-up.

There is no doubt that many religions are made up. Especially
Scientology which is based on ElRon's rather poor SciFi books.

> Still, I would bet that half of the gods started off not based upon
> real people.    Something like the Coyote god would be an avatar of
> coyotes.

Yes, you are quite correct. Many may be completely fictional.

> I don't believe Superman is based upon a real person.

:-O   That just ruined my day.

> I suspect fiction and movies are a big factor in secularizing the
> world.

_Knowing_ that it _is_ fiction does, yes.

I know that Scientology was started on a $10 bet at a SciFi conference
according to Van Voght (or was it Arthur C) who took the other half,
and was just the SciFi stories made mystical.

To many the fiction is much more attractive than reality and the more
you know about anything the more interesting it is. I do find that the
more I learn about all the various religions the more I see how all of
them may have come about.

For example the Canaanites had their El and a pantheon of other gods
that were the 'Elohim' - the family or house of El. This was most
likely a ruling class of lords (or warlords) and spun off two sub
groups under Jehovah and the family of Baal (Baalim), one of which was
Baal Zebub.

Jehovah managed to get a ready made population contracted to him for
his new territory and formed a new nation by overruning his neibours.
Nothing new there, happed all the time.
```

```
On Sat, 18 Mar 2006 20:46:08 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>But why would worshipping a non-existent deity be offensive?  (IMO, 
>there are lots of religions that do this.  :>  In your opinion, they all 
>do!)

Several reasons.    For instance, some people worship a being that
they believe is omnipotent, but whom they believe allows the vast
majority of people to be tortured beyond all understanding forever and
ever without hope of parole - for being fooled.

How can this evil *not* be offensive?

Others use their worship as an excuse to hate (by picking and choosing
which parts of the scriptures matter to them).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-20T16:25:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9IATf.7409$A4.4297@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 18 Mar 2006 20:46:08 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> How can this evil *not* be offensive?

    It's interesting (to me anyway) to consider what is the actual source of 
the offense in the above scenario. Is the person actually being offended by 
a God which they don't believe exists? Or are they offended by the fact that 
someone believes in this God? Or does the offense rather come from the fact 
that people believe in the teachings of this God, and it this belief of the 
teachings that is offensive, regardless of whether the God actually exists 
or not?

    I looked up the definition of "offend", and two of the definitions I saw 
seem to apply (there were a few others that didn't): "to cause dislike, 
anger, or vexation" and "to cause to feel vexation or resentment usually by 
violation of what is proper or fitting". Under these definitions, people 
could be offended by anything. If the fact that the sky is blue angers or 
vexes me, then it is said that I am offended by the blueness of the sky.

    So if someone believes in something, and as a result you are vexed, what 
is it exactly was it that made you vexed? The belief itself? The fact that 
someone belives it? The behaviour of that person as a side effect of those 
beliefs? The fact that someone convinced someone else of this belief? The 
fact that you don't share this belief?

    - Oliver
```

```
On Mon, 20 Mar 2006 16:25:41 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>> How can this evil *not* be offensive?
>
>    It's interesting (to me anyway) to consider what is the actual source of 
>the offense in the above scenario. Is the person actually being offended by

No.

>Or are they offended by the fact that 
>someone believes in this God? Or does the offense rather come from the fact 
>that people believe in the teachings of this God, and it this belief of the 
>teachings that is offensive, regardless of whether the God actually exists 
>or not?

Almost.   Someone can believe in an evil without being offensive.  

Worshiping an omnipotent being who they believe allows the vast
majority of us to be tortured because we have been fooled is
offensive.   Proclaming how good their God is who allows us to be
tortured for not being like them is offensive.    Saying it's our own
fault because we believe differently and thus we deserve everlasting
torture is offensive.
```

```
"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message
news:9IATf.7409$A4.4297@clgrps12...
[snip]
>     It's interesting (to me anyway) to consider what is the actual source
of
> the offense in the above scenario. Is the person actually being offended
by
> a God which they don't believe exists? Or are they offended by the fact
that
> someone believes in this God? Or does the offense rather come from the
fact
> that people believe in the teachings of this God, and it this belief of
the
> teachings that is offensive, regardless of whether the God actually exists
> or not?
>
>     I looked up the definition of "offend", and two of the definitions I
saw
> seem to apply (there were a few others that didn't): "to cause dislike,
> anger, or vexation" and "to cause to feel vexation or resentment usually
by
> violation of what is proper or fitting". Under these definitions, people
> could be offended by anything. If the fact that the sky is blue angers or
> vexes me, then it is said that I am offended by the blueness of the sky.

Mr Wong, I am offended by your use of "God" instead of
"god", as a "violation of what is proper and fitting". In your
first use, "a God", the indefinite article "a" implies "any one
of some class or group" (RHCD). This use, and the later
"this God" and "the God", requires lower case, while a
specific reference to the alleged Supreme Being, as God,
uses upper case. <g>
```

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
>
>     It's interesting (to me anyway) to consider what is the actual source of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or not?

That was exactly the point that I was attempting to get Lxi to see, but
he didn't seem capable of noticing.

>     I looked up the definition of "offend", and two of the definitions I saw
> seem to apply (there were a few others that didn't): "to cause dislike,
> anger, or vexation" and "to cause to feel vexation or resentment usually by
> violation of what is proper or fitting".

I am not sure that I was offended by it, but there is one story that I
found disburbing, though it does explain why some may prefer to believe
in some sort of religion.

In England a youth had, as often he had done, got drunk one night at a
party and then drove home. This time he smashed the car and killed his
girlfriend. His sister was a muslim and persuaded him to turn to her
'god' for help in dealing with his feelings of guilt. His
interpretation of muslim (which may or may not be true) is that 'Allah
has written everything that has been and will be', therefore the crash
was preordained and his girlfriends death was part of some master plan
and so _it_wasn't_his_fault_.

It also seems to me that Dyanetics and Scientology are, for many, also
about it not being their fault that they are failures.
```

```
I know people who have been helped (physically) by Dianetics. But then,

The point is, if people are prepared to give you money to make them feel 
better, and part of making them feel better is to tell them it isn't their 
fault they feel bad, then where's the harm?

They come out with less cash, but feeling much better. Isn't that what 
happens when you go to a standard doctor? (If you're lucky.... :-))

If I was bothered by this, I'd campaign to ban Scientology. Or I'd become 
one. :-) As I have done neither, it obviously doesn't offend me. (See

Pete.
```

```
wrote:
```

```
fundamental perversion of both the US in general and the US military in 
particular, outside the funeral of a military casualty of the war in Iraq in 
Asheville, NC, in about an hour.

As for me, I side (and have ridden) with the Patriot Guard Riders on this 
issue (see www.patriotguard.org ).  They began staging about an hour ago for

from the WBC do indeed keep their word as published on their website, the
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-03-22T22:54:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> eternal torment.

This is true.  It's sad that exercising our right to kick their butts up 
and down the sidewalk would probably land us in jail.  :(  I'm not 
disputing their right to do what they do.  However, in the case of folks 
like this, I have a few problems...

First, they're using the name of my particular religious denomination. 
I'm a member of Eastmont Baptist Church, and I've been leading music the 
last few months at Wares Ferry Road Baptist Church.  The beliefs of both 
these congregations are nowhere near those of the Westboro Baptist 
Church - but by the name, who knows?

Next, these WBC jokers have the Bible.  Yet, they're willingly twisting 
it to support their own hateful views.  Many times, a religion focused 
on one aspect of Christianity discards the context for their proof texts.

Just because I'm leaving tomorrow for the rest of the month (and 
therefore will see everyone's responses almost two weeks from now), 
let's deconstruct each of their points, gleaned from the home page at 
http://www.godhatesfags.com/ .  (For those reading, and don't want the 
point-by-point stuff for fear that it'll be to proselytizing ;), it's 
indented - just skip past it.)  Also, some of these refutations apply to 
more than one of their points - I'll not mention them multiple times

  - "Sodomy is an abominable sin, worthy of death"  The verse they quote 
is from Leviticus (the old law), and they quote Paul from Romans also 
referring to this law.  These aren't misquotes, but taken out of 
context.  Proverbs 20:16-19 
http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Proverbs%206%20:16-19;&version=9; 
lists 7 abmoniations to the Lord - homosexuality (or just sodomy, as 
they claim) is not listed among these.  Why not godhatesliars or 
godhatesmurderers?

  - "God hates all workers of iniquity"  I'm not so sure they'd be so 
vehement about this one if they turned over to Romans 3:10 - "There is 
*none* righteous, no, not one..." (emphasis mine)  If God hates all 
workers of iniquity, He must hate us *all*!

  - "Jesus Christ died only for those who believe"  Their supporting 
verse for this is, believe it or not, John 3:16.  They're focusing on 
the latter part of the verse, that says "that whosoever believeth in 
Him..."  But this is not consistent with the interpretation given later 
in the New Testament.  Revelation 22:17 specifically says "And whoseover 
will, let him take of the water of life freely."  Christ died, and 
offers His atonement for anyone who will accept it.

  - "Only God's Elect have the capability to believe"  Election and 
predestination date back to Calvin.  Several times, this concept of 
God's Elect is mentioned, mostly in the New Testament; it follows the 
thought process along the lines of "since God is omniscient, He knows 
who will be saved and who will not; if He knows they will be saved, then 
they will be; therefore, I have no responsibility for sharing the Gospel 
with them."  Some (as WBC does) take it to the next step - "since *I* am 
part of the elect and *they* obviously are not, they are less than me." 
  This isn't a proper view - rather, Jesus told His disciples to "go ye 
into *all* the world and preach the gospel to every creature" (Acts 1:8, 
emphasis mine).  Also, as John is setting the stage for his gospel, he 
writes "But as many as received Him..."  Election does not equal no 
responsibility - rather, election equals *more* responsibility.

  - "Sodomites are proud of their sin ("Gay Pride")...and you can't 
repent of something you're proud of"  Leaving aside the sweeping 
generalities of "all sodomites are proud" (they're not) and not 
repenting of something you're proud of (an experience I have had with 
some code I wrote)...  In their support Scriptures, they cite several 
passages where God declares His judgment on unrepentant peoples.  The 
easiest refutation of this is the story of Ninevah - a proud, heathen 
society that Jonah ran away from, and preached to not thinking they 
could ever repent, ended up repenting and following the Lord.  This also 
violates the principle that Paul sets up in Romans 12:19 - "vengeance is 
Mine: I will repay, saith the Lord".  It is not up to us to decide to 
write off anyone.

  - This next one is big, so I'm breaking it up...
     - "The sodomites only hope is to have the unambiguous truth 
preached to them"  OK, I'm with them on this one so far.  Romans 10:17 
says "So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the Word of God."
     - Back to the WBC quote - "and perhaps God will soften their hearts 
and grant them repentance to depart from their sin and name the name of 
Christ"  Here's where they start going off-track with this one.  The 
order is backwards - come to Christ, *then* work on your life after 
that.  Throughout Jesus' ministry, He was a "come as you are" 
evangelist.  4-time divorcee living with a 5th man?  Come.  (John 
4:7-29)  Stealing money from people under the pretense of collecting 
taxes?  Come.  (Luke 19:1-10)  Demon-possessed prostitute?  Come.  (Luke 
7:36-50)  (This last one - a cross-comparison of the gospels indicates 
that this was Mary Magdelene, who had been both a prostitute, and demon 
possessed at one time.)
     - One more WBC visit...  "Not very likely, though, since God has 
given them up"  Their support of this is Romans 1:24-28, which is part 
of Paul's anti-homosexuality treatise at the beginning of the letter. 
This could apply to some, but definitely does not apply to all - and, in 
Paul's letter, he addressed all forms of moral depravity, not just 
homosexuality.  Again, the key word in that verse is *God* - He's the 
one that's supposed to do the "giving up", if there's any to be done.

  - The last one - yay!
     - "There is a hell where all impenitent sinners will reside for all 
eternity"  Funny how when they get to the bottom of their list, they 
actually start making more sense than when they started.  *This* part of 
the statement is biblical.
     - "That includes sodomites (called "dogs")"  This is wrong.  The 
basis of who gets into heaven and who gets into hell is *not* based on 
whether they were queer or not.  It is solely based on their acceptance 
of Jesus' atonement for their sins.  Revelation 21:27 - "And there shall 
in no wise enter into it...but they which are written in the Lamb's book 
of life."  Also, sodomies are *not* called dogs *anywhere* in the Bible. 
  Back to Revelation 22 - verse 15 says "For without are dogs, and 
sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever 
loveth and maketh a lie."  Nowhere is the word "dogs" associated with 
any particular breed of sinner.

- - - End of point-by-point - - -

As you can see, there is much in the Bible that they have disregarded. 
Through their willful twisting of the Scripture, they give Christians 
(and Baptists) a bad name.  But, believe it or not, the Bible has 
something to say about people who do what they're doing as well.  I'll 
cite Revelation 22:18-19...

"For I testify unto every man that heareth the words of the prophecy of 
this book, If any man shall add unto these things, God shall add unto 
him the plagues that are written in this book: and if any man shall take 
away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God shall take away 
his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city, and from the 
things which are written in this book."

God doesn't take too kindly to His Word being tinkered with...

>> thanks to you and all those who volunteer for that very honorable task.
> 
> You're most welcome.   I hold that nobody who dies in the service of our 
> country deserves less.

I don't really have a response for that - I just quoted it because it 
was so well-said.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-23T08:33:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET...

>     - One more WBC visit...  "Not very likely, though, since God has given 
> them up"  Their support of this is Romans 1:24-28, which is part of Paul's 
> anti-homosexuality treatise at the beginning of the letter.

Citations below are KJV unless otherwise stated.

I have studied Romans 1:18ff in some significant depth, and I disagree with 
your conclusion that the tenor of this entire passage is an 
anti-homosexuality treatise.

It is my opinion that Romans 1:18-22 describes the *cause* -- idolatry, 
after having heard the truth -- and the remainder of the chapter describes 
the consequences.  While I have no question at all that Paul found 
homosexual activity abominable -- as would be expected of an observant Jew, 
based in particular on Leviticus 18 and 20 -- I do not hold that this is the 
focus of this part of the letter.  *Paganism* is, particularly the engaging 
in *pagan* practices *after having heard and absorbed the Message.

Moreover, it is not my experience that every homosexual person is "being 
filled with all unrighteousness, fornication [=TR "porneia", only in late 
manuscripts, not in any early ones], wickedness, covetousness, malice; full 
of envy, murder, debate, deceit, malignity; whisperers, backbiters, haters 
of God, despiteful, proud, boasters, inventors of evil things, disobedient 
to parents, without understanding, covenant-breakers, without natural 
affection, implacable, unmerciful ..." (Romans 1:29-31).   In all honesty, 
I've seen more than one of these characteristics in people who would claim 
on a stack of Bibles and on their grandmother's grave that they were devout 
and dutiful Christians and for whom neither homosexual thoughts nor actions 
are any part of their frame of reference.

Some scholars contend that the activities and the results described in this 
passage actually relate to pagan ritual temple homosexual prostitution.  I 
think that's likely, given the focus on *pagan practices* at the beginning 
of this section, but I wouldn't state that as fact, or even entirely clear.

Some also argue that "against nature" (v.26) in Koine Greek never refers to 
"nature" in the abstract but rather to the *particular* nature *of an 
individual*.  For that reason, the passage does not apply to those whose 
individual *nature* is homosexual, only to those who are fundamentally 
heterosexual people *engaging in homosexual acts*.  There are a number of 
cases in the New Testament -- in particular in Paul's writings -- where 
"para physin" (TR) ("against nature" or, in my opinion, more accurately 
"alongside what is natural") is clearly referring to the characteristics of 
the specific individual; the concept of "Nature" in the abstract wasn't part 
of Paul's world.  I'm not convinced that these scholars are right, but I'm 
also not convinced that they're flat wrong.

But most importantly, I think the key to understanding of this entire 
section is "For this cause, ... " ( = TR "dia touto ...") at the top of 
v.26.  What is the referent for "this"?  I believe it's v.21 -- "because 
that, when  they knew God, they glorified him not as God, neither were 
thankful; but became vain in their imaginations, and their foolish heart was 
darkened."  V.22 talks about the resultant *attitudes*, v.23 talks about 
making *idols*,  v.24 talks about general profligacy, and v.25 summarizes 
this as worshiping creation rather than the creator.  I believe the issue of 
homosexual behavior as such is *only*  raised specifically at the end of 
v.26 and in the whole of v.27.

It strikes me that the other two Pauline passages taken to be indicative of 
Paul's condemnation of homosexuality -- 1 Cor 6:9 "... nor effeminate, nor 
abusers of themselves with mankind ... " (TR " ... oute malakoi, oute 
aresenokoitai ..." and 1 Tim 1:10 (TR "arsenokoitais") speak more to the 
expectation among some cultures of the time that a powerful man would have a 
young "protege" that exchanged sexual favors for the privilege of being 
among powerful men.   This is particularly clear in the first of these two 
passages, where TR "malakoi" has to do with soft, luxurious clothing -- I 
would contend this refers to the "protege" -- and "arsenokoitai" is a word 
that appears nowhere else in Greek literature save in Paul's writings that 
is fairly coarse, and in fact ambiguous in construction, but *most probably* 
refers to those who maintain "proteges".

In sum, I believe the primary emphasis of the *whole* of Romans 1:18ff is 
walking away from God after having come to some understanding of spiritual 
matters.

> This could apply to some, but definitely does not apply to all - and, in 
> Paul's letter, he addressed all forms of moral depravity, not just 
> homosexuality.  Again, the key word in that verse is *God* - He's the one 
> that's supposed to do the "giving up", if there's any to be done.

I agree, but again, that homosexual activity (*and not homosexuality per 
se* -- the distinction is an important one!) is one of the things Paul lists 
as a no-no does *not* render this passage as an "anti-homosexual treatise", 
as you have characterized it.

>  - The last one - yay!
>     - "There is a hell where all impenitent sinners will reside for all 
> eternity"  Funny how when they get to the bottom of their list, they 
> actually start making more sense than when they started.  *This* part of 
> the statement is biblical.

Well, I'm not convinced that it's appropriate for *ANY* of us to treat that 
as cause for rejoicing.  Is *that* biblical?

>     - "That includes sodomites (called "dogs")"  This is wrong.   ... 
> Nowhere is the word "dogs" associated with any particular breed of sinner.

As I recall it, they're wrong in another way on this one.  What I think 
they're referring to is Deuteronomy 23:18:  "Thou shalt not bring the hire 
of a whore, or the price of a dog, into the house of the LORD thy God for 
any vow: for even both of these are abomination unto the LORD thy God."   I 
have read elsewhere that "dog" is actually a euphemism in this passage for a 
*male prostitute*.  The reference here, again, is to *prostitution* -- in 
the first case heterosexual prostitution, in the second case homosexual 
prostitution.  The prohibitions are *equally strong*, and perhaps more 
importantly the passage strikes me as indicating that occurrences are pretty 
much *equally likely*.   The passage doesn't make much sense otherwise, and 
Philippians 3:2 and Revelations 22:15 both seem to me to support that 
understanding of the OT passage.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-03-25T23:54:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3v1c2299l350gro9b4ic44if2e8mv9gg2d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Thu, 23 Mar 2006 08:33:27 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
>news:b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>in *pagan* practices *after having heard and absorbed the Message.

I agree for this passage that you have the correct emphasis and that
homosexuality in this passage is the result of sin rather than the
sin.  Whether Paul was correct in this view is another discussion.
>
>Moreover, it is not my experience that every homosexual person is "being 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>are any part of their frame of reference.

I interpret this passage in Romans and the other two as giving a list
of sins of which homosexual practices were among them.  This would
mean that gossips (NRSV translation if I recall correctly) are every
bit as sinful as those who engage in homosexual practices.  My
contention is the latter have done far more harm to the church body
than those who engage in homosexual practices.  
>
>Some scholars contend that the activities and the results described in this 
>passage actually relate to pagan ritual temple homosexual prostitution.  I 
>think that's likely, given the focus on *pagan practices* at the beginning 
>of this section, but I wouldn't state that as fact, or even entirely clear.

What is Paul's view on sex outside of marriage?  If my understanding
of him believing it to be sinful is correct, and if my understanding
of Christ defining marriage as being between a man and a woman
(polygamy not allowed?) then Paul would believe the activity to be
sinful.  

I am finally waking up and saving these threads because they are good
serious theological lay discussion from a number of points of view
(Christian of varying heritages as was as agnostic/atheist).  
>
>> rest snipped only to save band width
>
>    -Chuck Stevens 
>
```

- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET...

> Omnipotent doesn't equal rude.  In Revelation 3:20, God says "Behold, I 
> stand at the door, and knock: if any man hear my voice, and open the door, 
> I will come in to him, and will sup with him, and he with me."

Interesting passage, that.  I don't see very many qualifications -- words 
like "unless" or "until", for example, in it.  What does one have to do 
besides open the door in response?

And that brings up a point.  Whenever I see a passage directed to a 
particular group, I have to wonder why it is directed specifically to that 
group and not to the world at large.   Should that which is divinely 
directed explicitly to the Laodiceans be taken to heart with no less, or 
even more, fervor by the Philadelphians, or the Ephesians, or for that 
matter by the Anaheimians or the Montgomerians?    On what scriptural basis?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 18)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-03T21:35:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> matter by the Anaheimians or the Montgomerians?    On what scriptural basis?

While I agree in principle, there are a couple of reasons I think this 
particular one applies to all...

  - This is in the New Testament.  Generally, most precepts given in the 
NT are applicable to everyone.  Are we to ignore "Love thy neighbor as 
thyself" because Jesus said it to a crowd in Israel, and that's 
obviously not us?  :)

  - The letters to the seven churches (from which that verse was taken) 
dealt with a particular problem in each church.  Rather than only being 
good for that geographical region, I rather believe that they are good 
principles for dealing with the problems they were meant to address.

Also, the "world at large" is the target audience of the Bible.  Some 
things in it are recorded simply to pass along history - I don't think 
God needs any of us to stand between two pillars and push the way Samson 
did.  Others give us general precepts for living - I'm thinking here 
about Proverbs (among other passages).

When Jesus forgave prostitution and adultery, this gives us an example 
to follow (which is also applicable to WBC).  When He condemned those 
who were preying on worshippers, we can get an insight into how God 
feels about those who are supposed to lead His people, but lead them 
astray.  When Paul told the Thessalonians to "rejoice evermore" and 
"recompense no man evil for evil", I don't think that those things are 
limited to the Thessalonica city limits.

As far as a Scriptural basis, as Paul wrote to Timothy, "All scripture 
is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for 
reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness..."  (2 
Timothy 3:16)  Why would God have preserved these instructions if they 
were only directed at a few thousand people?  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T10:21:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
>> news:636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET...
>>

>  - This is in the New Testament.  Generally, most precepts given in the NT 
> are applicable to everyone.  Are we to ignore "Love thy neighbor as 
> thyself" because Jesus said it to a crowd in Israel, and that's obviously 
> not us?  :)

What Jesus said to a crowd in Israel is a different context from the eight 
letters (one to Asia in general and seven to individual groups in Asia) that 
*explicitly* specify and address their intented *and limited* audiences.  It 
is one thing to say "hey, folks, anybody who's listening", and another to 
say "Hey, Laodiceans! Listen up! This is for you!".

>  - The letters to the seven churches (from which that verse was taken) 
> dealt with a particular problem in each church.  Rather than only being 
> good for that geographical region, I rather believe that they are good 
> principles for dealing with the problems they were meant to address.

Well, that's kinda my point.  How do *you* know that *my* failings are those 
addressed by this passage to the Laodiceans?   I am fully capable of beating 
myself all the way to suicide if I spend all my time concentrating on how 
every single misstep on my part is driving yet another thorn into the head 
of the poor bleeding Jesus, represents yet another hammer-blow on the nail 
piercing His wrist, or is analogous to yet another taunt from the onlookers 
at the crucifixion, as I was brought up to believe.  I regard that tendency 
now as a *failing* on my part, however well-meaning the folks were who 
taught me to do that, and I just don't go there anymore.

> Also, the "world at large" is the target audience of the Bible.  Some 
> things in it are recorded simply to pass along history - I don't think God 
> needs any of us to stand between two pillars and push the way Samson did. 
> Others give us general precepts for living - I'm thinking here about 
> Proverbs (among other passages).

Well, you and I disagree about the monolithic nature of the Bible.

I believe the New Testament is a collection of books assembled over a period 
of four or five centuries.  Some of these books were included because 
Everybody Knew they were of apostolic authorship -- which underlying 
principle was the primary reason they were included.  Even Martin Luther 
regarded some of them as spurious.  Eusebius' History discusses the 
development of the New Testament Canon at some length.

More obviously controversial today is the Old Testament, in which the 
Orthodox and Catholic canon has more books (evolving from the Septuagint in 
the Alexandrian tradition) than the Protestant (evolving after Luther's 
rejection of the Apocrypha from the Jewish canon of the Masoretes out of the 
Palestinian tradition).

The premise that the Apostles decided the contents of the Bible, Old and New 
Testament, as we now have it, is quite simply not supported by the 
historical record.

> As far as a Scriptural basis, as Paul wrote to Timothy,

... direct authorship is in significant scholarly dispute ...

>  "All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for 
> doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in 
> righteousness..."  (2 Timothy 3:16)  Why would God have preserved these 
> instructions if they were only directed at a few thousand people?  :)

Presuming Paul wrote this, what does "pasa graphe theopneustos" actually 
*mean*?  Is he referring to "all inspired-of-God scripture is ... " or "all 
scripture is inspired of God, is ... "?  There's no explicit verb in the 
sentence and there are two equally-reasonable places to presume an elided 
"estin".

And did Paul really mean "God-breathed", that every single letter of every 
single word of the passages being referred to was dictated directly by God? 
He seems to have invented the word "theopneustos", so it's tough to figure 
out exactly what he meant by it (particularly since he wasn't exactly fluent 
in Greek, based on evidence in the indisputably-Pauline epistles).

And as to "graphe"  (="writing"), I think the big question is what's he 
referring to?   If Paul himself wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been written 
before his execution under Nero, whose reign ended in 67AD.  From what I 
read,  the earliest plausible dates for the Gospels are:  Matthew, after 
70AD and probably about 85AD; Mark, around 70AD; Luke/Acts, about 75AD, and 
John, 90-100AD.   If Paul wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been before his 
martyrdom.  He thus would have had to foresee the acceptance of four, only 
four, and exactly four Gospels as deserving of being considered "scripture" 
*that had not yet even been written*!  He would also be placing himself in 
the position of saying "Hey!  Everything *I've* written deserves to be 
treated as DIVINELY-INSPIRED SCRIPTURE!!!! FOREVER!!!" were his intent to 
include what we now know as the New Testament as part of this reference.

I don't think that these are supportable suppositions.

More likely, since he was someone with a strong Jewish background, I believe 
that to which he was referring was that which was accepted as "scripture" 
*at the time*.  The distribution and collection of Paul's letters occurred 
in the century after his death, and the solidification of the New Testament 
canon occurred three centuries and more after that.  That to which "pasa 
graphe" refers thus has to be, taken in its broadest possible 
interpretation, the Old Testament.   Not the New.  It didn't exist as a 
unit, and much of it didn't exist at all, when this part of 2 Timothy was 
written, if indeed Paul wrote it.   And Jesus' own words support this in 
Luke 24:44-45:  "the law of Moses, and in the prophets, and in the psalms 
... the scriptures ..." (the last referring to the first three).

That I find a passage in the New Testament useful does not have anything to 
do with whether I think "pasa graphe theopneustos" in 2 Timothy refers to 
anything written after, say, 49AD.  I don't, and that includes the letters 
to the Churches in Asia in Revelation.

By the way, I also believe that Revelation 22:18-19 can only be reasonably 
taken to refer to the Book of Revelation.  The word used in Greek for "book" 
here is "biblion" -- a small book, a scroll; it's a diminutive of "biblos". 
I know many Christians who believe the verse is an explicit and specific 
reference to everything from Genesis 1:1 through Revelation 22:21, but I 
think both the historical record and the direct meaning of the Greek text 
(KJV "and if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this 
prophecy ...") argues strongly against the plausibility of such a view.

And is Martin Luther's "part ... of the book of life" taken out because he 
didn't much care forHebrews, James, Jude and Revelation?

I will note that 2 Peter 3:15ff elevates the writings of Paul to the level 
of Scripture (="graphas"); the question I have is direct Petrine authorship 
of this epistle, and the consequential dating of this epistle (as well as 
the assembly of Paul's complete collected letters) as having been completed 
before 67AD.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 20)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-04T21:41:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> say "Hey, Laodiceans! Listen up! This is for you!".

Were not most epistles written to one church or person, but distributed 
around to many?  The Laodiceans had a specific problem which was given a 
specific solution.  It would be rather presumptuous of us to assume that 
if we encountered a similar problem, the solution would be different.  :)

>>  - The letters to the seven churches (from which that verse was taken) 
>> dealt with a particular problem in each church.  Rather than only being 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> addressed by this passage to the Laodiceans?

I don't, and I don't believe I've ever insinuated that you had failings 
at all.  None of this discussion has, at least on my part, been intended 
to identify failings.  This particular part of the discussion originated 
when Howard said it was mean-spirited of God to allow anyone to die if 
He doesn't wish them to.

> I am fully capable of beating 
> myself all the way to suicide if I spend all my time concentrating on how 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> taught me to do that, and I just don't go there anymore.

Well, the folks who taught you to do that missed it a bit, too.  Jesus 
would have gone through everything He did even if only one of us needed 
it.  This is the "freedom" that Paul (yes, I've read further) was 
talking about "in Christ".  What's been done to Jesus is done - no 
sinning or lack of sinning on anyone's part will change the severity of 
what has already taken place.

>> Also, the "world at large" is the target audience of the Bible.  Some 
>> things in it are recorded simply to pass along history - I don't think God 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> historical record.

I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible, and have obviously 
done less studying on it than you have.  But, if you successfully pick 
away at one piece of it, its claim of infallibility then is gone.  Once 
that's gone, on what do you base your belief?

I have yet to have someone show me a discrepancy, where the Bible 
contradicts itself, that wasn't manufactured and taken out of context. 
Scientists have even found a missing day - coincidentally, around the 
time the "sun stood still" during an Old Testament battle.  Research on 
Mount Ararat, where remains of Noah's Ark have been found, has done 
nothing but prove the Biblical record.

Obviously, the Bible was written by men, and compiled by men.  Believing 
that these men allowed God to inspire them and guide their choices 
doesn't mean that it's all the work of man, and full of flaws.  God has 
preserved what He wants preserved, and I truly believe that stuff like 
"The Da Vinci Code" and "Misquoting Jesus" are serving to distract a 
large number of people, and make them question not only the pieces they 
challenge, but the Scriptures as a whole.

(Full disclosure - I have read neither book mentioned above in their 
entirety.  I have read more than one review of both books.)

>> As far as a Scriptural basis, as Paul wrote to Timothy,
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> "estin".

Either way, the effect is the same.  If it's Scripture, it's inspired of 
God.  If you have something claiming to be Scripture but not inspired of 
God, it's false.  In both cases, the profitability only applies to 
inspired Scripture.

> And did Paul really mean "God-breathed", that every single letter of every 
> single word of the passages being referred to was dictated directly by God? 
> He seems to have invented the word "theopneustos", so it's tough to figure 
> out exactly what he meant by it (particularly since he wasn't exactly fluent 
> in Greek, based on evidence in the indisputably-Pauline epistles).

Creating a word - heh!  :)  I guess that's been going on for millenia...

> And as to "graphe"  (="writing"), I think the big question is what's he 
> referring to?   If Paul himself wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been written 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't think that these are supportable suppositions.

How about the supposition that 2 Timothy 3:16 is God-breathed Scripture? 
  God said to write it, and "Paul" wrote it.  It didn't matter what was 
accepted into the canon at the time.  (In fact, "at the time", I imagine 
the church was a bit less organized than it was a few centuries later.) 
  God knew how it would be put together, and that's what He wanted written.

Is that supportable?

[snip]
> By the way, I also believe that Revelation 22:18-19 can only be reasonably 
> taken to refer to the Book of Revelation.  The word used in Greek for "book" 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> before 67AD.

With all these questions, what do you believe?  Are these deep-rooted 
questions, or are they "hey, I'm kinda curious about this - doesn't 
change my view of it, I just wonder" type of questions?

I'm just trying to figure out how one could believe the Bible without 
believing it to be the inspired, inerrant, infallible Word of God. 
Without that solid foundation, how can anything else be built?

(These questions aren't meant to imply that you don't - I'm just having 
trouble understanding...)
```

```
> I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible,

Well, you should find out.  Most of it was argued over at Nicea in 325
when most groups agreed to disagree about what it should be (except the
jews who weren't invited).

> and have obviously
> done less studying on it than you have.  But, if you successfully pick
> away at one piece of it, its claim of infallibility then is gone.
> Once that's gone, on what do you base your belief?

Hopefully you learn about the real world instead of the myths and
legends of the middle east.

If I was to claim that thunder and lightning was made by Thor hitting
his anvil with his hammer then 2 or 3 thousand years ago that might
have seemed a reasonable belief. They didn't know any better and could
relate to what happened in the blacksmith's shop. If anyone said it
today they would be dismissed even if they claimed that meteorology was
'just a theory' and 'weathermen always got it wrong' so they should
teach 'Intelligent Weather' in meteorology classes.
```

- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> > I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible,
>
> Well, you should find out.  Most of it was argued over at Nicea in 325
> when most groups agreed to disagree about what it should be (except the
> jews who weren't invited).

I do agree that finding out the facts before speaking is a good idea.
The story that the canon was decided at Nicaea is a myth, however.  I
researched it a while back and put or linked to all the material I
could find in the ancient sources online:

http://www.tertullian.org/rpearse/nicaea.html

The canon of the NT was more or less closed by general agreement during
the 4th century, and everything with any real claim to apostolic origin
was included.  But there is no one point at which this happened.

> > and have obviously
> > done less studying on it than you have.  But, if you successfully pick
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> legends of the middle east.

Do the irrational period values of one tiny society in one period of
history deserve such uncritical praise, or the religion of 2000 years
of humanity such stale dismissal?

In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
they happen to live?  Can we even state what we believe in terms that
make sense, if we are not Christians (or some other
ideologically-defined group)?  In my experience, we cannot.  We do not
ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.

> If I was to claim that thunder and lightning was made by Thor hitting
> his anvil with his hammer then 2 or 3 thousand years ago that might
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> teach 'Intelligent Weather' in meteorology classes.

Are there not several strawmen in this argument?  Believing that the
theory of evolution explains how life got here is good if it is based
on rational examination.  It is bad when it is advanced merely because
those who set the media agenda of our day tell us that anything else is
like Thor and his hammer so we must believe it.  It's even worse if we
find ourselves repeating such, surely?

All the best,
```

```
<roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...
> Richard wrote:
>> > I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I found that interesting. Thanks.

> The canon of the NT was more or less closed by general agreement during
> the 4th century, and everything with any real claim to apostolic origin
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Things don't always get what they deserve.

> In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
> conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
> they happen to live?

I can't speak for others, but my world is far from unexamined.  I conform to 
the values I do because they make sense to me. (After considerable gathering 
of data and evaluation). I don't expect anyone else to do the same (although 
I'd sure be glad if they did :-)). For the most part, I'm happy if people 
grant me the same courtesy I grant them. Live and let live, I reckon...


>  Can we even state what we believe in terms that
> make sense, if we are not Christians (or some other
> ideologically-defined group)?

I certainly believe I can.

> In my experience, we cannot.  We do not
> ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.
>

I don't ask fish about water for a number of reasons. None of them is 
because fish can't see it. Besides, short of becoming a fish, how can one 
know what they see?

>> If I was to claim that thunder and lightning was made by Thor hitting
>> his anvil with his hammer then 2 or 3 thousand years ago that might
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> on rational examination.

All Science SHOULD be based on such a premise.


>It is bad when it is advanced merely because
> those who set the media agenda of our day tell us that anything else is
> like Thor and his hammer so we must believe it.  It's even worse if we
> find ourselves repeating such, surely?
>

I don't think Evolution is advanced as a theory because the alternatives are 
just too fanciful. It may be mischievous to repeat something you know is 
spurious, unless you disclaim agreement with it and offer it purely for 
examination and debate. Putting something forward that you are not sure 
about or haven't made your mind up about, seems perfectly legitimate to me.

> All the best,
>
Likewise. Interesting points. Thanks.

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> I found that interesting. Thanks.

You're welcome.  There's too much assertion going around on these
things (often in good faith, of course, but not right).

> >> > and have obviously
> >> > done less studying on it than you have.  But, if you successfully pick
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Things don't always get what they deserve.

After which it seems almost a moral duty to swindle someone.

> > In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
> > conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
> > they happen to live?
>
> I can't speak for others, but my world is far from unexamined.  ...

I'm sure that we all think this, and it isn't my intention to pillory
individuals.  If you had asked any collection of Victorians, for
instance, they would have claimed that their views were the 'assured
product of scientific investigation' rather than merely a reflection of
the period in which they happened to live.

But would we now agree?  Is it not the case that everyone pretty much
lives by some subset of societal values?  I rather doubt that anyone
reading this is living by (e.g.) Victorian values, for instance.  It is
the menu of ideas that is characteristic of a period; and it changes.
That being the case, our beliefs, if derived from this purely temporary
set, must be mistaken.

> > In my experience, we cannot.  We do not
> > ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> know what they see?

I'm not sure that we're communicating here, you know.  It isn't about
fish... <grin>

> >> If I was to claim that thunder and lightning was made by Thor hitting
> >> his anvil with his hammer then 2 or 3 thousand years ago that might
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> All Science SHOULD be based on such a premise.

Indeed.  Believing that something is objective even more so.

> >It is bad when it is advanced merely because
> > those who set the media agenda of our day tell us that anything else is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> just too fanciful.

I agree.  But I'm not sure that everyone who advocates it does.  After
all, when you strip it right down, there are only 3 explanations of why
the world exists; (1) that someone made it, (2) that it made itself (3)
that it always existed.  These views are ancient, whatever new terms
may be invented for them, and are really philosophical rather than
scientific, and I can't help feeling that they will remain so until our
science has reached a final state of knowing everything about
cosmology.

> It may be mischievous to repeat something you know is
> spurious, unless you disclaim agreement with it and offer it purely for
> examination and debate. Putting something forward that you are not sure
> about or haven't made your mind up about, seems perfectly legitimate to me.

I agree entirely with this.

> Likewise. Interesting points. Thanks.

And yours.  Thank you.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

- **References:** `<636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <49i2loFnupfrU1@individual.net> <1144253470.127631.172560@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <yaSYf.27985$Ph4.6820@edtnps90> <i8s7329mmbo0gkbt407omu8u2t86indvi8@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:i8s7329mmbo0gkbt407omu8u2t86indvi8@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 05 Apr 2006 16:23:58 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3. It always existed?

    Probably (1), another bird (or pair of birds) made your bird.

    How about a star? They're formed when a group of particles happen to 
gather together in one spot in space, creating a gravitational field that 
attracts other particles, creating a stronger gravitational field, and so on 
in a snowball effect. I think that's an example of (4), a bunch of events 
occuring to cause the creation of the star with no one particular 
instigator.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T14:08:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uh9Zf.29238$Ph4.1333@edtnps90>`
- **References:** `<dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <49i2loFnupfrU1@individual.net> <1144253470.127631.172560@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <yaSYf.27985$Ph4.6820@edtnps90> <1144278416.902218.93150@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144314765.907343.250150@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com>`

```

<roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144314765.907343.250150@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com...
> Alistair wrote:
>> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Same thing, at this level.

    Arguable, but I'll let it go for now.

>
>> > and (4) the events were such that it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Same as #1.

    This I disagree with. I'll actually split (4) into two like I did with 
(1). (4a) is "Many things contributed in the creation, and there is no clear 
'primary creator'" and (4b) "It is not clear that any outside force created 
it".

    If I answer (1) or (1b), you could say "Well, whatever that someone, or 
something is, let's call it God." But if I answer (4a), then you'd have to 
say "each person or thing that contribute is one particular God". There are 
some religions like this (I believe some Native American religions believed 
that there was a holy spirit to be respected in every flower, rock, river, 
tree, animal, etc.) but I don't think this belief is compatible with 
Christianity.

    If I answer (4b), then there there isn't anything you can point to and 
say "Let's call that thing God". (4b) might occur in the case of a particle 
and its anti-particle pair coming into existence out of a vacuum. It's not 
clear that anything created the particle. It's not clear that anything 
created the anti-particle. It's not like the particle made itself, because 
if they could make themselves, you'd probably see an abundance of particles 
being created spontaneously all the time without its anti-particle pair. But 
in reality, when a particle is created, it's always with its anti-particle 
pair, so clearly the particle can't just create itself. By a symmetric 
argument, the anti-particle can't just create itself either. It just sort of 
happens.

>
>> >     (Also, by "world", do you mean planet Earth, or the known universe, 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I imagine in ancient times all these terms are more or less equivalent.

    Yes, but I'm asking what do YOU meant when you wrote it. I'm guessing 
you mean known universe.

>
>> And what about my favourite: I'm not sure that it exists?
>
> Sincerity is a pre-requisite for intelligent discussion.

    I'm not sure that Alistair's responce can be considered unreasonable (or 
"unintelligent"): At any one point in time, you cannot know for sure if the 
world you're experiencing around you is real. Whatever you're experiencing 
right now might be a hallucination, and your memories of the past which seem 
to lead to your current experiences of the present might be false memories 
created by your brain to support your belief in your current reality.

    E.g. Notice how often (but not always), when you're dreaming, you don't 
realize that you're dreaming? It's only *afterwards*, when you wake up, do 
you realize how ridiculous the "reality" of your dream was, and you can't 
understand how you could have possibly believed in it. But you did.

    So how can you know that's not what's happening right now? Hence 
Alistair's argument, how can you be sure the world around you right now 
really exists?

    - Oliver
```

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1144268026.903416.198230@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>> Besides, short of becoming a fish, how can one
>> know what they see?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't think so, Richard.

The argument was: "We do not  ask a fish about water, because it can't see 
it."

It is a valid refutation of this argument to respond that nobody knows what 
a fish can see. If it were true, for example that a fish CAN see water then 
the argument is lost. (Or won, if you look at it from my point of view :-))

As the fact is that there is no definitive knowledge of exactly what a fish 
DOES see, this leaves the argument, at the very least, in doubt.

As it happens, I don't talk to fish, NOT because they can't see water. I 
wouldn't talk to them if they COULD see water :-)

I don't talk to fish because they can't understand  or respond to what I'm 
saying. That is the real point.

Pete.
```

```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> >  Can we even state what we believe in terms that
> > make sense, if we are not Christians (or some other
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Perhaps we should ask a marine biologist to answer that one. Wait a
minute, I have a degree in marine biology!

To understand what a fish may see it is first necessary to understand
the structure and composition of the fish eye. Then you have to
understand the nature of the medium. Finally, you must consider the
structure and function of the fish brain. When all is said and done,
most fish can see as clearly as we can (some species have superior
visual accuity) in transparent media and can detect colours. However,
seeing is not the same as comprehension. I keep fish which seem quite
happy to swim within range of a catfish which could easily eat them;
they can see it but do not comprehend the danger it poses.

Ask a silly question, get a silly answer.    :-)
```
