```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144278260.782423.309640@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Here in Tauranga the local PolyTech runs one of the best Marine Biology 
courses in NZ. (We have easy access to the Pacific Ocean in both exposed and 
sheltered states.) The emphasis on this course has always been on the 
practical side, with most of the course taking place on the ocean or beaches 
and much Scuba diving involved. (One of my friends is a lecturer on this 
course and I have met a number of the students at social functiuons. They 
unanimously extol the course.) Now the PolyTech is seeking to upgrade to 
University status and so next year the syllabus will change to conform to 
the University of NZ (as practised specifically in Auckland) syllabus. This 
is much more theoretical than practical and I think it is a pity.

Where a Tauranga student would go and test fish with various colour 
detection and visual acuity practical tests, an Auckland student would 
simply dissect a fish eye and attempt to deduce the function from the form. 
:-)

Obviously, both approaches have their merits and as they are not mutually 
exclusive, you would hope that both would be applied. But the emphasis would 
depend on where you were studying...

I don't think your answer is silly, Alastair.

I think the whole analogy was off the beam (or should that be 'bream'?:-)) 
to start with...:-)

Pete.
```

```
On 5 Apr 2006 03:02:04 -0700, roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk wrote:

>In our societies do not most people base their beliefs on an unexamined
>conformity to some subset of the societal values of the period in which
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.

People accept those beliefs from the society they were born in.   If
they lucky enough to be born in the right society, the vast majority
who accept those beliefs will be rewarded with eternal bliss.    If
they were unlucky enough to be born in any of the other societies, the
vast majority who accept those beliefs will be punished with eternal
torture.

It's only fair and just (according to those who plan on looking smugly
down upon those being tortured).
```

```
> <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Two things:

1.  I have to tell people.
2.  When I do, a lot of them deny it.

But actually I think it's obvious that this is the source of our
beliefs, as soon as you think about it.  But a lot of people really do
not get that far, because it isn't explicily stated every night on TV.

>     (2) Depending on what those beliefs are, why should we do something
> about it?

Wouldn't it better to have beliefs that have some rational basis,
rather than whatever the Great and Good want us to believe?

> E.g. if the beliefs of one particular society can be summarized as
> "Everyone should be free to believe in whatever they want", should we really
> "do something" to change that?

This comment involves various beliefs on your part, which you presume
that I share.  From where do these beliefs of yours come <grin>?  You
see?

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T11:40:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e12ums$41b$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1144253633.762187.148740@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <XcSYf.27986$Ph4.10749@edtnps90> <1144315056.474677.198120@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1144315056.474677.198120@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
 <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote:
>Oliver Wong wrote:
>> <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>1.  I have to tell people.

Gah... with all due respect, Mr Pearse, note the use of '*we*' (emphasis 
original) above... one of the reasons why I, personally, try to avoid use 
of a first person plurals.  That you have to tell 'people' does not 
require a conclusion that you have to tell 'a certain subset of people' 
(the first-person plural group to which Mr Wong referred).

(the matter of necessity - 'I have to' - may be addressed at another time)

>2.  When I do, a lot of them deny it.

Gah again... this reminds me of an exchange I heard between two members of 
a (substance) Anonymous group regarding what is sometimes called 'casual 
use':

Person A: 'No, that's not how it is at all... you're just in denial, man.'

Person B: 'Ummmmmm... is there *any* way that I can disagree with you 
about this and *not* be 'in denial'?'

Person A: (no pause) 'No, no way, anything you say against it, it's 
denial.'

[snip]

>>     (2) Depending on what those beliefs are, why should we do something
>> about it?
>
>Wouldn't it better to have beliefs that have some rational basis,
>rather than whatever the Great and Good want us to believe?

Answering a question with a question, Mr Pearse, is no answer at all... 
but leaving that aside for a moment 'credo' is, by definition, not 
'ratio'; it may not spring from the same source and should not be subject 
to the same criteria of examination.

(hmmmmm... to take it a step further, at least according to the rules of 
English as She is Spoke... a permissible prefix for 'not' is 'ir-'; 
allowing for that substitution my assertion might be mangled into ''credo' 
is, by definition, 'irrational'')

DD
```

```
the accuracy of your perceptions are you asking that 'games like this' not 
be played with words or that 'words like this' (whatever 'this' refers to) 
not be the subject of game-playing?

(along the lines of 'I dropped the glass on the table and it broke'... it 
must have been a rather fragile table if such could break it)

>
>> >2.  When I do, a lot of them deny it.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Well said.

Pfoo... you'se jes' easily impressed.

>But I was not engaged in such games; I was merely
>responding to your point that (a) people get their views from society
>and (b) don't need telling that this is so.  They do!

This was not the point I intended... interesting that you see it as such, 
though.

>
>I have snipped the remainder as, again, you're not engaging with my
>comments and instead playing games with words.

I am attempting to apply a bit of logic, that's all... but logic, of 
course, is a game played by a particular set of rules, or so Wittgenstein 
tells it.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T08:32:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e13ca6$1l5s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1144253633.762187.148740@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <XcSYf.27986$Ph4.10749@edtnps90> <1144315056.474677.198120@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <e12ums$41b$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e12ums$41b$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <1144315056.474677.198120@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,

> Gah again... this reminds me of an exchange I heard between two members of
> a (substance) Anonymous group regarding what is sometimes called 'casual
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> denial.'

Ummmmm .... when was the last time person A talked to his sponsor?  ;-)

And are you sure this is a *substance* abuse Anonymous group?  This sounds a 
lot like something I might expect to hear among relative newcomers to the 
likes of Adult Children of Alcoholics or even Al-Anon, no insult to those 
groups intended!

To be fair, I know some members of a (liquid substance) Anonymous group who 
continue to engage in "casual use" of a certain leafy substance whose use is 
decidedly frowned upon by the Federal Government, but I would have to say 
most of the people with significant time in that particular program look 
askance at the "casual" use of *any* mind-altering substances under *any* 
circumstance.

And while I know some members of a certain (decidedly illegal substance) 
Anonymous group who think they don't have a problem with liquid substances, 
the basic text (or is it Basic Text?) of the group clearly states that 
consumption of alcoholic beverages is in the same category as slamming their 
Drug of Choice.   It's my observation that those who choose to ignore that 
particular passage don't seem to be all that successful at avoiding the 
behavior they came into the program to overcome.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
> Howard Brazee wrote:

    How do you know they don't encourage you to question their assertions? 
Do you know which "Great and Good teachers" Howard is referring to 
specifically? This ties in to my concern with your other post "We have to 
change the beliefs that society is encouraging into ones that have a 
rational basis". How do you know that the specified society isn't already 
promoting beliefs that have a rational basis?

    Maybe you mean you have you change the specific society *YOU* live in; 
in which case, if you expect our help, you should at least specify which 
society that is. The societies I happen to interact with tend to promote 
rational beliefs already. The teachers I've learned from, those that I would 
consider "Great and Good" anyway, encouraged me to question their 
assertions.

    - Oliver
```

```
society who indoctrinates into their members the belief that "beliefs that 
have some rational basis are good". Would you "do something" about this, to 
force the society to change what belief they are promoting? Do you think 
*anyone* should do something to force the society to change? What beliefs 
should they promote instead?
```

```
Mr Pearse, I cannot speak for Mr Wong... but I barely know why *I* think 
something is so, let alone anyone else.  You might have Very Good 
Arguments for think something is or is not so... or you may think it 
because your parents were blood-relative or because Space Aliens implanted 
brainwaves into your cranium.

might be best to start simply, with Definitions, Common Notions and 
Postulates before moving on to proofs... but not everyone's capable of 
that, granted.

DD
```

```
roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk wrote:
> Richard wrote:

> > Well, you should find out.  Most of it was argued over at Nicea in 325
> > when most groups agreed to disagree about what it should be (except the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The story that the canon was decided at Nicaea is a myth,

That is not what I stated.  I said it was argued about and disagreed.
In fact they also disagreed about dogma, what is the nature of Jesus:
was he a god in his own right, or a demigod like Hercules, or part of
the god of the jews, or just a man.

> > Hopefully you learn about the real world instead of the myths and
> > legends of the middle east.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of humanity such stale dismissal?

Actually I find that there are thousands of religions which have
different histories, different gods, different values mainly because
they originated in different areas. Even within the major grouping of
some of the more popular of these there are vast differences.

The reason that christianity spread in europeans was that jews, then
and now, are semi-nomadic and carried it to europe where it was imposed
by the remnants of the Roman Empire as a buffer against the pagan
and/or heathen barbarians.

I am sure that most christians dismiss the religions of the norse, the
classical greeks, and the romans. They also may dismiss the
Rastafarians (did I mention ...), the scientologists and many others.
In fact studying how and why recent religions have been formed shows
the process of creating religions around charismatics for the purpose
of social control by providing the promise of something better.

I just don't see any particular difference in any of them that would
make one 'right' and the others 'wrong'.  Rastafarians are just as
convinced and sincere in their beliefs as the most rampant
fundementalist.

>  We do not ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.

I don't ask fish about anything, nor chicken entrails, though I am sure
that some may do so, and use the advice given, or pass it on to their
followers.

However, as on goldfish said to another: "If there is no god then who
changes the water ?".

> Are there not several strawmen in this argument?

Is Thor a strawman or an analogy ?

> Believing that the
> theory of evolution explains how life got here

Actually Darwin explicity stated that he was not explaining 'how life
got here' but his theory was not 'evolution' but was 'natural
selection' and 'common decent' which are explanations for the
observable occurance of evolution.  His is one of several theories
explaining various and different aspects of how evolution works.

You should note that in spite of the FUD produced by religionists
evolution is not a theory, it is natural selection, sexual selection,
and common decent that are the explanations of how it works.

> is good if it is based
> on rational examination.  It is bad when it is advanced merely because
> those who set the media agenda of our day tell us that anything else is
> like Thor and his hammer so we must believe it.  It's even worse if we
> find ourselves repeating such, surely?

The 'anything else' consists of hundreds of diverse and incompatible
creation myths many of which you would most likely label as 'like Thor
and his hammer'.

The point is that man trys to create explanations for the evidence they
see. On mountains one sees the fossils of sea creatures and sea shells,
one also finds remains of unknown animals. One hypotheis for this is
that the sea was once so high that sea creatures lived here and in
doing so drowned other creatures.  Hence the story of a flood as an
explanation for what is observed.

Today we understand (or at least those who are educated do) about
tectonic plates and moutian forming so, for example, the southern alps
here (NZ) are the scapings from the sea bed piled up as the pacific
plate collides with the Autralasian (this is measurable). So there is
no need for the old mythologised explanations of why sea shells are up
mountains.
```

```
Richard wrote:
> roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk wrote:

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> That is not what I stated.  I said it was argued about and disagreed.

Indeed you did.  But in fact the subject was not discussed. Hey, the
whole idea's a myth!

> In fact they also disagreed about dogma, what is the nature of Jesus:
> was he a god in his own right, or a demigod like Hercules, or part of
> the god of the jews, or just a man.

This also is mainly fiction, I am afraid, and again just stuff that is
repeated around the web.  The council was concerned with whether the
second person of the Trinity was of the same substance (homoousios) as
the first person, or of like substance.

But don't take my word for it.  Why don't you take half an hour and
look over all the ancient sources which mention the council, and see
what they say?  You won't find this stuff in them: it's all a modern
invention.  I linked to or uploaded them all at:

http://www.tertullian.org/rpearse/nicaea.html

(I know some brain-dead people then suggest that I am quoting my own
website as an authority.  But of course such people can do the legwork
for themselves, then -- I merely created the shortcuts as a help to
everyone).

> > > Hopefully you learn about the real world instead of the myths and
> > > legends of the middle east.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Actually I find that there are thousands of religions ... (snip)

I'm sorry, but these comments don't deal with my point, do they?  And
the game of calling Christianity a religion, then saying there are
loads of different religions therefore all of them (and especially
Christianity) must be untrue is a fraud.  Those who practise it always
have a set of beliefs and values that they propose to exclude from this
process, because they would be equally debunked by it.

> The reason that christianity spread in europeans was that jews, then
> and now, are semi-nomadic and carried it to europe where it was imposed
> by the remnants of the Roman Empire as a buffer against the pagan
> and/or heathen barbarians.

This is not a good representation of the history of Western Europe from
30AD- 410: sorry.  Where did you find this one?

> >  We do not ask a fish about water, because it can't see it.

Well, I'd stop listening to donkeys if I were you... (Gotcha...!)

All the best,
```

- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144208066.683858.289540@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1144231324.339601.92070@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <1144267591.491087.261230@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <1144430839.566147.92310@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com>`

```

<roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144430839.566147.92310@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com...
>
> the game of calling Christianity a religion, then saying there are
> loads of different religions therefore all of them (and especially
> Christianity) must be untrue is a fraud.

    In my experience, what actually happens is an advocate for a given 
religion might say "All other religions are wrong, but mine is right." 
Someone might respond "What evidence do you have to support this claim?" to 
which the advocate responds "How dare you question my religion! You cannot 
prove the inexistance of my God! your logic has failed you! My religion 
wins!".

    Note that this hypothetical advocate has jumped to conclusions. The 
person did not actually claim that the advocate's religion was untrue; 
rather, he was just asking for evidence of its truthfulness. There's a big 
difference. It's not uncommon to ask for evidence for a claim which one 
suspects is true. E.g. "I believe it should be possible to open files in 
COBOL, but I just haven't been able to figure out a way to do so. Can 
someone demonstrate a program which does this?" In this example, I'm asking 
for evidence that COBOL can open files. I'm not claiming that COBOL cannot 
open files.

    So the "game" usually isn't saying that (especially) Christianity is 
untrue. Rather, it's asking "where's the evidence?", or more specifically, 
"That other advocate from another religion over there says HIS religion is 
right and yours is wrong. Who should I believe, and why?"

    - Oliver
```

```
That one has always intriqued me... :-)

If I'm dead and a spirit, why would a burning lake of sulphur or anything 
else that's hot, for that matter, bother me? How do the Laws of 
Thermodynamics apply to disembodied entities? If I'm just a psychic energy 
pattern, why would I be concerned about heat? How do you 'burn' the 
unburnable...?

As for some psychotic believing God told him I need to believe that his 
religion is the true one, tell him that God has since appeared to me and 
confirmed that there was a misunderstanding. I am not welcome or eligible 
and should not bother the Prophets or waste their time... "For all have 
sinned and come short of the Glory..." except that, in my case, it is SO far 
short I must be declared 'Stony Ground' and anyone seeking to persuade me 
further is guilty of the sin of timewasting on God's ministry... :-)

Pete.
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T12:10:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e114mt$b0j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144243113.134237.323110@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144243113.134237.323110@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...

> Perhaps it was authored by Paul but subsequently edited by some other
> divinely-inspired member of the church?

Authorship meant something different in the first century than it does 
today, and that's part of the issue with New Testament authorship (books 
have been written on the subject).  In summary, someone being considered the 
"author" of something included the premise that the basic teachings they 
espoused were the *authority* behind the text.

Note that if 2 *Peter* was actually *written* by *Paul*, it would go counter 
to that premise!

Both Peter and Paul died before about 67AD, and the historical record seems 
to indicate that the copying, collection and distribution of Paul's letters 
didn't occur for a few decades after that.   The tradition reflected in this 
passage that Paul's letters *had been* collected and deserved to be treated 
with significant respect *cannot* have originated during Peter's lifetime.

And there's *nothing* in the passage that indicates *which* of Paul's 
authentic letters were to be included, to say nothing of which letters once 
attributed to Paul but whose authorship (in either sense) is now unknown, 
like the Epistle to the Hebrews, and whose direct Pauline authorship (like 
the Pastorals) is in significant question on linguistic grounds.   Even if I 
accepted the passage as having applicability to certain letters indisputably 
written by Paul, for me to accept it as having applicability as well to 
those that Paul *didn't* actually write is a bit of a stretch.

And if I followed Luther's advice, or I were of the Syriac Orthodox 
persuasion, I'd be dismissing the passage (along with the one in Revelation) 
as non-Scripture, so what body of literature the passage should be 
interpreted as referring to would be a meaningless question to start with!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
But I don't think death is bad.   It is being tortured beyond all
understanding forever and ever without hope of parole.

Allowing death is allowing release.   Allowing the other is pure and
simple evil.
```

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET...

> Were not most epistles written to one church or person, but distributed 
> around to many?  The Laodiceans had a specific problem which was given a 
> specific solution.  It would be rather presumptuous of us to assume that 
> if we encountered a similar problem, the solution would be different.  :)

It would be equally presumptuous of us to assume or declare that somebody 
else had the same problem, and/or that it was our responsibility to point it 
out to them and to describe to them exactly what the solution to the problem 
we perceived in them ought to be.   For me to apply a solution like this to 
myself for a problem that I perceived myself as having -- or even that 
reading the passage led me to believe that I was having -- is one thing.  To 
apply either the diagnosis or the solution to anybody else is quite another.

Reminds me of a group of Christians who accosted a group of gay men as they 
were leaving a meeting of a twelve-step program that dealt with sexual 
issues, telling them what sinners they were and how they needed to change 
their ways by becoming heterosexual or celibate, right now, and generally 
haranguing them with the Usual Scriptural Citations.  Well, for some of 
these guys, finding out their sexual partners' first name before having sex 
with them was a goal that *they* thought was achievable and worthwhile 
pursuing.     Is it appropriate for a Christian to declare publically that 
that is a goal such folks should *not* pursue?

>> I am fully capable of beating myself all the way to suicide if I spend 
>> all my time concentrating on how every single misstep on my part is 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well, the folks who taught you to do that missed it a bit, too.

No kidding.

> Jesus would have gone through everything He did even if only one of us 
> needed it.  This is the "freedom" that Paul (yes, I've read further) was 
> talking about "in Christ".  What's been done to Jesus is done - no sinning 
> or lack of sinning on anyone's part will change the severity of what has 
> already taken place.

According to you.  According to them, the torture continues.  I *don't 
agree*, but I can't declare them *wrong*.

> I don't know who decided the contents of the Bible, and have obviously 
> done less studying on it than you have.

Well, I can't say that, but I can say that I've done enough research to 
write a paper on the subject, which paper has been accepted as appropriate 
for the education of the ministry of our church.   So I do know a fair 
amount about it.

> But, if you successfully pick away at one piece of it, its claim of 
> infallibility then is gone.

"*Its* claim of infallibility?"  Part of my issue is that I *do not believe* 
that such a claim is made *for the Bible as you or I know it*, in so many 
words, *in the Bible*.

The author of 2 Peter makes the claim for the collected letters of Paul, *as 
that author understood the composition of that collection to be at the 
time*.  Revelation makes dire threats about altering *Revelation*.  But the 
Syriac Orthodox seem to reject *both* Revelation *and* 2 Peter as Scripture 
(see below) (although some 6th-7th century translations from Greek into 
Aramaic are included in modern copies of the Peshitta).

> Once that's gone, on what do you base your belief?

I accept the premise that God *guided* the authors of various books, and 
that by and large the books that have been incorporated into what we know as 
the Bible today were written under such guidance.  I do not believe, given 
the way the Bible was *assembled* over a period of centuries, that it is 
perfect and complete in any of the forms in which we have it today.  In 
fact, I would say for me to assume that this book is Divinely Perfect is, 
pure and simple, *idolatry*.   Bottom line, it's a *graven image*.  I use it 
as a tool.  I will *not* worship it.

As to the history itself, a good starting point is the Wikipedia article on 
"Biblical Canon" (a better one is the article in The New Jerome Biblical 
Commentary on Canonicity, if you can get hold of a copy).  The Wikipedia 
article will illustrate several important points:

The Syriac Orthodox Church alluded to be above apparently has historically 
rejected 2 John, 3 John, 2 Peter, Jude and Revelation as scripture, and some 
in that organization hold that the Peshitta New Testament, in Aramaic, is 
the original and the Greek texts are translations.  Few scholars agree with 
that position, but the Lamsa translation of the Peshitta has a fair 
following *outside* the Syriac Orthodox community (and some of the arguments 
for the differences from the traditional Greek are plausible! The one that 
springs to mind is "It is easier to pass a rope through the eye of a needle 
... " based on the fact that the trigrammaton GML is both *rope* and *camel* 
in Aramaic, and written Aramaic doesn't record the vowel differences!).

The Armenian Orthodox include 3 Corinthians as canonic New Testament 
material.   Most, *but not all*, Christian groups accept the familiar 
27-book New Testament canon.

As to the Old Testament, the Greek Orthodox canon includes Psalm 151, 1 
Esdras, 3 and 4 Maccabees, the Psalms of Solomon, the Odes of Solomon, and 
the Letter of Jeremiah alongside the Roman Catholic canon of 46 books (and 
additions in other books), and also considers the Septuagint to be the 
divinely inspired version of the Old Testament.

The Ethiopic Orthodox is somewhat similar in its "narrow" canon; it uses a 
different underlying text for the first three books of the Maccabees; omits 
4 Maccabees, 2 Esdras, Psalms of Solomon, Odes of Solomon, and the Letter of 
Jeremiah from that canon; and adds Enoch (the Ethiopic, not the Syriac 
version) and Jubilees.

And as I indicated before, Martin Luther himself argued for the removal of 
Hebrews, James, Jude and Revelation from the canon.

> I have yet to have someone show me a discrepancy, where the Bible 
> contradicts itself, that wasn't manufactured and taken out of context. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> prove the Biblical record.

I think "found" and "prove" might be a little strong.

> Obviously, the Bible was written by men, and compiled by men.  Believing 
> that these men allowed God to inspire them and guide their choices doesn't 
> mean that it's all the work of man, and full of flaws.  God has preserved 
> what He wants preserved,

Where, in which languages, in which texts or manuscripts, and containing 
which books?  The Syriac Orthodox community has, as far as I can tell, 
*always* rejected several New Testament books, *including* Revelation.

Has the entire Syriac Orthodox community run afoul of God's will on the 
basis of Revelation 22:19 by rejecting Revelation as canonic?

Is the entire Armenian Orthodox community condemned to suffer all the 
plagues described in Revelation for having accepted 3 Corinthians and 
thereby added to what God thinks the Bible ought to be?   Or can we rejoice 
that God's Just Punishment for this abomination has been demonstrated in the 
Armenian Genocide at the hands of the Ottoman Empire about a century ago, 
just like, according to one devout Christian group, the deaths of American 
soldiers in Iraq and Afghanistan are God's just punishment on the United 
States and we should rejoice in their family's pain and suffering?

> "The Da Vinci Code" and "Misquoting Jesus" are serving to distract a large 
> number of people, and make them question not only the pieces they 
> challenge, but the Scriptures as a whole.

Well, I've always thought "The Da Vinci Code" was pretty much a ripoff of 
"Holy Blood, Holy Grail", which I read quite a few years ago, but what do I 
know.  I have not read "Misquoting Jesus".  My challenge has always been 
"But what do the Scriptures *really say*?"

And as to the "Scriptures as a whole", that's my point.  There have *always* 
been differences of opinion as to what is the body of work that constitutes 
"the Scriptures", as well as which underlying text (or translation) 
represents the divinely-inspired (or even, the "God-breathed") version of 
each book.    You may accept one set (e.g, the MT for the Old Testament and 
the TR for the New, as they underlie the KJV tradition), others might argue 
for more modern OT translations that include influence from the DSS and for 
Oldest Text translations based on the latest Nestle-Aland or even Shortest 
Text translations like the Jerusalem Bible, and still others accept the 
Peshitta.

"The Bible", *as you know it*, is not now and has never been as concretely 
and inalterably defined as you seem to believe, and nowhere is that clearer 
than the division as to what constitutes the Old Testament between the Roman 
Catholic tradition and the Protestant tradition.  I don't count myself a 
Roman Catholic today, but as far as the contents of Scripture goes, between 
the Catholics and the Protestants, guess who moved?

And if Luther felt free to reject books of the Old Testament that had been 
accepted as Scripture for a millennium (namely, the Apocrypha, starting with 
2 Maccabees because of its implicit support for expiation for the sins of 
others through intercessory prayers for the dead in 12:42), indeed, what 
value is Scripture and how immutable should we view it?

My opinion, based on my research, is that the contents of the Old Testament 
had pretty well solidified to the *46-book* Roman Catholic canon, and that 
of the New was *generally* regarded as being the 27-book Canon that we know, 
*in the West*, by about the early 5th century.  In the East, the situation 
was far less clear.   The removal of the Apocrypha from Protestant Bibles is 
a *recent* phenomenon (becoming general only in the early 19th century).

I've read the Epistles of Barnabas and Clement, and I think they're Good 
Stuff -- for me, there's more Good Stuff than in, say, Philemon.  I think 
that the material that the Gospel of Thomas from the Nag Hammadi Library 
shares with the synoptic Gospels is arguably of an older form than is found 
in the latter, that it reflects more accurately the Sayings Gospel form of 
the "Q text" that has for some time been postulated as underlying those 
three, that some of the material that *isn't* represented in the synoptics 
is probably Good Stuff, and some of that extra material is Garbage.  The 
problem is in determining which is Good Stuff and which is Garbage on the 
basis of content, and that's pretty much impossible, so I don't make 
theological or behavioral decisions based on the Gospel of Thomas.

I actively *disagree* with the author of Titus on the subject of the 
Universal Truth about Everyone from Crete (see 1:12ff); I have to say 
expressing this attitude, or acting on the generalizations expressed there, 
would not strike me as Christian behavior today.  Yet the Word of God says 
it, so it must be True.

Did Jesus include "... for thine is the Kingdom ..." when he told the 
Apostles how to pray, or was this a medieval scribal insertion that started 
out as a pious footnote?

Did John actually make trinitarian doctrine explicit in 1 John 5:6-5:7 (" 
... in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost:  And these three 
are one.  And ...", or was this a later addition (the Johannine Comma)  that 
first appeared in a late-first-millennium Spanish manuscript of the Vulgate 
and that was back-translated into Greek in the sixteenth century for the 
Textus Receptus?

Personally, I lean toward the idea that these were later *insertions* to the 
original text.  I do not hold that God saw fit to ensure that they were 
*inserted* into later manuscripts to lead to the Divine Perfection that the 
Textus Receptus represents.

> Either way, the effect is the same.  If it's Scripture, it's inspired of 
> God.  If you have something claiming to be Scripture but not inspired of 
> God, it's false.  In both cases, the profitability only applies to 
> inspired Scripture.

The import of the capitalized word "Scripture" as we understand it today may 
or may not be deduced from the Greek.  Revelation claims *for itself* that 
its modification is a Bad Idea.  2 Peter makes supportive claims for some 
collection of Paul's letters as that collection existed at the time.  I know 
of no other claim in any other New Testament book that self-identifies as 
Scripture.   The determination as to which book is Scripture and which book 
is Not Scripture, and even the determination of which verse or word within a 
verse is Scripture and which is Not Scripture, is a *human* decision.

>> And did Paul really mean "God-breathed", that every single letter of 
>> every single word of the passages being referred to was dictated directly 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> accepted into the canon at the time.

There are a bunch of *assumptions* in that paragraph that I've already 
addressed.   The question of direct Pauline authorship (which I seriously 
doubt) isn't relevant to the point as to what the author understood 
"theopneustos" to mean, or whether it was a postpositive adjective or a 
predicate adjective intended to follow an elided verb, or the body of 
writings that was being referred to as "graphe".  I think the premise "well, 
he *might* have meant the Old Testament when he wrote it, but what *God* 
really wanted the Apostle Paul (who died no later than 67AD) to convey, was 
that "graphe" *really* included a number of books that weren't going to be 
written for another forty years because, well, everybody knows that they 
ended up as scripture, so that's what God really must have meant.  Right?

I don't think so.

Which set of Greek, Hebrew and Aramaic texts -- and I mean specific 
editions -- reflects the One True God-Breathed set of information that 
should be regarded as Scripture, leaving the authors and supporters of all 
*additions* to be subjected to all the plagues, and the authors and 
supporters of all *subtractions* to lose their share in the tree of life and 
the holy city, as described in Revelation?

I've already gone on record as believing the *earlier* the textual witness, 
the more likely it is that it represents the *original* text as the (human) 
author wrote it.  Your premise seems to be that the *earlier* the textual 
witness, the more likely it is to diverge from God's Real Intent, as 
reflected in ... what?  The Textus Receptus, which contains 
back-translations from Latin because *no* Greek text could be found 
supporting the reading that the guy collecting these texts wanted reflected? 
I've asked this before.  It *does* make a difference.  The Johannine Comma 
is one *glaring* case.  There are others.  The Secret Gospel of Mark may be 
a case of an *elision* from the original text that may have some interesting 
theological implications.  The long-term disagreement among Christian groups 
as to what the list of Old Testament -- and even New Testament -- books 
*actually is* does make a difference (e.g., 2 Maccabees and the issue of 
purgatory).

> (In fact, "at the time", I imagine the church was a bit less organized 
> than it was a few centuries later.)

And?   The Church in question is what we know today as the Roman Catholic 
Church, yet I'm pretty sure you don't accept the canon they seem pretty much 
to have agreed upon by 500AD or so, and which they insisted upon at the 
Council of Trent in response to Luther's *rejection* of the Apocrypha and 
several New Testament books that you *do* accept?

>  God knew how it would be put together, and that's what He wanted written.

How do you prove either of these premises?  And explain to me how, if I 
accept the premise that the Bible, despite having been written down by man, 
is Divinely Perfect Just As It Is, I cannot *possibly* be worshipping a 
graven image, even if it be an image (graven by man) of God's Word?

How do *you* know that *He* wanted the Johannine Comma included and the 
Secret Gospel of Mark excluded?  How do *you* know the Syriacs and Luther 
*cannot* have had it right because they reject the very books that make the 
claim for New Testament Scripture (2 Peter and Revelation)?

> Is that supportable?

Not unless I want to do what looks to me like worshipping an idol.

> With all these questions, what do you believe?  Are these deep-rooted 
> questions, or are they "hey, I'm kinda curious about this - doesn't change 
> my view of it, I just wonder" type of questions?

They are fundamental to my understanding of what the Bible *is*, and what it 
*says*.  The etymology of the word "worship" is basically "to ship or 
deliver worth or respect".   I do not wish to *worship* the work of man, 
which also means I'm unwilling to attach more divine attributes to it than I 
believe are appropriate.

Do I believe the Bible as a whole is "useful for teaching ..."?  Yes, and 
that includes 2 Timothy in its entirety.

Do I believe God told Paul to write what was written in 2 Timothy, given 
that Paul's (and for that matter Jesus') understanding of what the word 
"graphe" referred to was different from what God *really* meant, that in 
addition to what Paul understood the Old Testament to contain at the time it 
would also include all of Paul's own letters and several books that hadn't 
even been written yet?   Uhhh ... no ...  And if you're a "sola scriptura" 
kind of guy, on what *scriptural* basis can you make such an assertion?

> I'm just trying to figure out how one could believe the Bible without 
> believing it to be the inspired, inerrant, infallible Word of God. Without 
> that solid foundation, how can anything else be built?

I believe the composition of the Bible was *guided* by God.  Did His will 
get followed 100% of the time in that process?  No, I don't think so.  I 
think there were many plain and precious parts left out, and I think there 
were many additions; *I do not import absolute divine perfection to it*.  Do 
I think God might have had a hand in the development of other books, both 
then and today?  Yes, I do.  The Bible is the ultimate *guide*.  It is *not* 
God Himself.

> (These questions aren't meant to imply that you don't - I'm just having 
> trouble understanding...)

The individual books of the Bible were composed by men, their contents have 
been corrupted by accidental and deliberate changes over nearly two 
millennia, and the composition of the collection of a whole is not the 
subject of universal agreement in Christendom.  That means for me to pick 
one as being the *perfect* representation of God's word, I must reject all 
other such representations as Satanic.  I don't think I have the knowledge 
to state which original text is the "real" Word of God as God Intended It to 
Be.  I know for me to take such a stance exhibits a whole lot of pride on my 
part that might be warranted, and if I treat any such collection as divinely 
Perfect, I believe I would be committing idolatry.

I respect the Bible and its contents, pretty much however it's composed.  My 
own research has led me to a greater *respect* for the Earlier Text position 
than for the Textus Receptus position, which I find as an example of what my 
father used to refer to as "magical thinking".

What I will not do is *worship* the Bible, something it appears to me you 
are suggesting I should do.

And I still don't have an answer to the question "which Bible is The 
Bible?", although I think it's clear I probably would *not* agree with your 
answer no matter what it was, since my Earliest Text position is subject to 
the discovery of Earlier Texts (in Greek or otherwise) than are presently 
accounted for, and is thus subject to change!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Wed, 5 Apr 2006 11:54:10 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>How do *you* know that *He* wanted the Johannine Comma included and the 
>Secret Gospel of Mark excluded?  How do *you* know the Syriacs and Luther 
>*cannot* have had it right because they reject the very books that make the 
>claim for New Testament Scripture (2 Peter and Revelation)?

The wording of the Bible that I was introduced to in my youth is the
very Word of God.   All other versions are wrong.   The beliefs that I
was introduced to in my youth are the beliefs that save people.   All
other beliefs will cause eternal suffering and punishment. 

I know that in my heart.   What others know in their hearts are
obviously wrong, and for this their punishment will be beyond severe.
Because my God is the omnipotent God of Love.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T21:11:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e113oh$ack$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <e113oh$ack$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> apply either the diagnosis or the solution to anybody else is quite another.

I'm guessing you're talking about your past in this - as I used that 
passage to Howard with no implication anywhere.

I appreciate your answers to my questions, which you provided without 
being defensive.  I'm not going to try to reply to all of them, but I 
did read them.

What it boils down to, for me, is this - there is one part of the Bible 
that is paramount above all others, and that is the need to accept 
Jesus' sacrifice in our place to atone for our sins.  Without that, it 
doesn't matter which canon is "right", or whether Protestants are 
missing out because we don't read Maccabees, Enoch, or Thomas.

Thanks again...  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 23)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T10:50:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e13kca$1puj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <e113oh$ack$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> passage to Howard with no implication anywhere.

The premise I was trying to get at was *applicability*.  You seem to be of 
the opinion that even though the addressing of this passage was explicitly 
to the Laodiceans and not to the Smyrnians, a Smyrnian reading it ought to 
pay *just as much* attention to it as to the passage that was specifically 
addressed *to the Smyrnians*.   And since I number neither Smyrnians nor the 
Laodiceans (or for that matter any Asians at all so far as I know) among my 
ancestry, I'm at some loss as to why I should pay particular attention to 
*these passages* if doing so might consume resources better spent on 
passages addressed to a more general audience!

> What it boils down to, for me, is this - there is one part of the Bible 
> that is paramount above all others, and that is the need to accept Jesus' 
> sacrifice in our place to atone for our sins.

That is a passage that is paramount *to you*.   As I indicated, I spent many 
decades concentrating on the sacrifice Jesus made.  For many years I'd 
gladly have taken a few hours nailed to a cross before dying as preferable 
to the various traumas of my childhood and teenage years.   I do not 
identify with that whole "sacrifice" thing very well.  Never really have.

"God is love; and he that dwelleth in love dwelleth in God, and God in him" 
is a far more important focus for me.

> It doesn't matter which canon is "right", or whether Protestants are 
> missing out because we don't read Maccabees, Enoch, or Thomas.

It does to a person that takes the position that a Bible that contains any 
of these is Not a Real Bible, and that consequently Revelation 22:18-19 
apply both to the compilers and to the readers of it ... ;-)

Personally, I don't think "inspiration" is closed now, much less was closed 
at the end of the apostolic period.  My point about such things is 
summarized by the comment a good friend, a Messianic Jew, once made:  "Put 
it in front of Torah.  If the light of Torah shines through it, it is good."

(Note that even before I discussed this with him, I came to the conclusion 
on linguistic and compositional grounds that, if any parts of the Bible were 
literally, letter-for-letter, word-for-word, "God-breathed", I'd have to say 
the Torah qualifies for me.  The intricacies and subtleties in the Hebrew of 
the first chapter of Genesis are nothing short of miraculous, as far as I'm 
concerned).

For some, the Light of Torah shines through Esdras, Maccabees, Revelation, 2 
Peter, Hebrews, etc.  For others it does not.  For some it shines through 
the first 164 pages of the book "Alcoholics Anonymous", for others it does 
not.   For some it shines through the Lamsa translation of the Peshitta, for 
others it would not, and some would seek to have the book confiscated and 
burned.    The same is true for the Book of Mormon.   I daresay for some the 
light shines through the Qu'ran, though I will admit, having read it in 
translation, that I'm not among them.

I say let the light shine through wherever it shines through.  But that's a 
whole 'nother topic.

> Thanks again...  :)

You're welcome!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> passages addressed to a more general audience!

We'll just have to disagree on this point then.  Why would God want the 
Laodiceans to "open their door" to Him, and not the Smyrnians?  Balanced 
against other Scripture, this is *very* applicable to every human who 
has ever lived!

>> What it boils down to, for me, is this - there is one part of the Bible 
>> that is paramount above all others, and that is the need to accept Jesus' 
>> sacrifice in our place to atone for our sins.
> 
> That is a passage that is paramount *to you*.

No, that is the paramount piece of the doctrine presented in the Bible. 
  Nearly everything in the Old Testament points to it, and without it, 
the New Testament is a waste of time.  The entire message of the Bible 
hinges on this point - regardless of the book's origins or who decided 
they should be part of the whole.

>> It doesn't matter which canon is "right", or whether Protestants are 
>> missing out because we don't read Maccabees, Enoch, or Thomas.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> apply both to the compilers and to the readers of it ... ;-)

True - but, though Revelation says that, Matthew 7:1 says "Judge not, 
lest ye be judged yourselves."  It's not my call to point out those who 
violate this.  :)

[snip]

> I say let the light shine through wherever it shines through.  But that's a 
> whole 'nother topic.

Heh - who would have thought that a thread titled "Any Comments?" would 
be taken so literally?  ;)

I honestly never dreamed my missive on WBC would take some of the turns 
this thread has.  It's certainly been a wild ride.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 23)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-06T16:18:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e147ku$25bc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <e113oh$ack$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET...

>> It would be equally presumptuous of us to assume or declare that somebody 
>> else had the same problem, and/or that it was our responsibility to point 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> passage to Howard with no implication anywhere.

I wasn't really clear about this.  Let me take another example that I think 
I've mentioned before.  (Direct Pauline authorship is assumed herein for the 
sake of simplicity).

In Titus 1, I read (using the NAB) "My purpose of leaving you in Crete was 
that you might accomplish what had been left undone, especially the 
appointment of presbyters in every town.  As I instructed you, a Presbyter 
must be irreproachable ...  In his teaching he must hold fast to the 
authentic message ... There are many irresponsible teachers ... They must be 
silenced ... A man of Crete, one of their own prophets, has testified 
'Cretans have ever been liars, beasts, and lazy gluttons,' and that is the 
simple truth!  Admonish them sharply ..."

Now, how should we read this passage?

*My* inclination is to read it that Paul was pretty tweaked about the 
behavior of *some*, or even *many*, of the elders he'd appointed there, and 
was simply venting his frustration through a generalization he knew NOT to 
be universal.    Moreover, the only people Paul is *really* concerned about 
here are the elders -- the "them" in "admonish them sharply" -- and the 
whole passage is intended as a caution to Titus to keep an eye on what 
*these elders* are teaching and to whip 'em into shape if they wander.

If the passage has significant applicability today, it's to the ministry as 
an admonishment for *them* to keep an eye on *each other's* teaching.

Now, if I take this as the Perfect, Unchanging Word of God, I can't presume 
that Paul wrote something the way he did because he was tweaked over a 
particular behavioral issue among a subset of the Christians on Crete, and 
moreover I have to presume that he wasn't just writing to Titus, he was 
writing to *everybody*.  Moreover, *God*, by directing Paul's pen in this 
particular way, is commanding us *today* to treat everybody who happens to 
be from (or on) the island of Crete as a bunch of louts whether *we* think 
their behavior today (individually or collectively) warrants it or not. 
They're liars NOT because they're in the habit of telling untruths, but 
because God said they were liars, and God wants *me* to treat them that way. 
End of discussion.

I just don't buy that conclusion, and I think the conclusion logically 
follows from the premises, so I don't buy the premises.

Paul (and maybe Titus too) apparently ordained a bunch of real jerks on 
Crete, had some serious problems with them, and Paul at least got his shorts 
all twisted up on that point.  But the fact that Paul lost his temper when 
he wrote this passage does *not* mean his commands ("admonish them sharply") 
apply outside of the particular context of concern.   And it wouldn't be the 
first time Paul lost his temper in writing -- 1 Cor 5 is contains some 
pretty harsh language as well -- but I don't see that passage *generalizing* 
the issues beyond the direct participants the way 1 Titus does.

*That's* what I mean by applicability issues.

    -Chuck Stevens
```
