- **In-Reply-To:** `<e147ku$25bc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <e113oh$ack$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET> <e147ku$25bc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now, how should we read this passage?

I believe your first interpretation here is the right one...

> *My* inclination is to read it that Paul was pretty tweaked about the 
> behavior of *some*, or even *many*, of the elders he'd appointed there, and 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> an admonishment for *them* to keep an eye on *each other's* teaching.

Yup...

> Now, if I take this as the Perfect, Unchanging Word of God, I can't presume 
> that Paul wrote something the way he did because he was tweaked over a 
> particular behavioral issue among a subset of the Christians on Crete, and 
> moreover I have to presume that he wasn't just writing to Titus, he was 
> writing to *everybody*.

Why not?  You're taking one half ultra-literally, while generalizing the 
other half.  If it always applies to people from Crete, then it also 
only applied to Titus.  Your rendition above (minsters, keep each other 
in line) generalizes *both* sides of the equation.

And that's what I mean when I said that even the Smyrnans could learn 
from what was told to the Laodiceans - and we can learn from it as well. 
  If we read the Scriptures for the principles found therein, and 
balance them against other principles to help interpret what we read, we 
can glean these general principles from a specific situation.

> Moreover, *God*, by directing Paul's pen in this 
> particular way, is commanding us *today* to treat everybody who happens to 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> End of discussion.

They don't call them "cretins" for nothin'!  ;)

> Paul (and maybe Titus too) apparently ordained a bunch of real jerks on 
> Crete, had some serious problems with them, and Paul at least got his shorts 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the issues beyond the direct participants the way 1 Titus does.

Too, Jesus' harshest rebukes were for the moneychangers in the temple, 
and the religious leaders who were fakes.  I don't see sharp 
admonishment as losing his temper.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-07T10:21:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e16722$9nl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <e113oh$ack$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <7e4e9$443478e0$45491d7a$27836@KNOLOGY.NET> <e147ku$25bc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <49ee7$4435bd75$45491d7a$17592@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:49ee7$4435bd75$45491d7a$17592@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> I believe your first interpretation here is the right one...

Well, it should go without saying that I do, too ...     ;-)

>> *My* inclination is to read it that Paul was pretty tweaked about the 
>> behavior of *some*, or even *many*, of the elders he'd appointed there, 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> line) generalizes *both* sides of the equation.

The passage of most interest here (NAB again) is " 'Cretans have ever been 
liars, beasts, and lazy gluttons,' and that is the simple truth!".  *Paul* 
is the one that seconded this and went on to characterize it as "the simple 
truth" about Cretans -- not just some of the presbyters who were messing up, 
but Cretans *in general* -- not I.

The logic is: If Paul wrote it, and it ended up in the Bible, that means God 
dictated it, and every word of it is God's perfect truth, then and now.

> They don't call them "cretins" for nothin'!  ;)

In all seriousness, I thought the etymology of "cretin" really was "someone 
from Crete" and had its current semantics at least in part because of this 
passage (I think I was told that by someone who was in a position to know 
back too many decades ago).

However, I checked to be sure, and found that Merriam-Webster disagrees on 
that point: "Fr. dial. *cretin*, Christian, human being, kind of idiot found 
in the Alps [1779])".   No mention of Crete.

I don't have an OED here at the office, and I would defer to that (because 
its etymological entries are *much* more detailed than M-W's) if it differed 
substantially, but I'd be really surprised if M-W and Oxford disagreed in 
any significant way on the etymology of the word "cretin".  When I think of 
it at home I'll double-check the OED.

> Too, Jesus' harshest rebukes were for the moneychangers in the temple, and 
> the religious leaders who were fakes.  I don't see sharp admonishment as 
> losing his temper.  :)

Paul didn't admonish the individual Cretan presbyters who were misbehaving, 
he told Titus to take care of it.  That's not the problem I see with the 
passage.

First he quoted somebody as saying "Cretans always [are] liars, evil wild 
beasts, lazy gluttons" (Berry's Interlinear of the TR) with the indication 
"Hey, this is one of their own folks saying this!.  Even that's not the 
issue.

What he did next was characterize and second that opinion as absolute truth. 
There's *no* implicit or explicit indication that the unnamed prophet's 
opinion -- or Paul's support for it -- applies to anything less than the set 
of all Cretans, living as well as dead.   *That's* where I think Paul lost 
his cool.

We can say "Yeah, well, I know that's what he *said*, but it's not really 
what he *meant*" -- which is pretty much what I've done.

But to the literalist, the "simple truth" is that the Bible says Cretans are 
liars, beasts and lazy gluttons, Paul agrees, and that means God agrees if 
God dictated the Bible and the Bible is without any errors (semantic, 
grammatical, diplomatic or otherwise).   Nobody from Crete is, or ever has 
been, or ever will be, fundamentally able to rise above that 
characterization.  "God dictated it, Paul wrote it, I believe it, and that's 
the end of it."  That's not the camp I'm in, at least in part because that 
sort of generalization is what is logically required from the absolute 
literalist position.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T18:35:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49p3tqFpri2bU2@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c58a9$44332e6f$45491d7a$872@KNOLOGY.NET> <e113oh$ack$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:e113oh$ack$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[393 more quoted lines elided]…
>    -Chuck Stevens
Chuck, I found this fascinating. Thanks for posting it. It gives insight 
into why you personally would care about commas and capitals, and I can see 
your point.

However, as I observed earlier, I don't believe we can ever get closer to 
understanding this than to accept the general gist of what is reported to 
have been said, (just as we do with modern newspapers, and we all know how 
often what is there is mis-reported, mis-emphasised, or edited to spin the 
meaning.)

Maybe someday technology will enable us to get to the point in the universe 
where the light waves from those events are now, and we can see it for 
ourselves. (We'd still have to recreate the sound waves by manipulating the 
air molecules in our viewing environment, but a technology that could 
transcend Relativity to bring us the pictures would probably have no trouble 
with the sound :-)). Until then, I contend it is impossible to know the 
intention of the speaker, and the stress and emphasis that was made at the 
time.

A general consensus of reports can simply give us an insight. We should not 
get too wound up about the details of the reports...

And I found your idea of placing to much emphasis on the Bible until it 
becomes like idolatry a very interesting one.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 20)_

- **From:** roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T06:38:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144244335.128539.177090@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> of four or five centuries.

I am unclear why the canon of the NT being so assembled demonstrates
that the bible is not 'monolithic.'

> The premise that the Apostles decided the contents of the Bible, Old and New
> Testament, as we now have it, is quite simply not supported by the
> historical record.

You do not say why we should suppose this.  The canon of the OT is that
which the apostles used.  As for the 'contents of the NT', it was
assembled from their writings.  I'm afraid that complaining that the
apostles didn't compile it in their lifetime seems a curious complaint
to me.

> > As far as a Scriptural basis, as Paul wrote to Timothy,
>
> ... direct authorship is in significant scholarly dispute ...

On religious or political issues, the consensus of scholars is only
that of those who appoint them.  A century ago these same scholars
asserted all sorts of tripe on these issues, since shown to be wrong.

> And did Paul really mean "God-breathed", that every single letter of every
> single word of the passages being referred to was dictated directly by God?

Who knows?  But does it matter?  If so, why and to whom?  The sense in
which he meant it is clear enough.

> And as to "graphe"  (="writing"), I think the big question is what's he
> referring to?   If Paul himself wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been written
> before his execution under Nero, whose reign ended in 67AD.

Agreed.

> From what I
> read, the earliest plausible dates for the Gospels are:  Matthew, after
> 70AD and probably about 85AD; Mark, around 70AD; Luke/Acts, about 75AD, and
> John, 90-100AD.

Such dates are curious.  Luke/Acts must have been composed ca. 61 AD
since Paul is in prison in it, and his release, his further journeys,
his execution, the criminalisation of Christianity, and the destruction
of Judaism as recorded in  it, all happen in the next few years, yet
are not mentioned.  It was therefore probably written in Rome.

Luke also uses some form of Mark, verbatim.  Yet Mark was not completed
until after Peter's death.  Thus we get proto-Mark's -- likely enough,
since Luke tells us of such things and Mark was recording Peter's
preaching.

Matthew is undateable, except after Mark in the same way, and before
John.

John was clearly written after the execution of Peter, and also rather
later than the others.  Ca. 90 AD is reasonable, since John died in 100
AD.

> If Paul wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been before his
> martyrdom.  He thus would have had to foresee the acceptance of four, only
> four, and exactly four Gospels as deserving of being considered "scripture"
> *that had not yet even been written*!

Why?  He is referred to the OT, of course; but the words of Jesus must
always have been considered scripture also, by definition.

> The distribution and collection of Paul's letters occurred
> in the century after his death, and the solidification of the New Testament
> canon occurred three centuries and more after that.

Since Tertullian, ca. 200, is working with very much the same NT as we
are -- and indeed using the term! -- this must be mistaken.

> That I find a passage in the New Testament useful does not have anything to
> do with whether I think "pasa graphe theopneustos" in 2 Timothy refers to
> anything written after, say, 49AD.  I don't, and that includes the letters
> to the Churches in Asia in Revelation.

If we reject the teachings of Jesus as recorded by the apostles and
preached in the churches, but accept the OT, I think this makes us a
Jew.

> By the way, I also believe that Revelation 22:18-19 can only be reasonably
> taken to refer to the Book of Revelation.  The word used in Greek for "book"
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> prophecy ...") argues strongly against the plausibility of such a view.

No doubt the author of Revelation thought this.  But no doubt the
compilers of the canon rightly consider that the same applies to all
the rest.  I'm not sure why you would have a problem with this.

> And is Martin Luther's "part ... of the book of life" taken out because he
> didn't much care for Hebrews, James, Jude and Revelation?

I'm afraid that, as an argument against the protestant view of
scripture, this appears to be a jibe rather than a reasoned argument.

> I will note that 2 Peter 3:15ff elevates the writings of Paul to the level
> of Scripture (="graphas"); the question I have is direct Petrine authorship
> of this epistle, and the consequential dating of this epistle (as well as
> the assembly of Paul's complete collected letters) as having been completed
> before 67AD.

While it was undoubtedly disputed by some in antiquity, the consensus
of those far better informed than we are was that it was genuine.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Deconstructing WBC)

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-05T13:30:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e119d7$djd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <e6ht121003qannkpbja0u519pqs64p8dn3@4ax.com> <dvpj9a$s0b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4e12c$44209c32$45491d7a$12452@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs3tr$2d1j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b7e44$44222a08$45491d7a$12848@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuiko$qt6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1c2e5$44304aa2$45491d7a$29570@KNOLOGY.NET> <g1n032lu3rn8lpfu862o2p5f7k5mmg22hd@4ax.com> <636bc$44307cca$45491d7a$2408@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0rfam$318o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eb911$4431db8b$45491d7a$24058@KNOLOGY.NET> <e0u9ul$1m2p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1144244335.128539.177090@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com>`

```

<roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1144244335.128539.177090@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com...

> I am unclear why the canon of the NT being so assembled demonstrates
> that the bible is not 'monolithic.'

See for example the rejection by Luther, as well as some Orthodox 
Christians, of certain books in the New Testament.

I do not believe the assembly of the Bible *resulted* in a monolith, because 
there are any number of *accepted* lists of the books that comprise it, as 
well as any number of disagreements as to what the original texts are.  This 
is a matter of historical and currently-observable fact; all you have to do 
is look at the Table of Contents of a Roman Catholic Bible, and compare it 
with a King James version from anytime after about 1830!

>> The premise that the Apostles decided the contents of the Bible, Old and 
>> New
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> to me.

The Canon of the Old Testament for Roman Catholics, Greek Orthodox, and 
Protestants does not include the Book of Enoch, which the Epistle of Jude 
cites explicitly at Verse 14.  If the author of Jude was an Apostle, and he 
used the Book of Enoch in his writings, why isn't it included?

> As for the 'contents of the NT', it was
> assembled from their writings.

There are other writings that are arguably authentic that weren't included, 
and some of those that were accepted as being directly written by Apostles 
during the collection process that almost certainly weren't.   Who did the 
assembly, and was each and every one of their choices divinely inspired? 
What direct evidence do we have that makes it necessary for us to accept 
that?

> I'm afraid that complaining that the
> apostles didn't compile it in their lifetime seems a curious complaint
> to me.

I'm not talking about in *their* lifetime, or even in the subapostolic 
period.  The New Testament canon was at least somewhat fluid for some 
centuries, and some books (Hebrews, 2 Peter, Jude, Revelation in particular) 
have been controversial all along.   The Old Testament canon was even more 
fluid, particularly in the East, remained so during the Great Schism, and 
remains fundamentally different among several groups to this day, both 
between the East and the West and between Protestants and Catholic/Orthodox. 
Which list is *the only true, correct, and divinely perfect* list, and why?

> On religious or political issues, the consensus of scholars is only
> that of those who appoint them.  A century ago these same scholars
> asserted all sorts of tripe on these issues, since shown to be wrong.

There is no such thing as independent scholars?   And the *same* scholars 
made assertions both recently and a century ago?   Really?

Who appointed the scholars *you* respect, and what preconcieved ideas might 
*they* have brought to the table?  Or are the *only* impartial people those 
who agree with your position?

> Who knows?  But does it matter?  If so, why and to whom?  The sense in
> which he meant it is clear enough.

No, I don't think it is that clear, and yes, I do think it matters.  It 
matters whether God breathed the Johannine Comma or the SGM, or even whether 
Jesus included " ... for Thine is the Kingdom ..." in his instructions on 
how to pray, and whether God the Father *really* intended those words or 
that sentiment to be included.   If I am to assert, for example, that the 
doctrine of the Trinity is clearly and explicitly expressed in the Word of 
God, it follows that I accept the Johannine Comma as part of God-Breathed 
text and not a later (however pious and well-intentioned) scribal insertion.

>> And as to "graphe"  (="writing"), I think the big question is what's he
>> referring to?   If Paul himself wrote 2 Timothy, it has to have been 
>> written
>> before his execution under Nero, whose reign ended in 67AD.

> Agreed.

If Paul isn't the direct author, though, it could date as late as the early 
second century.

>> From what I
>> read, the earliest plausible dates for the Gospels are:  Matthew, after
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Such dates are curious.  Luke/Acts must have been composed ca. 61 AD

"Early Christian tradition ascribes the composition of the Lucan gospel and 
Acts of the Apostles to approximately 75AD ..." (NAB footnotes).    Why is 
that footnote wrong and your opinion right?

> since Paul is in prison in it, and his release, his further journeys,
> his execution, the criminalisation of Christianity, and the destruction
> of Judaism as recorded in  it, all happen in the next few years, yet
> are not mentioned.

"Many attempts have been made to explain what seems to be the abrupt ending 
of Luke's Acts of the Apostles.  The author has Paul still in detention, 
though he intimates a bright outcome for his case.  Perhaps the most 
satisfactory explanation is that Luke does not regard the subsequent 
missionary activity of Paul or his martyrdom as necessary for his account of 
the origin and development of the church."  Again, why is your scholarship 
superior to that underlying the footnotes of the NAB?

> It was therefore probably written in Rome.

And?   ... The NAB notes don't speculate on where Luke wrote these books.

> Luke also uses some form of Mark, verbatim.

And?  So does Matthew.  Your point, if Luke was completed 5 years after Mark 
was completed?

> Yet Mark was not completed
> until after Peter's death.

"Following the suggestion of Clement of Alexandria and Origen, the majority 
of modern scholars consider the gospel to have been written in Rome.  The 
date of composition is c 70 A.D."  (ibid.)

What's your point?

Also, "The Petrine influence on Mark should not be exaggeraed.  Modern 
scholership has shown quite clearly that Mark's sources cannot be explained 
solely in terms of Peter's preaching.  ..."

> Thus we get proto-Mark's -- likely enough,

Well, we get an underlying set of sayings, but I'm not sure about 
"proto-Marks".

> since Luke tells us of such things

Not necessarily Mark; could even be a prototype of the Gospel of Thomas, 
which I suspect you don't hold in much esteem!

> and Mark was recording Peter's
> preaching.

... see above ...

> Matthew is undateable, except after Mark in the same way, and before
> John.

"In the judgment of modern scholars, ... this ... is a theological 
development within the gospel trdition that requires a date for the Gospel 
of Matthew later than that of Mark and Luke.  Current and more common 
opinion dates the composition of the Gospel of Matthew between 800-100AD, 
or, roughly, 85AD.  ..."

All that means for this discussion is that if Paul actually wrote 2 Timothy, 
and if Paul really intended to include the Gospel of Matthew among "all 
Scripture", then he had to have had some foreknowledge that the Gospel was 
*going* to be written, and that in addition it was *going* to be divinely 
inspired, and *should then* be included in Scripture *as he understood it*.

I just don't find that *plausible*.

> John was clearly written after the execution of Peter, and also rather
> later than the others.  Ca. 90 AD is reasonable, since John died in 100
> AD.

Yeah, that's what I said, though parts of it might have been earlier: . 
"The final editing of the gospel and arrangement in its present for probably 
dates between A. D. 90 and 100." (ibid).

> Why?  He is referred to the OT, of course; but the words of Jesus must
> always have been considered scripture also, by definition.

Well, that brings up another point.  The Roman Catholic Church has always 
held that authentic Words of Jesus were maintained in the *oral* tradition. 
A prerequisite to being considered *scripture* is, by definition, that they 
were *written down*.    "In the beginning there were Four Gospels ..."?  No, 
I don't think so.  It seems to me that the Christian reverence for the early 
writings -- Paul's letters as well as the Gospels -- *grew* over time.  I 
don't think, for example, that the Gospel of Mark was completed on a Tuesday 
in Rome, and was added to the Old Testament as part of Holy Writ on the next 
day by the Christians in Jerusalem at the suggestion of the local Bishop 
because God told him so (or because he knew Paul was going to tell him so in 
a letter he hadn't seen yet).  That just doesn't seem to me to be a likely 
scenario.

> Since Tertullian, ca. 200, is working with very much the same NT as we
> are -- and indeed using the term! -- this must be mistaken.

"Very much the same" is not the same as "the same".  Tertullian was in 
Carthage.  What of those Christians in Damascus that didn't work with the 
same NT, and *still don't*?

> If we reject the teachings of Jesus as recorded by the apostles and
> preached in the churches, but accept the OT, I think this makes us a
> Jew.

Who said I did that?


> No doubt the author of Revelation thought this.  But no doubt the
> compilers of the canon rightly consider that the same applies to all
> the rest.  I'm not sure why you would have a problem with this.

Partly because "biblion" is a diminutive.  If the author of Revelation were 
referring to anything outside of the immediate text at hand, he would not 
have used that word; "biblos" would be more appropriate.

If the assemblers of the Canon thought God blessed their work enough to 
bring all the plagues described in Revelation to anyone who thought maybe 
they missed a phrase or an entire book that should have been included, or 
that God condemned a person that thought something they included was maybe 
spurious to eternal damnation, I'd say that's pretty arrogant of them, and 
I'd be inclined to suggest maybe *their* inspiration was suspect on the 
grounds of arrogance alone!

That begs the question of "assembled *WHICH* canon?"


> I'm afraid that, as an argument against the protestant view of
> scripture, this appears to be a jibe rather than a reasoned argument.

Well, Luther is recorded as having rejected these books *as scripture*. 
Their reincorporation came later.


>> I will note that 2 Peter 3:15ff elevates the writings of Paul to the 
>> level
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> of those far better informed than we are was that it was genuine.

"Early Christian tradition was not so impressed by the claim of Petrine 
authorship of this epistle ... as to concede its authenticity without 
question.  Both Origen (185 - 254) and St. Jerome (342-420) found it 
debatable.  In the early fifth century the epistle was accepted into the 
canon of the Scriptures. ... Only after the destruction of Jerusalem, in 70 
A.D., did Christians begin slowly to realize that the parousia was reserved 
for a date in the remote future ... The epistle was written when Christian 
teaching was placing less emphasis on the proximity of the parousia, and 
therefore not while Peter was still living."  (ibid.)

I'm citing footnotes in a Bible I have handy, and I can probably support 
similar premises from The New Jerome Biblical Commentary, edited by the late 
Raymond E. Brown.  Luther certainly didn't think 2 Peter was scripture, 
which carries the implication that either (a) he didn't think much of Peter, 
apostle or no, or (b) he didn't think Peter the Apostle wrote it.

What references do you have for the *consensus* of modern (Catholic, 
Protestant, Orthodox and otherwise) scholars that, *contra* both Origen and 
Jerome, the Apostle Peter wrote this epistle (and therefore it was completed 
before the end of 67AD)?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> <roger_pearse@yahoo.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:1144244335.128539.177090@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christians, of certain books in the New Testament.

Curious.  Luther's bible contains the same books as everyone else's.
Some Eastern churches simply never took part in the final stages of the
process.

But neither of these addresses my question, I think.

> I do not believe the assembly of the Bible *resulted* in a monolith, because
> there are any number of *accepted* lists of the books that comprise it

Nope.

> as well as any number of disagreements as to what the original texts are.

I am unclear what this means or how it relates to the point above.

> This
> is a matter of historical and currently-observable fact; all you have to do
> is look at the Table of Contents of a Roman Catholic Bible, and compare it
> with a King James version from anytime after about 1830!

I'm afraid that the fact that this proposition has to be stated in such
vague terms is a damning refutation of it.  All we are saying is that
protestants and catholics don't agree about the OT apocrypha.  Yes
indeed.  So?  <smile>

> >> The premise that the Apostles decided the contents of the Bible, Old and
> >> New Testament, as we now have it, is quite simply not supported by the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> used the Book of Enoch in his writings, why isn't it included?

I could tell you.  But I won't, because, frankly, I don't see how this
supports your assertion.  Paul quotes Menander.  Does that make
Menander part of the OT?

> > As for the 'contents of the NT', it was
> > assembled from their writings.
>
> There are other writings that are arguably authentic that weren't included,

Such as?

> and some of those that were accepted as being directly written by Apostles
> during the collection process that almost certainly weren't.

In the opinion of whom?

> Who did the assembly, and was each and every one of their
> choices divinely inspired?

I am unclear why we are required to know this.

> What direct evidence do we have that makes it necessary for us to accept
> that?

I am unclear why we are required to know this.

What we have here is an ever-growing list of demands for knowledge
about the process of canonisation, and an unstated presumption that
unless I, living 15 centuries later, can answer every one of them, then
this proves that the bible cannot be inspired.  Pardon me, but this is
an invalid form of argument, and a non sequitur.  Arguments that never
quite get stated often have these problems.  But you are welcome to
prove to me that this argument has validity.

> > I'm afraid that complaining that the
> > apostles didn't compile it in their lifetime seems a curious complaint
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> period.

Then I am afraid that I do not understand your comment, since you were
making such a demand.

> The New Testament canon was at least somewhat fluid ...

Yes.  We know.  Indeed the canon of the OT is, in a sense, not agreed
even now.  Yet neither Catholics nor protestants have a problem.  This
tells us that the argument, again, is misconceived.

> > On religious or political issues, the consensus of scholars is only
> > that of those who appoint them.  A century ago these same scholars
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> who agree with your position?

What a lot of nonsense!  Kindly address my comment, rather than come up
with these evasions.  You may trust a scholar who tells you "believe my
religion!" if he has a doctorate.  I do not.  We may trust a scholar
who tells us that a manuscript was written in a chinese hand of the 4th
century.  Only a fool would trust a scholar who tells us that this
proves that socialist-dialectic therefore destroys the logic of
capitalism.

I am sorry that you adopt arguments because you find them convenient.
It is usually best to *suspect* such arguments.

(Various points snipped where context is lost)

> >> From what I read, the earliest plausible dates for the Gospels are:  Matthew, after
> >> 70AD and probably about 85AD; Mark, around 70AD; Luke/Acts, about 75AD,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> that footnote wrong and your opinion right?

I envy you your faith in a modern footnote, but I'm sorry to tell you
that it is bunk. There is nothing in 'early Christian tradition' to
that effect.  But by all means produce some ancient writer who says
otherwise.

> > since Paul is in prison in it, and his release, his further journeys,
> > his execution, the criminalisation of Christianity, and the destruction
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> superior to that underlying the footnotes of the NAB?

Why can't you think for yourself?  How is the above 'scholarship'?
Where are the facts?

(Various comments snipped that have the same problem: unquestioning
credulity)

> > It was therefore probably written in Rome.
> > Luke also uses some form of Mark, verbatim.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> which I suspect you don't hold in much esteem!

By all means offer evidence for this assertion.

> > and Mark was recording Peter's preaching.
> > Matthew is undateable, except after Mark in the same way, and before
> > John.

(More blind faith snipped)

> All that means for this discussion is that if Paul actually wrote 2 Timothy,
> and if Paul really intended to include the Gospel of Matthew among "all
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I just don't find that *plausible*.

This is a strawman, tho, as I have noted.

> > Why?  He is referred to the OT, of course; but the words of Jesus must
> > always have been considered scripture also, by definition.
>
> Well, that brings up another point... (snip)

One at a time, please...

> > Since Tertullian, ca. 200, is working with very much the same NT as we
> > are -- and indeed using the term! -- this must be mistaken.
>
> "Very much the same" is not the same as "the same".

More demands.  Sorry, but just inventing 'problems' doesn't really
constitute an argument for your position.  Life is full of complexity.

> Tertullian was in Carthage.  What of those Christians in Damascus
> that didn't work with the same NT, and *still don't*?

<chuckle>  This really sounds desperate.  By all means work with a
West-Syriac canon, if you prefer -- in Syriac, of course.

> > If we reject the teachings of Jesus as recorded by the apostles and
> > preached in the churches, but accept the OT, I think this makes us a
> > Jew.
>
> Who said I did that?

You did.

> > No doubt the author of Revelation thought this.  But no doubt the
> > compilers of the canon rightly consider that the same applies to all
> > the rest.  I'm not sure why you would have a problem with this.
>
> Partly because "biblion" is a diminutive.

This, frankly, is neither here nor there.

> If the author of Revelation were
> referring to anything outside of the immediate text at hand, he would not
> have used that word; "biblos" would be more appropriate.

Did you not read my comment?

Out of time.

RP

"Clark Morris" <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message 
news:qtk122tvefqdhj0p75es9ccam7q4ldi14e@4ax.com...

> I am fairly certain that I agree with you but could you give a brief
> synopsis of what the Westboro Baptist Church is doing and why they are
> going to Asheville to preach their message?

As a starting point, I'd like to defer to the Wikipedia page on the Westboro 
Baptist Church; see 
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Westboro_Baptist_Church_%28Topeka%29 .  You can 
get their website name from there; as I said before, I'd rather not dignify 
it by posting it.

There was also a fairly lengthy report on Good Morning America on ABC-TV 
last Monday on the WBC's protest (and the PGR response) at Sgt. Gordon 
Misner's funeral in Colorado Springs, CO.  The report, and the video, can be 
reached at http://abcnews.go.com/GMA/US/story?id=1745591&page=1 .

There's also a report from CNN  at 
http://www.cnn.com/2006/US/03/06/btsc.lavandrera.funerals/index.html .

And an interview with the Phelps clan by a CBS reporter from 1998 can be 
found at
http://www.cbsnews.com/stories/1998/11/24/eveningnews/main23493.shtml .

These reports should give you a pretty good idea of their stated purpose.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

> On Sat, 18 Mar 2006 20:46:08 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

I think we can get a pretty good picture...  Did you hear that in the 
new liberated, democratic Afghanistan, they've sentenced a man to die 
for converting to Christianity?  Never mind that he did it years ago in 
another country - in fact, his conversion predated the law by several 
years.  None of that seems to matter though...

http://www.voanews.com/english/2006-03-18-voa7.cfm

Funny, I can't remember the last time a court in this country actually 
sentenced someone to die because they converted to Islam...

> We can't
> appreciate Mark Twain without a basic "Child's Picture Book of the
> Bible" knowledge.

As well we shouldn't.  The Bible contains the basis for many of the most 
important precepts in our founding documents, as well as our culture and 
entertainment (which covers Twain).

> We don't learn USAmerican history without knowing

Study them, of course!  :)  There are many texts that describe the 
tenets of various religions, easily available for perusal online.  Some 
of these may contain proselytizing, but a studios soul could discern 
what is fact and what is proselytizing.

Many denominations base their religion on the same Bible in which I 
believe.  That bible contains John 14:6, in which Jesus says "I am the 
Way, the Truth and the Life.  No man cometh to the Father but by Me." 
Is that proselytizing, or is that a basic tenet of the Christian faith?

(The basic tenets of the Constitution are 'to form a more perfect union,

lovely rhetoric, is not a precedent - should be, in and of it'sself, 
sufficiently telling.)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-22T00:32:59+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<48aa42Fiq8srU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvnltl$l45$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:dvnltl$l45$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
Hear! Hear! Very well stated, Doc.

Religion and politics are far too explosive a mixture to be condoned.

The state should guarantee freedom of religion, insofar as that religion 
DOES NO HARM to the people who do not ascribe to it. As soon as they start 
burning heretics (or even threatening to), close it down. Extreme behaviour 
in the name of religion should be grounds for banning the practise of said 
religion. In other words, practice your faith gently and personally, with 
respect for the rights of others to agree or disagree.

I cannot see a case for laws based on ANY religion, whether Islamic, 
Christian, Dervish, or whatever.

Laws should be about the welfare and protection of citizens, not concerned 
with their morals.

And Religion has no monopoly on morality, anyway. Yet I have had people say 
to me (on finding I am an atheist): "How can you behave right? How do you 
know what is right and what is wrong? How can you be moral if you don't 
believe in God?"

I can do it because I have senses to perceive with and a brain to process 
the information. My common sense tells me that right behaviour will make 
society a better place and wrong behaviour won't.

It isn't rocket science... If you want to live in a better world, try to be 
a better person. No divine inspiration required, just some taking of 
responsibility and the desire to be better than you were. Growth, if you 
like.

"For the sin that ye do by two and by two
 Ye must pay for one by one.
 And the God that you took from a printed book
 Be with you, Tomlinson."

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-21T11:35:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET...

> That bible contains John 14:6, in which Jesus says "I am the Way, the 
> Truth and the Life.  No man cometh to the Father but by Me." Is that 
> proselytizing, or is that a basic tenet of the Christian faith?

I'd question the accuracy of "by" as a good *modern* English translation of 
the Greek "dia" (actually, the last vowel is elided because of proximity to 
the opening vowel of "emou" in the Textus Receptus at hand).

I'd prefer "through", with the same ambiguity in Greek as exists in English, 
as a better translation of this word.

Many take "by" in this context to imply Jesus is acting as the Cosmic 
Bouncer, and you gotta get by him to get to the Father.   I don't think 
that's supportable from the Greek.

My understanding of the passage is that Jesus is indicating *His role* in 
*facilitating* such communications -- whether we as individuals recognize it 
or not.

All this, of course, depends on what one means by "the Bible".   Is the KJV 
"The Bible"?

And if you're quoting the KJV as "the Bible", I note with some interest that 
"way", "truth", "life" and "me" are not capitalized in the version at 
www.kingjamesbible.com .  By convention the capitalization of these words 
includes an implication that isn't present in the text you're citing (you 
also left out a comma before "but", but I don't believe that has the 
theological import that those initial capitals do).   I don't object to 
quoting scripture; what does tend to bother me is *mis*quoting it in ways 
that can be viewed as supporting your point!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> as a better translation of this word.

That works, and is how I viewed this particular passage even before your 
reply.  In fact, the NIV says "except through Me."

> Many take "by" in this context to imply Jesus is acting as the Cosmic 
> Bouncer, and you gotta get by him to get to the Father.   I don't think 
> that's supportable from the Greek.

Heh...  The "Cosmic Bouncer" - sounds like a wrestler!  (In fact, if I 
ever start wrestling, I think that'll be my name...)

> My understanding of the passage is that Jesus is indicating *His role* in 
> *facilitating* such communications -- whether we as individuals recognize it 
> or not.

Right - and, whether you think of "by" as the bouncer or the "through", 
the point of the passage is pretty much the same.

> All this, of course, depends on what one means by "the Bible".   Is the KJV 
> "The Bible"?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> theological import that those initial capitals do).

I was taught that nouns and pronouns that refer to God are to be 
capitalized.  Do you contend that "way", "truth", "life", and "me" do 
not fit that categorization?

(FWIW - the New American Standard and the Holman Christian Standard 
Bible capitalize the "Me"; The Message capitalizes all the words I did 
(although "way" becomes "road"); and the 21st Century KJV [my personal 
favorite] is identical to my quote (minus the missing comma).)

> I don't object to 
> quoting scripture; what does tend to bother me is *mis*quoting it in ways 
> that can be viewed as supporting your point!

My *point*, if you read the original thread, was about whether it's 
possible to "study" a religion without being proselytized by it.  I 
listed that verse more as a discussion point - is it possible to read 
that as part of a study, and not feel that one is being proselytized?

(Personally, I think it is.  I have yet to see Mr. Brazee's reply, though.)
```

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You raise an interesting point, Daniel.

I believe it is possible to study anything new and keep an open mind, but it 
isn't easy.  (We see people here measuring new information against what they 
know already, before they have really looked at or used the new information. 
It's one reason I'm not so keen to respond to posts about OO and 
components.)

The classic example, I think, is people studying philosophical logic for the 
first time and encountering Aristotle. The awesome simplicity of a two 
valued logic system is very attractive to most people and the majority of 
students (self included, when I first encountered it) measure the remainder 
of the course by applying Aristotlean reasoning to it. We see it in everyday 
life, where people who are proud of their logical reasoning ability often 
mean that they have a good grasp of Aristotlean logic and rhetoric. Later, 
when one encounters completely different systems (some of which are multi or 
even infinity valued) Aristotle, despite the great brilliance of this 
ancient mind, pales into insignificance. (That doesn't mean he is worthless; 
far from it...:-) it just means that different logic systems may deal with 
different types of problem... differently.

I spent over 20 years studying comparative religions, both by reading and 
conversing, and visiting important sites. I'm an atheist. If I was 
proselytized, it certainly didn't work very well... :-)

Pete.
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:i9r222dhickk71p6973hs49av8l8c3tckc@4ax.com...
>
> Teaching *my* religion on an equal basis with *your*
> religion implies that they both have equal value, when in fact, *my*
> religion is the Truth, and yours is an artifact of Statan.

    I took one of those "Which Religion Is The Right One For You?" quizzes 
on the Internet, and it told me I should look into Satanism. So I took at 
look, expecting something along the lines of encouraging rape, torture, 
sacrificing goats on altars surrounded by candles arranged in a circle, etc. 
but instead found something that seemed to be promoting utilitarialism, 
which is very compatible with my life philosophies. Satanism doesn't require 
one to believe in the existence of an actual being called "Satan", as some 
people might assume.

    However, I don't claim to be a Satanist because:

    (1) I don't fully understand what this "religion" is about yet, and so 
maybe there's some aspects to it that I'll fundamentally disagree with;
    (2) I suspect a lot of people associate (prejudicial?) connotations with 
the term "Satanist", and that's not the sort of connotations I want to 
convey.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-22T10:45:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET...
>> All this, of course, depends on what one means by "the Bible".   Is the 
>> KJV "The Bible"?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> capitalized.

And I was taught when quoting scripture to quote it *exactly*, at the risk 
of altering the meaning.  Few things will send me up the wall than having 
somebody "adjust" the text of Scripture so that it can be shown more clearly 
to support your position.

In this instance, you seemed to be quoting the KJV/AV, and in that recension 
the nouns aren't capitalized, even though Father is.   Part of the reason, I 
think, is that these are Jesus' words in quotation, and it's my opinion that 
*He* would have thought it at best unseemly for him to be recorded as 
Describing Himself Using Capitals on All Applicable Nouns and Pronouns. 
There's a big difference between Jesus' saying "I am the way, and the truth, 
and the life" and his saying "I am the Way, the Truth and the Life".  (Out 
of curiosity, do any translations have the *reductio* of that particular 
point *ad absurdum*, which I would hold to be  "I am The Way, The Truth and 
The Life"?)

> Do you contend that "way", "truth", "life", and "me" do not fit that 
> categorization?

I contend that the capitalization adds a component of meaning to these 
nouns -- drawing an equation between Jesus and these nouns as distinct from 
describing His role -- that *cannot be demonstrated* either directly from 
the Greek original or by implication from the English of the KJV.  Had the 
translators of the KJV intended these nouns (and the pronoun) to be 
capitalized, I daresay they would have capitalized them.   I think they knew 
what they were doing.

> (FWIW - the New American Standard and the Holman Christian Standard Bible 
> capitalize the "Me"; The Message capitalizes all the words I did (although 
> "way" becomes "road");

And I would contend that each of these has added theological content by 
doing so that is not present *iu this passage* in the original Greek. 
Neither recension of the New American Bible has any of the capitals.   Nor 
does the NIV.

> and the 21st Century KJV [my personal favorite] is identical to my quote 
> (minus the missing comma).)

Interesting that they would use "cometh" in a 21st-century English 
translation.  That form of the word has been archaic for rather longer than 
my lifetime, I'd warrant.

(As a side note, the NIV (mentioned above), a very popular modern 
translation, is my *least* favorite translation.)

But more to the point, www.kj21.com/intro.htm states "The 21st Century King 
James Version of the Holy Bible (KJ21(r)) is an accurate updating of the 
King James Version (KJV) of A.D. 1611.  While easier to read and understand, 
it preserves the traditional Biblical language and sacred message of its 
historic predecessor."

Who says (besides the publisher), and on what authority?   It may be *your* 
personal favorite, but based on this example, I'm not at all convinced it 
ought to be *my* personal favorite!   Self-aggrandizement like this doesn't 
sit well.  State the translation philosophies and the underlying texts, and 
leave the choice to the reader!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> to support your position.

My "position" had nothing to do with the content of the verse - it was 
used as an example!  :(  Isn't that clear from my previous response?  We 
weren't discussing whether Jesus *was* (or is) the way, the truth, or 
the life...

> In this instance, you seemed to be quoting the KJV/AV, and in that recension 
> the nouns aren't capitalized, even though Father is.   Part of the reason, I 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and the life" and his saying "I am the Way, the Truth and the Life".

How do you say a capital letter different than a lowercase one?  :)

> (Out 
> of curiosity, do any translations have the *reductio* of that particular 
> point *ad absurdum*, which I would hold to be  "I am The Way, The Truth and 
> The Life"?)

None that I've seen.  (Probably one done by a former VB programmer would...)

>> (FWIW - the New American Standard and the Holman Christian Standard Bible 
>> capitalize the "Me"; The Message capitalizes all the words I did (although 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> does the NIV.

Maybe they simply conformed to the standard of English that was around 
at the time the translation occurred - especially if they were pursuing 
that ever-popular "readability" that some of the new translations seem 
to be going for.  (I feel that they often miss the mark...)

>> and the 21st Century KJV [my personal favorite] is identical to my quote 
>> (minus the missing comma).)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> my lifetime, I'd warrant.

I looked it up - yes, "cometh" is still there.

> (As a side note, the NIV (mentioned above), a very popular modern 
> translation, is my *least* favorite translation.)

I'm with you on that as well.  I grew up in a KJV-only church.  I bought 
my first NIV when I was attending BJU - as a ministerial major, I just 
*had* to see what was so bad about it.  :)  I didn't get to the level 
where I learned Greek, so my beef with it isn't in the translation, per 
se...  It's more just a feeling.  It's interesting to read side-by-side 
with another translation, but I've yet to preach or give a devotional 
from it.

> But more to the point, www.kj21.com/intro.htm states "The 21st Century King 
> James Version of the Holy Bible (KJ21(r)) is an accurate updating of the 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Who says (besides the publisher), and on what authority?

That is a fair question of any translation.  :)

> It may be *your* 
> personal favorite, but based on this example, I'm not at all convinced it 
> ought to be *my* personal favorite!

To each his own - I wasn't proselytizing...  :)

> Self-aggrandizement like this doesn't sit well.

How is stating my personal favorite equivalent to overstating my 
importance as a person?  You lost me with that one...

> State the translation philosophies and the underlying texts, and 
> leave the choice to the reader!

OK - all my quotes in the previous post were from KJV.
```

passage that Used Capital Letters in a Manner such as One Might Find Them in

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 18)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2006-03-23T16:54:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<44232736.EF14BA3B@mts.net>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <mda5221kiof5jm45if3gc1q6gt174r86fh@4ax.com> <uQzUf.2432$K11.1536@clgrps12> <4422DF1E.F22DEB07@mts.net> <dvup17$nm8$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> I also recall a chapter in Selby's 'Last Exit to Brooklyn', set in a
> public housing project, where some characters' words were depicted in
> mixed-case AND OTHERS WERE IN ALL CAPS, indicating, to me, shouting.
> 
> DD

Well, that's a convention and is learned behaviour.  Quite a few people
that used "green-screens" but weren't computer types were perfectly
happy with leaving the caps-lock on for their e-mail & receiving it that
way.  Some of the younger guys claimed to get headaches from upper-case
messages until I told them to acclimatize themselves or depart.  Most of
them were flexible enough to cope and the others were prickly in other
ways and I wasn't sorry to see them go.  Not applied to present company,
of course!

PL
```

LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>> There's a big difference between Jesus' saying "I am the way, and the truth,

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-23T08:44:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET...

>> In this instance, you seemed to be quoting the KJV/AV, and in that 
>> recension the nouns aren't capitalized, even though Father is.   Part of 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> How do you say a capital letter different than a lowercase one?  :)

The distinction I was making (I though explicitly) was whether or not the 
passage was *recorded as describing himself* using the inflections 
associated with capitalization or not, and absent evidence to the contrary I 
would suggest capitalizing these words wouldn't be accurate!

And I for one would contend that a competent reader in reading aloud would 
render the passage differently depending on whether it was written "I am the 
way ...", "I am the Way ...", "I am The Way", or "I am THE WAY ...".    It's 
a matter of stress and emphasis.


> I'm with you on that as well.  I grew up in a KJV-only church.  I bought 
> my first NIV when I was attending BJU - as a ministerial major, I just 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> it.

Well, I grew up with the Douay-Confraternity and the Vulgate.  I've dabbled 
in Greek, can't say I've learned it, but I've seen too many cases in which I 
believe they missed the mark in significant ways.  I can't cite examples off 
the top of my head, but I've run into several that literally raised the hair 
on the back of my neck, and was justified in that reaction after consulting 
the GNT (both TR and NA-26) and Greek lexicons.

>> Self-aggrandizement like this doesn't sit well.
>
> How is stating my personal favorite equivalent to overstating my 
> importance as a person?  You lost me with that one...

Self-aggrandizement *on the part of the authors* and/or the publishers, not 
on your part.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
Well, if there is, it's lost on me...

But then much of this Christian theology is lost on me. I've followed this 
thread with interest  and tried to understand the viewpoints expressed, but 
I have to confess I can't.

I don't see how the dissection of translations, has any bearing on the 
original thought as expressed by the speaker.

I can't see any difference betwen the use of capitals or non-use of them. (I 
imagine it was pretty immaterial in the original Aramaic... Jesus appears to 
be saying he is he only way to the Father. Capitals (added much later by a 
translator) would simply emphasise it.)

On the other hand, if we are to believe what he says, then that implies that 
anyone who is not a Christian can never know God.

I have seen fervent Chrisians endorsing this point of view both in church 
and in the street, as you might expect.

But it seems kind of "unChristian" to be smugly rejoicing because you have a 
ticket and others don't...Especially when the ticketholders are a minority. 
Bit like an exclusive club, really. (My position on that is very much 
aligned with Groucho Marx: "I wouldn't want to join any club that would have 
me as a member...")

Here are some other things I really don't understand:

1. God SO loved the world, he gave his only son to be tortured and killed as 
atonement for the sins of Mankind.

What were these sins that demanded such a sacrifice?
Well, a woman offered her man some fruit that God had said they weren't to 
touch. (It was a test; why would an omniscient God, who already knew what 
the outcome was going to be, do this? For a laugh? To help combat  the 
boredom of being eternal? Whatever, it strikes me as distinctly unGodlike 
behaviour...)
So a man (who happens to be the personification of God) gets flogged, 
humiliated, and crucified because an apple was stolen thousands of years 
before? Last time I looked, the cost of apples was not exactly so high that 
you'd kill for one...never mind torturing and humiliation.

2. And who was this terrible sacrifice made to?
Well.... God.
The same God who gave his son (who was actually himself) to the mob?
Er...Yes.

Isn't that a bit like deciding to cut your foot off with a blunt knife 
because the cat did something to offend you?
I dunno, ask a Christian.

3. And was this terrible sacrifice acceptable?
Apparently so.
For nicking an apple?
Yes.
And babies born every day are still 'tainted' by what this man and woman 
did?
Yes. Until they accept Jesus.

4. So, if I can bring myself to accept this whole fiasco, I'll be saved?
Yep.
Guaranteed?
Yep.
I don't have to do anything, except believe it?
Yep.
No personal responsibility, just hang it all on the man on the cross?
Yep. Mind you, you should try not to sin any more... but if you slip up, 
you'll be forgiven.
So, God really is kind and forgiving?
Yep. Infinitely so.
So why would he... Oh, never mind.

The above is not intended to be offensive to devout Christians and I 
apologise unreservedly if you find it so. I'm trying to honestly convey some 
of the confusion that Christianity gives rise to in me, at least.

Next week, the Buddhists... :-)
(Nope. Gautama never claimed to be God. Mind you, he never claimed not to be 
either... :-) He said he had an answer that could stop the suffering of the 
world. That was all. The rest of it has been added by the followers, who 
converted the purity of the teaching into a nice little earner, just like 
most of the great religions of the world.)

The point is that these religious debates, while they may be interesting at 
some levels, are unlikely to change anyone's mind.

(And probably just as well for all concerned...)

>> How do you say a capital letter different than a lowercase one?  :)
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And unless you were there and heard the original speaker, none of it means 
anything. The best you can hope for is to get the common gist, which seems 
to be pretty clear: "If you want to know God, listen to what I'm saying and 
try and live it."

That bit I have no trouble understanding.

>
>> I'm with you on that as well.  I grew up in a KJV-only church.  I bought 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Don't feel bad about not knowing Greek. The original was in Aramaic.

Gotta go now... there are some guys in hoods setting fire to a cross on my 
front lawn... must be Christians, I guess... It's a very violent 
religion....:-)


Pete.
```

```
On Wed, 29 Mar 2006 02:10:38 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:


>But it seems kind of "unChristian" to be smugly rejoicing because you have a 
>ticket and others don't...Especially when the ticketholders are a minority. 
>Bit like an exclusive club, really. (My position on that is very much 
>aligned with Groucho Marx: "I wouldn't want to join any club that would have 
>me as a member...")

While heaven doesn't appear to be very attractive - the concept of
smugly watching our loved ones - and most everybody else - being
tortured forever and ever beyond all understanding without hope of
parole makes you pray there ain't no heaven.

>Here are some other things I really don't understand:
>
>1. God SO loved the world, he gave his only son to be tortured and killed as 
>atonement for the sins of Mankind.

What is the mechanism for this?    If God is omnipotent, He could
create a Rube Goldberg type mechanism that required Him to give His
son to be sacrificed.   Or God could have avoided that altogether.  

I am not full enough of hate to be a Christian.
```

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:7fii2250hevicfpmujcqgrtsn453frdega@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 29 Mar 2006 02:10:38 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

".... pray there ain't no heaven."  I loved it, Howard :-) You've really got 
the hang of this religion thing; everyone else is praying that there IS a 
heaven...and they'll be in it, of course...:-)

It's a bit like praying for Faith; if you haven't got it, there's hardly any 
point in asking a deity who you don't believe in to provide it. You might as 
well pray for a jack to fix your flat tyre when you know perfectly well you 
left it home in the garage...Or incant a spell to summon Hecate (or the 
Tooth Fairy) and hope she brings a jack...


>>Here are some other things I really don't understand:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think the bit that gets me here is this whole 'sin' thing. How is doing 
what comes naturally sinful? Given that if you want to live in the society 
of other people, you need to take care that what you do doesn't hurt them in 
any way, then surely there can be no harm in whatever you do?  I don't need 
commandments from some Super Power to tell me that murder, stealing, 
adultery, and covetousness are bad for society (which includes me). I can 
figure that one out for myself. Respect for elders (and folks in general) 
and making sure I don't flog everyone to death working for me, again are 
simple facts, if you want to have a better life. I don't see any harm in God 
giving Moses these ideas (and the language is beautiful - read it in German; 
it sends shivers up your spine :-)), but why  then decide that Jesus had ot 
die because people were bad?    For God to make such an appalling sacrifice 
because He was offended by His creatures doing what He presumably designed 
them to, doesn't show much compassion or intelligence. I simply can't get my 
head around this.

I'm sorry, the 'Rube Goldberg' reference escapes me (culture gap again, I 
guess). Could you elucidate? Hang on... I'll check the Web... Ah, it is 
clear now... to non-American English-as-first-language people, it translates 
as "Heath Robinson".

> I am not full enough of hate to be a Christian.

Ouch!... Some of them are decent enough folks (as are some Muslims, 
Zoroastrians, Scientologists, Buddhists, Hindus,  Hare Krishnas, 
Theosophists, Rosicrucians, Satanists, and even atheists. <gasp!>) 
Christianity doesn't have a monopoly on hate. It is interesting to me that 
you often find hate and stupidity living side by side. And nobody has a 
monopoly on stupidity...:-)

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0hn8c$719$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <7fii2250hevicfpmujcqgrtsn453frdega@4ax.com> <491lcaFmgdfgU1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:491lcaFmgdfgU1@individual.net...

> everyone else is praying that there IS a heaven...and they'll be in it, of 
> course...:-)

Maybe my position is unusual on this, but as a Christian I've never been 
comfortable with the idea of aspiring to Heaven, much less presuming that 
reward.  It always struck me as a fundamentally egocentric (and not 
particularly mature) motivation for one's behavior.

For a metaphor of obedience, take the chore of washing the dishes after 
supper.  As a child in the family, what's the motivation?  To avoid getting 
punished?  To get an extra helping of dessert or an extra hour of 
television?  Or to lessen the workload on somebody else in the family?   I'm 
more comfortable with the last of these, although I am convinced that there 
ain't no such thing as an absolutely pure motive ...

I'll either make it or I won't.  I really don't worry about it much; I worry 
about doing the best I can do on a day-by-day basis to align myself with 
what I believe God's will is for me because I think it makes me a more 
productive and useful human being.  I certainly don't pray about Heaven (one 
way or the other).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

In article <e0hn8c$719$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

[snip]

>For a metaphor of obedience, take the chore of washing the dishes after 
>supper.  As a child in the family, what's the motivation?  To avoid getting 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>ain't no such thing as an absolutely pure motive ...

My memory is, admittedly, porous... but I recall reading, decades on back, 
a version of Sun T'zu's 'The Art of War' where somewhere - maybe in the 
text, maybe in the preface/footnotes/postscript - it was related how one 
should appeal to different 'types' of people in different ways... the 
lazy, so as they might avoid punishment, the greedy, so that they might 
find gain, the scholarly, so that they might show their worth to 
themselves and their colleagues, the desiring-of-power, so that they might 
gain the same... beyond the 'animal' basics that which motivates a collie 
might not be that which motivates a poodle might not be that which 
motivates a boxer might not be that which motivates a St Bernard; might 
one be generous enough to allow such to humans, as well?

DD
```

```
I had an interview technique assessment last Thursday and was advised
not to come across as argumentative, so here goes....

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> But it seems kind of "unChristian" to be smugly rejoicing because you have a
> ticket and others don't...Especially when the ticketholders are a minority.

Only 2.4 billion Bible bashers on this planet. (source: news.bbc.co.uk)

> Bit like an exclusive club, really. (My position on that is very much
> aligned with Groucho Marx: "I wouldn't want to join any club that would have
> me as a member...")

I wouldn't want to be a member of any club which
would have you as a member either  ;-)

>
> Here are some other things I really don't understand:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> boredom of being eternal?

Whoa! God had only been around approx one week. How come he gets so
bored so easy?

> Whatever, it strikes me as distinctly unGodlike
> behaviour...)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> you'd kill for one...never mind torturing and humiliation.

But us Brits used to export (sorry:) transport people for lesser
crimes.

> The above is not intended to be offensive to devout Christians and I
> apologise unreservedly if you find it so. I'm trying to honestly convey some
> of the confusion that Christianity gives rise to in me, at least.
>
> Next week, the Buddhists... :-)

I'll look forward to that one. Will you start off with the four
miniature flying elephants and the golden net?


> > And I for one would contend that a competent reader in reading aloud would
> > render the passage differently depending on whether it was written "I am
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> try and live it."

Language is so imprecise. Stick to Mathematics.


> Gotta go now... there are some guys in hoods setting fire to a cross on my
> front lawn... must be Christians, I guess... It's a very violent
> religion....:-)

Don't Muslims burn crosses too? Or is it just flags?
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:1143565437.850125.62550@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com...
>I had an interview technique assessment last Thursday and was advised
> not to come across as argumentative, so here goes....
>

Reading your response, I see you've taken this advice on board, Alistair... 
Well Done!...;-)

> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> But it seems kind of "unChristian" to be smugly rejoicing because you 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Wow! I thought the club was limited to 200,000...(I believe it's in the book 
of Revelation, but at the time it was written, 200,000 probably seemed like 
a huge number that would easily encompass everybody they wanted...)

>> Bit like an exclusive club, really. (My position on that is very much
>> aligned with Groucho Marx: "I wouldn't want to join any club that would 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> would have you as a member either  ;-)

ROFL! Great response... :-)
>
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Er...I believe the story is that God had been around for an infinitely long 
time. (It was the Earth that had only been around for a short time....) 
However, He probably got fed up with being a Word (especially when there 
wasn't anybody to say it), so it became necessary to build a whole 
infrastructure that would eventually produce people who had language, and 
could say the Word. (Except that they didn't know it, and anyone who 
attained an inkling of what it might be, would be immediately killed if they 
uttered it. And that raises another question: If the name of God is unknown 
but the penalty for uttering it is death, how would anyone know that what 
you said was the name of God? )

To this day, many religions hold the name of God to be so Holy it cannot be 
said or written. (Yeah, I know... how do they pass it on? Well, the priests 
leave clues for other faithful who come after them. And they make up a name 
to substitute for the real one.  Like "Yahweh" (Jehovah)  which simply 
stands for "Yet Another Way to Enter Heaven" ...there truly are lots of 
ways...I knew a girl once (but I digress...)).

I happen to know His real name is Fred. (You read it here first...) When I 
first encountered this sacred secret I asked if it was "the Fred you sew 
buttons on wif..." Apparently, He laughed so much there were several major 
volcanic eruptions and a tsunami...

>> Whatever, it strikes me as distinctly unGodlike
>> behaviour...)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, I guess there's always been something a bit Godlike about the Brits... 
;-)

It's inventing a navy that does it.

Once a bloke has survived storm at sea and getting pissed in half the 
world's ports, not to mention lobbing great hunks of red hot metal across 
miles to destroy other ships which are lobbing metal at you, the problems of 
everyday life probably pale into insignificance.... It is a spiritual thing.

>> The above is not intended to be offensive to devout Christians and I
>> apologise unreservedly if you find it so. I'm trying to honestly convey 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No. And we won't be discussing Garuda (the airline or the bird) either. All 
of this embroidery is simply not part of the fundamental message. It's a bit 
like Christianity and the Jehovah's Witnesses. Or the Mormons. Jesus said 
nothing about gold plates or Urim and Thrummin, but it has been a right 
little earner, so it goes on. (Now I've gone and offended the Mormons and 
the JWs... didn't mean to, sorry.) None of the other Christian sects are 
much better anyway. Ooops! that's everybody...better quit before the wrath 
of Fred is unleashed on me collectively, by all the faithful...

>
>> > And I for one would contend that a competent reader in reading aloud 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Language is so imprecise. Stick to Mathematics.

Blessed is the x, for it shall be unknown.
Blessed is the y, for with it ye shall find the x.
Blessed are the numbers (except zero, which is the work of the Devil and 
reduces everything to nothing...), for with them we shall count the profits 
every Sunday (or Saturday, depending on your persuasion...)

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Don't Muslims burn crosses too? Or is it just flags?

I understand the accepted practice is to have a hissy fit whenever someone 
disagrees, or even asks a question, or looks like they might be thinking of 
possibly asking a question, or suggesting something that might in any way 
reflect less than favourably on the political  or religious aims of the 
group.

Anything can be burned, or blown up, or have an aircraft crashed into it, as 
long as it is not a mosque.
(I believe this was the message in London, from someone who spent most of 
his life in a mosque... funny, that...).
Independent thought is not encouraged. (But then, it isn't in ANY 
religion...)

It is pretty obvious; the answer for the US is to stop building skyscrapers 
that are patently NOT mosques, and camouflage everything else to look like a 
mosque.  See, it wouldn't be that hard... you could easily stick a few 
minarets around the Hollywod Bowl, and the White House. The Statue of 
Liberty could have a veil on it, and an image of Bin Laden could be added to 
Mount Rushmore. MacDonalds ALREADY looks like a mosque... It's all about 
compromise...

Of course, only us atheists understand the REAL truth... :-)
"... which you can't reveal
 to the innocent youth,
except to say it isn't worth a dime."
      (Leonard Cohen - "Closing Time")

Pete.
```
