- **References:** `<1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET> <Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90> <59f88$4446e1be$45491d12$9184@KNOLOGY.NET> <EEM1g.769$Fd6.514@edtnps82> <rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <XIN1g.782$Fd6.205@edtnps82> <loaf421q1futfmv5k4nch8g5ok1ivamvl9@4ax.com> <CgQ1g.804$Fd6.730@edtnps82>`

```

"Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com> wrote in message 
news:CgQ1g.804$Fd6.730@edtnps82...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
> news:loaf421q1futfmv5k4nch8g5ok1ivamvl9@4ax.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is a zero sum game because the entire Universe is a zero sum game. It 
came from nothing. Von Neuman formulated games theory and the concept of 
zero sum to demonstrate a specific type of game.

I am prepared to show that any example you can show of it NOT being a zero 
sum game can be refuted.

The new products require resources that are diminished whether the old 
products are destroyed or recycled. The amount of that diminution is exactly 
what it takes to make the new products.


>    Right, but actually building the product is a service. When I buy a 
> stereo, I'm paying for the materials that the stereo is made out of, plus 
> the service of assembling those materials into a working stereo.
>
Services 'add value' to the overall. But as you rightly observed they 
consume time and energy.

>    If shopping were effortless, and I resold that stereo at a higher price 
> without making any changes to the stereo (i.e., not providing a service), 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> import stereos from Japan more easily than other people could).

Sure, so your gain, is their loss...
>
>    So the only question now is wether these services are adding worth to 
> the world, thus making money a non-zero sum game. I think it might still 
> be a zero-sum game.

I'm pretty sure it is... :-)


>When I'm paying for service, I'm essentially paying for time. That is, I 
>don't have to the time to perform a certain task, so I'm paying someone 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> that say about money?

What if, while you were 'wasting' time (you can't REALLY waste it by the 
way) someone stepped in and did something that you would otherwise have 
done? They have benefitted by your waste of time. It isn't a good example 
because the universal time line ticks down whether you use the time or waste 
it.

Money simply represents time and energy.

>
>    As a programmer, I'm pretty much purely providing a service. That is, 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No. If you weren't programming you'd be doing something else and someone 
else would do the programming or your employers would use their time and 
energy in a different fashion. Nothing is created or destroyed. It is simply 
transformed.

>    Maybe, maybe not. These children need resources (e.g. food) to survive, 
> so I had to spend resources (e.g. money) to provide get the resources they 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Probably... :-)

Take a logical short cut. The Universe came from nothing. All of the matter, 
energy, space, and time in it came into being at that moment. It doesn't get 
created or destroyed.  In other words, it is finite (although it is 
boundless). Therefore the sum of the 'losses' must exactly equal the sum of 
the 'gains' because the underlying 'currency' is inviolate.

Pete.
>    - Oliver
```

```
On Sat, 22 Apr 2006 13:59:11 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>>> When more new products are built than old products thrown out, it is
>>> not a zero sum game.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>zero sum to demonstrate a specific type of game.

The entire Universe could very well be a zero sum game, although that
hasn't been tested out over the life of the universe.   

But our money system isn't concerned with the entire universe.    In
our subset of the universe, we create added value to such an extent
that we have more stuff.

There are scenarios where we have less stuff as well.   The Universe
might not notice, but we would.
```

```
If I change your perception to believe that something is more useful than 
you thought it was, I have added value to it. (At least, as far a syou're

Zero Sum.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-25T09:17:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4atgvfFv499qU1@individual.net> <kukp425qg92om210b89faanig3fo88un4q@4ax.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net>`

```
In article <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
>news:kukp425qg92om210b89faanig3fo88un4q@4ax.com...

[snip]

>> The entire Universe could very well be a zero sum game, although that
>> hasn't been tested out over the life of the universe.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Added value is an illusion.

Perhaps, Mr Dashwood, no more and no less than any other kind of 'value' 
might be.

>
>If I change your perception to believe that something is more useful than 
>you thought it was, I have added value to it. (At least, as far a syou're 
>concerned :-))
>

'Useful' may be as much of a matter of interpretation as 'value', Mr 
Dashwood... and it might be possible to add a new interpretation.

>If I actually add functionality to something, I consume resurces in order to 
>do it.
>
>Zero Sum.

Eh?  Leaving aside how 'functionality' may be, as well, a matter of 
interpretation... how does adding it consume as much resources as the 
benefit gained, rendering it zero sum?

Consider the use of a stick as a crutch in order to keep weight off a 
twisted ankle so that a hunter might heal and be able to supply the tribe 
with food.  Being able to see a stick in this manner adds value to it and 
makes it useful to both the individual and the society; are you saying 
that the first person who saw that this could be done consumed enough 
resources to use up any gain this added function has ever given the 
species?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-25T12:11:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1145992260.580774.193280@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4atgvfFv499qU1@individual.net> <kukp425qg92om210b89faanig3fo88un4q@4ax.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
> Consider the use of a stick as a crutch in order to keep weight off a
> twisted ankle so that a hunter might heal and be able to supply the tribe
> with food.

Which may also be done without the stick, by sitting on a rock, or the
ground.

> Being able to see a stick in this manner adds value to it

It doesn't add value to the stick. It's still just a stick.  Putting
padding on the end and a rubber tip on it may add value, using it does
not add value.

> makes it useful to both the individual and the society; are you saying
> that the first person who saw that this could be done consumed enough
> resources to use up any gain this added function has ever given the
> species?

It isn't the stick that gave the added function, it is just a stick, it
is the brain that used the resource in a novel way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-26T09:47:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2nfka$qlh$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com> <1145992260.580774.193280@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1145992260.580774.193280@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>> Consider the use of a stick as a crutch in order to keep weight off a
>> twisted ankle so that a hunter might heal and be able to supply the tribe
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>ground.

That there may be other ways to accomplish the end, Mr Plinston, does not 
rule out that another, different method has been found.

>
>> Being able to see a stick in this manner adds value to it
>
>It doesn't add value to the stick. It's still just a stick.

Are you arguing for a sort of inherent/absolute value, Mr Plinston?  I'm 
sorry I did not make it clear, 'value' as I am using it is a matter of 
interpretation; metallic silver, for example, when it is just sort of 
sitting around, has no value except for that which humans give to it.  In 
that the stick can now be seen as having another set of attributes - in 
addition, say, to being potential kindling it is now a potential way for 
an injured hunter to supply himself and the group with food - it now has 
more value.

>Putting
>padding on the end and a rubber tip on it may add value, using it does
>not add value.

Ahhhhh... 'using it does not add value'.  We seem to have *very* different 
assumptions when it comes to such; a conclusion drawn from your assertion 
might be that using books has no value.

>
>> makes it useful to both the individual and the society; are you saying
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>is the brain that used the resource in a novel way.

In this instance, Mr Plinston, the brain can do all it wants... but until 
someone has a stick of the right attributes (diameter, length, etc.) then 
the injured hunter sits at home and the potential for additional protein 
goes wasted.

DD
```

```
have my worth from that book even though I no longer have the book.
```

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com> <1145992260.580774.193280@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <e2nfka$qlh$1@reader1.panix.com> <1146076789.624705.143360@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com> <vuhv421b07api1vrqvhk5b573bglbqnrkb@4ax.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:

> A used CoBOL manual is much more valuable than one that just takes up
> space.

It may be its value that is determining its usage, not the other way
around.

> A book that I paid $20 for, and read, and then sold for $10 has given
> me my $20 value, and then gave somebody else a $10 value.   I still
> have my worth from that book even though I no longer have the book.

That doesn't make the book more valuable. You got your $10 value from
reading it and that reduced its value down to the $10 you sold it for.

In the meantime the forest that supplied the paper and the stream that
took the pulp mill's waste and the oil reserves that supplied the
energy and the air that took the exhaust all were reduced in value by
some small amount.

Sure, it looks like a gain when you only use one eye to look at it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Value

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-26T21:46:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XSR3g.3638$fH.2493@edtnps82>`
- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com> <1145992260.580774.193280@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <e2nfka$qlh$1@reader1.panix.com> <1146076789.624705.143360@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com> <vuhv421b07api1vrqvhk5b573bglbqnrkb@4ax.com> <1146083687.350733.199570@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <lomv429pb8r9l2bvou0k2gtru2cg6tm5v1@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:lomv429pb8r9l2bvou0k2gtru2cg6tm5v1@4ax.com...
>
>>> A book that I paid $20 for, and read, and then sold for $10 has given
>>> me my $20 value, and then gave somebody else a $10 value.   I still
>>> have my worth from that book even though I no longer have the book.
>>
[...]
>
> I got my value from it when I read it.   When I sold it, someone else
> got his value without cutting down any forests.   And I still have the
> value of having read it.

    When you bought the book, you were paying for materials (paper and ink) 
and services (the act of arranging the paper and ink into something which 
can convey useful information by the writer, the store of keeping the copy 
pristine, shiny and new, and delivering it from the writer to a location 
nearer to you so that you could gain access to it).

    As you read the book, you spend the resource of time, and exchange gain 
knowledge.

    When you sell the book, you're selling materials (paper and ink) and the 
service of delivering the book to the next person from the bookstore, who in 
turn got it from the author (with perhaps other middlemen along the way). 
The person pays you slightly less because you didn't keep it in pristine 
state like the store did, so some of the pages may be a bit wrinkled or 
folded.

    Now they have the book too, but if they actually want knowledge, and not 
just a thick cup holder, they'll have to spend the resource of time to read 
it as well.

>
> Are you saying that a recycled book has less value than a new book
> because it didn't take as much of the sun's energy in creating the
> paper a second time?

    I think it actually takes more energy to recycle a book than to cut down 
new trees and make books from them. That's why recycle paper is more 
expensive than non-recycled paper (unless the government subsidizes some of 
the cost of recycling paper).

>
> How about finding an already-written object in your object library and
> using it in your new program.    Since it didn't cost much new, it
> must not be worth much - right?

    With digital information, there's almost no materials involved, so what 
you're exchanging is always services. You spent some time writing the 
object, and now you're using that object that you wrote. If you share it on 
the Internet, other people can use that object too. The "value" of that 
object can be said to be equal to the amount of time it took you to create 
it. However, as I've mentioned elsewhere, it seems like we can "generate" 
time (or man-hours) out of nothing by simply having lots of children; of 
course, the more children you have, the more resources they consume to stay 
alive, and the more effort (i.e. services) you have to expend in raising 
them, which is why I mentioned earlier that I'm not sure we have a zero-sum 
game once services enters into the pictures. The equations simply get too 
complex for me at that point.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Value

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-26T15:22:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1146090123.074374.199430@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<lomv429pb8r9l2bvou0k2gtru2cg6tm5v1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com> <1145992260.580774.193280@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <e2nfka$qlh$1@reader1.panix.com> <1146076789.624705.143360@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com> <vuhv421b07api1vrqvhk5b573bglbqnrkb@4ax.com> <1146083687.350733.199570@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <lomv429pb8r9l2bvou0k2gtru2cg6tm5v1@4ax.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 26 Apr 2006 13:34:47 -0700, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> Are you saying that a recycled book has less value than a new book
> because it didn't take as much of the sun's energy in creating the
> paper a second time?

Eventually most books will return to dust or will be eaten by mites
reducing their value to zero.  Or they will be damaged by water, oils,
wear and tear or dirt. Their value reduces as these effects take their
toll.  Over time the content of most books become obsolete or
unfashionable.

No matter how much value is added by turning trees into books
eventually that value will be lost.  While a very few books will gain
individual value this is because of rarity and the value of a single
remaining copy of a book is usually much less than the value of the
total production run when new.

> How about finding an already-written object in your object library and
> using it in your new program.    Since it didn't cost much new, it
> must not be worth much - right?
>
> Or maybe it's worth more than it was before now that is used more.

You're really struggling with this.
```

```
Richard wrote:
> Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I thnk it short-sighted to deny knowledge a "value". I also disagree
that the world is zero sum. Perhaps the universe is, but even that is
debatable. The only way that universe can be is a zero sum is if you
deny the possibility of creation. That means both a eternal universe(has
always existed, always will exist), and an existing universe in which
nothing can be created. The evidence does not bear witness to that.

Post-moderism has simply gone beyond to existentionalism("I think
therefore I am")to the post-existentalism("I defined it, therefore it
exists".). *Howard* increases in value, as a result of the book
existing. The book creates effect, and anything that can create effect
is "real" by definition. We know how it happens, and watch it happen
every day. How do you balance the creation of knowledge? Or the ability
to communicate that knowledge to future generations?.

Donald
```

```
In article <1146076789.624705.143360@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Using the stick does not add value _to_the_stick_.

Repetition does not make for greater validity of argument, Mr Plinston.  
In that the stick, when used as a crutch for people who can then garner 
more food for themselves and their tribe, has an increased utility or 
importance than does a stick which has only the attributes of kindling, 
then it has by definition more value (http://www.m-w.com/value, 3)

>
>> Ahhhhh... 'using it does not add value'.  We seem to have *very* different
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Using a book does not add value _to_the_book_.

In that a book can serve to transmit entertainment, information or 
education by its use (ie, by its being read) then it has greater utility 
or importance than does a book which can only be used as tinder; given 
that 'relative worth, utility or importance' is a definition of value (see 
citing above) then the conclusion applies.

>In fact it detracts
>value as can easily be seen by the fact that used books sold in garage
>sales, or even on amazon, fetch much reduced prices compared to new
>unused books.

Mr Plinston, you seem to be equating price and value here; it might be 
that we are using different definitions.  I've cited the one that I feel 
is applicable to the situation and its source; might you be so kind as to 
cite the one you are using and where you found it?

DD
```

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

It may well be that a particular size of stick that is strong enough
may be more valuable than one that is just a twig only useful for

it strong enough.

It may well be also that a person may be more valuable when mobile then
when crippled.

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

But reading a book does not make blank pages into entertainment. The
_printing_ of a book adds value to the paper, the reading of it makes
no difference to the book, except wear and tear.

> Mr Plinston, you seem to be equating price and value here; it might be
> that we are using different definitions.  I've cited the one that I feel
> is applicable to the situation and its source; might you be so kind as to
> cite the one you are using and where you found it?

You should take that up with Howard who was the one who put numbers of
dollars to his book.
```

```
>

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

My apologies, Mr Plinston; my attempt was to demonstrate that this 
particular argument was based on a definition from a commonly-accepted 
source.

>
>It may well be that a particular size of stick that is strong enough
>may be more valuable than one that is just a twig only useful for
>kindling, but sticking a piece of kindling under your arm does not make
>it strong enough.

No stick will be used as a crutch, Mr Plinston, until someone thinks of 
doing so... unless you wish to posit some kind of universe where sticks 
leap into this position of their own volition.  Are you saying that the 
value of stick-as-crutch exists in the stick before anyone knows it can be 
used as such?

>
>It may well be also that a person may be more valuable when mobile then
>when crippled.

Already postulated, Mr Plinston.

>
>> >Using a book does not add value _to_the_book_.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>But reading a book does not make blank pages into entertainment.

Not blank, Mr Plinston, printed.  Reading a book makes the configurations 
of white and not-white spaces into something interpreted; if the book 
cannot be read - for whatever reason, whether because of ignorance or 
degraded page-quality - then its value, by the definition given above, is 
less.

>The
>_printing_ of a book adds value to the paper, the reading of it makes
>no difference to the book, except wear and tear.

I do not know what makes a difference to a book, Mr Plinston; the question 
is one of 'value', which is, I believe, a condition generated by human 
beings.

>
>> Mr Plinston, you seem to be equating price and value here; it might be
>> that we are using different definitions.  I've cited the one that I feel
>> is applicable to the situation and its source; might you be so kind as to
>> cite the one you are using and where you found it?


>You should take that up with Howard who was the one who put numbers of
>dollars to his book.
>

from 
<http://groups.google.com/group/comp.lang.cobol/msg/e3089017a9e87658?dmode=source>

--begin quoted text

Using a book does not add value _to_the_book_. In fact it *detracts value* 
as can easily be seen by the fact that used books sold in garage sales, or 
even on amazon, *fetch much reduced prices* compared to new unused books.

--end quoted text

(emphases added)

Your words, Mr Plinston, and they appear to equate price and value.  
Second request, then: you seem to be equating price and value here; it 
might be that we are using different definitions.  I've cited the one that 
I feel is applicable to the situation and its source; might you be so kind 
as to cite the one you are using and where you found it?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-27T14:29:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Az44g.811$nq3.409@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <1146076789.624705.143360@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com> <e2p19g$1tq$1@reader1.panix.com> <1146099700.397496.132270@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <e2pu48$9so$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e2pu48$9so$1@reader1.panix.com...
>
> Are you saying that the
> value of stick-as-crutch exists in the stick before anyone knows it can be
> used as such?
[...]
> you seem to be equating price and value here; it
> might be that we are using different definitions.  I've cited the one that
> I feel is applicable to the situation and its source; might you be so kind
> as to cite the one you are using and where you found it?

    When I first brought up all this zero-sum stuff, it was in the context 
of economics. Specifically, I had said something along the lines of "Every 
time I earn $1, someone out there must have lost $1." and the statement was 
made to argue that the concept of inheritance will hurt your descendants in 
the long run. While it's interesting to think about, I didn't mean for my 
statement to imply anything about conservation of matter in the universe, or 
the laws of thermodynamics and entropy.

    I had initially equated the value of something with its price for 
simplicity. More specifically, I was under the belief that the agreed upon 
definition of "value" in the context of economics is "whatever the market is 
willing to pay." [*] So the value of a book is whatever price (primarily in 
dollars) at which people are willing to pay to buy it. I guess for an object 
in a code library, before it exists, there's some platonic ideal of it in 
your mind, and you decide you want it, even though it doesn't exist yet. So 
its value (although it doesn't exist) is however much effort you were 
willing to put to write it. Once it exists, and you distribute it for "free" 
on the Internet, its value is whatever amount of effort others are willing 
to put into reading your documentation and learning how to use it.

    So under this definition, the price (and thus the value) of things can 
fluctuate over time. But usually, as economists, we're not interested in the 
"absolute value" of things, but rather the relative value. Let's say I have 
2 chickens, and you have 2 ducks, and "the market" has determined that the 
value of a duck is $20, and the value of a chicken is $20. We want to trade 
with each other, but we don't actually have any currency, so I give you one 
of my chickens, and in exchange, you give me one of your ducks.

    Suddenly, hyperinflation occurs, and ducks are now worth $200, and 
chickens are now worth $200. Are we now $360 richer? Perhaps, but I can 
still only get 1 duck for my each of my chickens and vice versa.

    I'm suppose that, by definition, if we're only interested in relative 
value, then the total value MUST be zero-sum (or at the very least 
constant). That is, no matter what everyone owns, we would always calculate 
relative value by taking the aggregation of all things owned, and define 
their summed value to be 1 (or some other constant value), and then define 
each individual item to have some value relative to that.

    It's sufficient, I think, for "the game" to be constant-value, as 
opposed to zero-sum, for the "inheritance hurts descendants" argument to 
work. But just for fun, I think I can still work in the possibility for our 
world to be economically zero-sum. All that means is that the constant 
mentioned above would have to be 0 (i.e. the sum of the value of all things 
is zero). The only way this would be possible is if nothing existed 
(empirical evidence shows this not to be the case), or if it were possible 
for things to have negative value.

    Is it possible for someone to have something that they would be willing 
to pay to get rid of? A few things come to mind: trash, debt, crime, 
disease. Imagine for example, that a company builds a factory that makes 
lots of money, but as a side effect causes polution and sickness in the 
people living around it. In a sense, the factory is taking the people's 
cleanliness and health, and turning it into cash.

    The people later have to pay money to have doctors and environmentalist 
to buy back their cleanliness and health.

[*]: It turns out economists do not agree on a definition of "value". See 
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value_%28economics%29

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 66)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-27T20:35:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1146195311.092891.248390@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e2pu48$9so$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <1146076789.624705.143360@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com> <e2p19g$1tq$1@reader1.panix.com> <1146099700.397496.132270@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <e2pu48$9so$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> Are you saying that the
> value of stick-as-crutch exists in the stick before anyone knows it can be
> used as such?

Not at all. I have been saying all along that using a stick as a
crutch, or to beat people with, or to prop up the clothes line does not
make the stick more valuable. It is just a stick and if it gets thrown
on the fire you can go and get another. Now, when you _can't_ get
another then that would make it be more valuable.

> Not blank, Mr Plinston, printed.  Reading a book makes the configurations
> of white and not-white spaces into something interpreted;

It actually changes patterns in the brain and this may make the _brain_
more valuable to the reader. The book, once read, may become less
valuable to the reader because it loses some of the ability to make
further improvements to the brain.

> if the book
> cannot be read - for whatever reason, whether because of ignorance or
> degraded page-quality - then its value, by the definition given above, is
> less.

The value does not change. It is could not be read then its value (to
that person) as reading material was zero before it was opened and zero
after it was closed.

> I do not know what makes a difference to a book, Mr Plinston; the question
> is one of 'value', which is, I believe, a condition generated by human
> beings.

And each may generate a different value for the same object or a copy
thereof.

> Your words, Mr Plinston, and they appear to equate price and value.

Price and value may have no connection but where two items are for sale
in the same context (such as both being available on Amazon) then the
relative monetary value is an indicator of _someones_ idea of the
relative value.
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dashwood... and it might be possible to add a new interpretation.

I don't doubt it.

>
>>If I actually add functionality to something, I consume resurces in order 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> benefit gained, rendering it zero sum?

The same way as vaporising steel leaves exactly as many iron atoms as you 
started with, just in a different ocncentration.
>
> Consider the use of a stick as a crutch in order to keep weight off a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No. I am saying that the visibility or otherwise of the stick adds no 
intrinsic value to it. Value is added by the Hunter, who processed light 
waves form the stick in order to do so, thus burning calories and adding to 
the heat entropy of the Universe. With his added value stick he is then able 
to hunt and supply more energy to his brain so he can think and add more 
value to his perception of other things and so the calories are consumed and 
the entropy rises and ....

Zero sum.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-27T12:31:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2qdi6$qb9$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com> <4bblsdF10g5caU1@individual.net>`

```
In article <4bblsdF10g5caU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e2kpfq$irg$1@reader1.panix.com...
>> In article <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net>,
>> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>> Consider the use of a stick as a crutch in order to keep weight off a
>> twisted ankle so that a hunter might heal and be able to supply the tribe
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>intrinsic value to it.

Ummmmm... there seems to be a shift here from 'value' to 'intrinsic 
value'.  I am using 'value' according to http://www.m-w.com/value, 3: 
relative worth, utility, or importance.  In that it is, by definition, 
relative, then intrinsic - http://www.m-w.com/intrinsic, 1 - would not 
appear to be applicable in that it requires an isolation of an object from 
its surroundings.

(whether such an isolation is possible or is, intrinsically, merely the 
shifting from one environment to another might be a topic for a doctoral 
dissertation)

>Value is added by the Hunter, who processed light 
>waves form the stick in order to do so, thus burning calories and adding to 
>the heat entropy of the Universe.

I disagree; increased value - in this case utility - is added by the 
person who invents (devises by thinking) the use of a stick as a crutch; 
this person may or may not be the hunter.

>With his added value stick he is then able 
>to hunt and supply more energy to his brain so he can think and add more 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Zero sum.

Ummmmmm... leaving aside this shift back again, from 'intrinsic value' to 
'value to his perception of other things'... it might be that the hunter's 
brain's is not the only one changed, as others of the group eat what he is 
able to bring in.

DD
```

- **References:** `<jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET> <Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90> <59f88$4446e1be$45491d12$9184@KNOLOGY.NET> <EEM1g.769$Fd6.514@edtnps82> <rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <XIN1g.782$Fd6.205@edtnps82> <loaf421q1futfmv5k4nch8g5ok1ivamvl9@4ax.com> <CgQ1g.804$Fd6.730@edtnps82> <4atgvfFv499qU1@individual.net> <kukp425qg92om210b89faanig3fo88un4q@4ax.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <v5cs4251us8rknuj4ano1degclnke2rvsp@4ax.com>`

```
> If food isn't more valuable to you than seeds,

To turn seeds into food requires land, which may mean, say, cutting
down rain forest and this may (and does) lead to desertification. Or it
may require fertilizer and sprays and so on which uses resources such
as oil.

So adding value to seed may result in less value elsewhere, or at
another time.

For example when all the Brazilian rain forest is turned to desert then
the whole country will be valueless.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-25T14:33:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1146000789.305185.254760@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<v03t421fje3b46n54othp40cmellpcjfce@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <XIN1g.782$Fd6.205@edtnps82> <loaf421q1futfmv5k4nch8g5ok1ivamvl9@4ax.com> <CgQ1g.804$Fd6.730@edtnps82> <4atgvfFv499qU1@individual.net> <kukp425qg92om210b89faanig3fo88un4q@4ax.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <v5cs4251us8rknuj4ano1degclnke2rvsp@4ax.com> <1145992605.782857.223800@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <1qus42tf55oss14vf4tj52ud35eh8k5l2l@4ax.com> <1145998147.228460.25570@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <v03t421fje3b46n54othp40cmellpcjfce@4ax.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> My bad.   I still don't see it as a zero sum for the system that uses
> money (our society).   The amount of stuff we have is different than
> before as we grow new stuff, mine more stuff, and create more services
> and art.

That is probably because, like capitalists of old, you consider that
all resources are infinite (for example the mine will never run out)
and that waste products are some one else's problem (who cares that
slagheaps go to school in Wales), and that other countries and the poor
are merely there for the exploitation by capitalists and imperialists.

In other words: as long as _you_ get 'more stuff' you neither see nor
care about those that wind up with 'less stuff'.

A capitalist argument is: if everyone mows their neibours lawn instead
of their own and charges and pays $30 for this service then the economy
will be $3 billion better. They will then try to charge a small fee for
coordinating this to make themselves a few $million better.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **References:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <XIN1g.782$Fd6.205@edtnps82> <loaf421q1futfmv5k4nch8g5ok1ivamvl9@4ax.com> <CgQ1g.804$Fd6.730@edtnps82> <4atgvfFv499qU1@individual.net> <kukp425qg92om210b89faanig3fo88un4q@4ax.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <v5cs4251us8rknuj4ano1degclnke2rvsp@4ax.com> <1145992605.782857.223800@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <1qus42tf55oss14vf4tj52ud35eh8k5l2l@4ax.com> <1145998147.228460.25570@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <v03t421fje3b46n54othp40cmellpcjfce@4ax.com> <1146000789.305185.254760@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <260420061808390686%usrr@post.no.mail> <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net> <270420062003576669%usrr@post.no.mail>`

```

Joseph Katnic wrote:
> >
> I did say "Practical". Presumably, practical means as far as we can
> imagine it to be.

No. 'practical' means 'in practice' not 'in imagination'.

'Practically' means that which can be practiced, or actually done,
excluding those parts that can only be imagined or are not achievable.
The usage has, however, degraded to merely meaning 'almost' or
'nearly'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 67)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-27T12:38:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <1146000789.305185.254760@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <260420061808390686%usrr@post.no.mail> <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net>`

```
In article <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>"Joseph Katnic" <usrr@post.no.mail> wrote in message 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>not infinite.

Gah... Mr Dashwood, language is starting to break down here.  'Boundless' 
is, I believe, 'without a boundary'; http://www.m-w.com/boundary shows 
'something (as a line, point, or plane) that indicates or fixes a limit or 
extent'.

Finite, according to http://www.m-w.com/finite, 1, is 'having definite or 
definable limits'... and the 'in-' prefix used shows negation or absence, 
'not infinite' = finite.

Given those definitions your statement can be rendered 'the Universe does 
not have something (as a line, point, or plane) that indicates or fixes a 
limit but it has definite or definable limits'.

How does something have definite or definable limits without anything to 
indicate or fix said limits?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-28T01:07:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4bejanF119no9U4@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <1146000789.305185.254760@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <260420061808390686%usrr@post.no.mail> <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net> <e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> limit but it has definite or definable limits'.

Exactly.
>
> How does something have definite or definable limits without anything to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Consider the Earth (or a balloon or anything else, like the Universe, 
roughly spherical)

I can wander in an infinite number of paths on the surface of any such 
sphere (or through it, if that is physically possible as it is with the 
Universe or, by imagination, the balloon).  There is no limit to the paths I 
could trace. For every path you can visualise, I can go one step further. An 
infinite number of journeys. Yet I cannot move off (or out of) the sphere. 
So my infinite journeys are bounded by the sphere. Yet I can go around it as 
often as I like,  and none of my journeys are physically stopped by the 
sphere, hence, it is boundless.

Now apply that model to your statement above.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 69)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-28T15:55:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2tdsp$7am$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net> <e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com> <4bejanF119no9U4@individual.net>`

```
In article <4bejanF119no9U4@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>news:260420061808390686%usrr@post.no.mail...

[snip]

>>>> The resources of the universe that we can see are to all practical
>>>> purposes infinite.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Exactly.

Well... glad I got that right.

>>
>> How does something have definite or definable limits without anything to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>could trace.

There is a limit, however, to the space that can be covered, ie the 
surface area of the sphere.  For it to be otherwise seems to be saying 
that to re-trace one's steps over the same ground, constantly, constitutes 
a possibility of infinity.

>For every path you can visualise, I can go one step further.

Given that you've postulated my ability to visualise as a boundary... I am 
not sure how you are using 'further' here.  I visualise a path, starting 
at one point, one footstep wide, in a spiral.  Where you begin may be 
called a 'pole point'; the spiral expands as you approach the 'equator' 
(full circumference) and contracts until you reach the opposite 'pole 
point'.

When this occurs you have covered the entire surface, there are no more 
paths to be described except those which go over areas already covered.

>An 
>infinite number of journeys.

An infinite retracing or duplicating of steps (points), perhaps... but a 
finite area exists.

>Yet I cannot move off (or out of) the sphere. 
>So my infinite journeys are bounded by the sphere.

Only if you limit yourself to the boundary of the surface, Mr Dashwood.  
In that it possible to leave the surface your journeys are not bounded.

>Yet I can go around it as 
>often as I like,  and none of my journeys are physically stopped by the 
>sphere, hence, it is boundless.
>
>Now apply that model to your statement above.

I did... and it seems the conclusion that 'if one wishes to repeat 
themselves then any area to which one wishes to confine one'sself is 
infinite'... but that seems to neglects the metaphysic of choosing to 
confine one'sself.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 68)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-27T14:34:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OD44g.813$nq3.721@clgrps12>`
- **References:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <1146000789.305185.254760@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <260420061808390686%usrr@post.no.mail> <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net> <e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> indicate or fix said limits?

    Imagine you're an ant on a ball that is suspended in space. You walk 
along the surface of the ball, trying to find the "edge of the universe", 
but no matter where you go, you will never find an edge. The surface has no 
boundaries. Now let's say you start marking every location you've been to, 
by painting the surface there black. After a while, you will have painted 
the entire surface of the ball black, thus showing that the ball is 
"finite", but "boundless".

    Last time I checked (late 90s), the most popular theories were that the 
universe was shaped like a hypertorus (i.e. the shape of the universe is to 
3D as what a donut is to 2D), which like a sphere, has the properties of 
being finite, but boundless.

    - Oliver
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()

- **References:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com> <4bbo05FvoqgmU1@individual.net> <e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com> <OD44g.813$nq3.721@clgrps12>`

```
In article <OD44g.813$nq3.721@clgrps12>,
Oliver Wong <owong@castortech.com> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e2qdve$hh4$1@reader1.panix.com...
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Imagine you're an ant on a ball that is suspended in space.

I migh just do that, Mr Wong, when I have the desire to discuss what 
conclusions I could come to were I to imagine I am an ant on a ball that 
is suspended in space.  Right now I'm trying to figure out how, as a human 
being sharing almost the same language, Mr Dashwood would want others to 
perceive a statement which, by definition(s), appears to violate the 
Aristotelean principle of non-contradiction.

[snip]

>    Last time I checked (late 90s), the most popular theories were that the 
>universe was shaped like a hypertorus (i.e. the shape of the universe is to 
>3D as what a donut is to 2D), which like a sphere, has the properties of 
>being finite, but boundless.

Monodimensional string theory was popular at one time, as well... I wonder 
whatever happened to that (but I don't wonder hard enough to motivate 
myself to research it).  The hypertorus you describe sounds like some 
Lobachevskian constructs with which I might be familiar.

DD
```

```
> "Joseph Katnic" <usrr@post.no.mail> wrote in message
> news:260420061808390686%usrr@post.no.mail...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

You must be right Pete because I agree with you! If you subscribe to
the notion that we are confined to a "light-cone" (see Hawking's "A
brief history of time") then the resources available to us are finite
and limited to those available to us within the light-cone. If you
choose to subscribe to the idea of faster-than-light travel, the
resources available to us are greater in measure but still limited.
```

- **References:** `<CgQ1g.804$Fd6.730@edtnps82> <4atgvfFv499qU1@individual.net> <kukp425qg92om210b89faanig3fo88un4q@4ax.com> <4b5a4tFuut7uU1@individual.net> <v5cs4251us8rknuj4ano1degclnke2rvsp@4ax.com> <1145992605.782857.223800@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <1qus42tf55oss14vf4tj52ud35eh8k5l2l@4ax.com> <1145998147.228460.25570@y43g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <v03t421fje3b46n54othp40cmellpcjfce@4ax.com> <1146000789.305185.254760@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 25 Apr 2006 14:33:09 -0700, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>That is probably because, like capitalists of old, you consider that
>all resources are infinite (for example the mine will never run out)


Sure sunlight will run out - but in my subset of the universe, I can
assume that we will keep getting more sun energy, adding value to the
seeds I planted.

The sun is getting diminished - whether or not I use that solar energy
to my gain.

>and that waste products are some one else's problem (who cares that
>slagheaps go to school in Wales), and that other countries and the poor
>are merely there for the exploitation by capitalists and imperialists.

Is recycling a net zero process?

If so, then there obviously is not reason to recycle.  


>In other words: as long as _you_ get 'more stuff' you neither see nor
>care about those that wind up with 'less stuff'.

What I'm saying is that there is more net stuff everywhere.    More
people avoid starvation.  

If it was a net zero, then every time I have seconds, someone else in
this world skips a meal.   That doesn't happen.

>A capitalist argument is: if everyone mows their neibours lawn instead
>of their own and charges and pays $30 for this service then the economy
>will be $3 billion better. They will then try to charge a small fee for
>coordinating this to make themselves a few $million better.

The capitalist argument says that when a buddy and I get together to
fix yards, the guy with the mower mows, and the guy with the trimmer
trims, and we get the work done more efficiently, get done, and
produce other stuff such as Usenet messages or symphonies.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:bvuu42dvhjj9na6kkbtu0l5bq486jtp5lc@4ax.com...
> On 25 Apr 2006 14:33:09 -0700, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Strictly speaking the sun is getting redispersed. The solar energy is 
neither created nor destroyed. It has existed since the big bang. It is 
transformed into heat and light (via hydrogen and helium if you like). 
Before it coalesced into the sun, it was totally dispersed, but there is 
just as much of it now as there was then.

>>and that waste products are some one else's problem (who cares that
>>slagheaps go to school in Wales), and that other countries and the poor
>>are merely there for the exploitation by capitalists and imperialists.
>
> Is recycling a net zero process?

Yes. Transformation is what happens to the energy (and therefore the matter) 
in this universe. It is transformed into matter and back into energy again. 
Nothing is lost; nothing is gained. Some of it pops out of existence across 
the Planck level; an equal amount pops back into existence the same way. 
Matter and antimatter. Reality and Virtuality. But the same number of chips 
at the end of the game as was on the table at the start. It came from 
nothing and it equates to nothing. The significance of it is assigned by us.

>
> If so, then there obviously is not reason to recycle.

Recycling brings order to the environment and allows resources to last 
longer in a form that we can use. (the cost is heat and energy, but that 
dissipates to the point where we are not affected by it.)

>
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> this world skips a meal.   That doesn't happen.

That is SO not true, Howard. It is certainly true that when you have 
seconds, someone else doesn't get to eat your second...
>
>>A capitalist argument is: if everyone mows their neibours lawn instead
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> produce other stuff such as Usenet messages or symphonies.

You work on the symphonies...me, I just had an idea for a million dollar 
lawn mowing business... :-)

Pete.
```

```
On Thu, 27 Apr 2006 23:22:18 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>But all of it (even the sevices and the creative art) consume energy, time, 
>and resource. At a fundamental level, what is 'consumed' (and nothing ever 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Zero Sum.

It is making the word "value" valueless.

When we leave the ivory tower and look at real life, we have values.
We value some things more than others.

Zero sum means that any change only increases value of one thing by
decreasing value of something else.    This yin/yang philosophy
implies that when you pick up a crying baby and take value in that
baby's content - that value is paid for by a loss of value elsewhere.

Everything is futile.   Do good, and evil will be created to keep the
Zero Sum ledger.    Write a program, and it will be balanced by bugs
being created elsewhere.
```

```
On Thu, 20 Apr 2006 15:24:39 GMT, "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
wrote:

>    Right, but I was measuring money (or more generally, financial worth), 
>not happiness or emotional value.

Money is just a measure of stuff.   If the stuff remains constant,
then it's a zero sum game.   If we have a different amount of stuff,
then it is not a zero sum game.

(The fact that "stuff" comes from solar energy and the sun is being
used up really doesn't enter into a discussion of money).
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1145561440.079139.64360@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <9296e$44457510$45491d7a$6607@KNOLOGY.NET> <Vtr1g.16308$Vg.10089@edtnps90> <59f88$4446e1be$45491d12$9184@KNOLOGY.NET> <EEM1g.769$Fd6.514@edtnps82> <rp6f42phvutir799q0ua2ravuchqvol69n@4ax.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote:

>  Move a factory into a
> town where people are starving and the rich don't start missing meals
> in order to feed the newly hired workers.   Everybody is better off.

What about those in the town that^H^H^H which the factory moved out of
?

> But we have more stuff.    If "winning" is having more stuff than your
> neighbor, then you're right - you either win or lose.    If "winning"
> is having enough so you would be happy if your richer neighbor was
> poor, then it's not a zero sum game.

But it also applies to countries. Imperialism works by using the
resources and labour of the poor countries to keep more of the rich
countries richer and the poor countries poorer.

British imperialism make Britain great (though 'Great Britain' means
something completely different), German Imperialism (from 1935 to 1945)
attempted to make Pan-germanism great. Now corporate imperialism via
outsourcing and the world bank is keeping the corporations richer and
the serfs and the rest poorer through debt, supported by Bush and the
US forces.

> Even if we were all billionaires, we couldn't do even a tiny
> percentage of the fun stuff we want to do.

Even if I had no money I can do as much 'fun stuff' as I want to, for
my version of what 'fun stuff' is. The problem is where you believe the
corporate version of what you should think that 'fun stuff' is: buy a
bigger 4WD, fly to far away places, throw money at a casino, build a
bigger house.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 54)_

```
On 20 Apr 2006 12:30:40 -0700, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>But it also applies to countries. Imperialism works by using the
>resources and labour of the poor countries to keep more of the rich
>countries richer and the poor countries poorer.

Currently sweat shops mean more people eat, and less jungle is cut
down.   I have to admit that mining doesn't have the advantage that
factories raising the long term standard of living though.

>Even if I had no money I can do as much 'fun stuff' as I want to, for
>my version of what 'fun stuff' is. The problem is where you believe the
>corporate version of what you should think that 'fun stuff' is: buy a
>bigger 4WD, fly to far away places, throw money at a casino, build a
>bigger house.

Think of the example of "more stuff" as "enough food to feed your
family".     Today, most starvation is in countries with civil war.
War is a negative sum transaction.
```

```
On Tue, 18 Apr 2006 18:24:11 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>This is laughable.  And, you're missing one very important part of what 
>makes capitalism work every time it's tried - *motivation*.  If you know 
>that you can leave none or very little of your accumulated wealth to 
>your children, then where's the motivation to build your business? 
>Don't *you* want *your* children to have it easier than you did?

There's an interesting movement among the very wealthy to decide how
much is best to leave to their children.    Then they give everything
else away to other projects they care about that won't get spoiled by
having values different from what created that wealth in the first
place.
```

- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It won't if there are many such entities. That was my point above.

> Doing away with death duties will solidify that trend. Note that Habeus
> Corpus, about the only right as old as the Magna Carta, has been
> repealed in the states.
>
Good Grief! I didn't know that! Why would they do that?!!!

> The United States has become a monarchy.  The only difference is their
> monarchy is for limited term, and a new monarch has to be chosen every
> couple years.

I think that's a very fair comment. Maybe it started with Kennedy and 
"Camelot"....

Certainly the deification of the cult of Celebrity in the US is a lot like 
Monarchy, and the First Family are the epitome of Celebrity.


> LX-i reminds me of one of her majestie's loyal officers,
> willing to die for the crown, regardless of what the crown does or says.

Such men have been a part of every Court. The Samurai in Japan, the Old 
Guard in Napoleonic times, The SAS and Green Berets today. For a 
professional soldier there is no dishonor in serving. Neither should there 
be.

I was a  (conscripted) soldier. I refused to swear an oath of allegiance to 
"Queen Elizabeth the Second (nothing personal; I respect her and think she 
is a good Queen), Her heirs and Successors". They said "Will you swear to 
defend NZ and obey the Commander in Chief? "

That seemed fair so I agreed. They gave me a Bible; I told them they might 
as well give me a Donald Duck comic... "OK, no point in you swearing... Will 
you give us your solemn promise?" I did. And that cuts some ice with me... 
I've kept it. I am no longer on the Reserve but if we are ever invaded you 
can bet I'll make good my promise...:-) (One reason we are unlikely to be 
invaded is because a number of young Americans gave their lives at the 
Battle of the Coral Sea in WW2. Had they not done so, I would be writing 
this in Japanese, always assuming I was able to write it at all...)

This weekend in New Zealand we mark the Anniversary of the Anzac (Australia 
and NZ Army Corps) landings on Gallipoli in the first World War. A total 
disaster and chronicle of mismanagement, bad intelligence, bad 
communication, and military incompetence. Our boys died there by the 
thousand, misled by officers who were chinless wonders  from the English 
aristocracy. Men wrote letters to their wives and families knowing they were 
about to face certain death, then went 'over the top' into the Turkish 
machine guns and were mown down like ripe corn. Were they stupid? Should we 
despise their unflinching, unquestioning dedication ?

You can if you like. Me, I weep for them...

And I'm proud of them too.

Daniel (LX-i) is a serving airman. He has chosen that profession and 
dedicated his life (literally) to it.  (at least for the term of his 
enlistment). All serving soldiers serve the government of the day. That's 
why the US Army (or the British or NZ army for that matter) will never take 
part in an overthrow of the government. In a Democracy, the armed services 
are sworn to serve the people, through the duly and legally elected 
representatives of the people.

It is unconditional.

Not "Only if I agree with them" or "As long as I believe they're right." 
You serve. The only exception would be when/if you are commanded to do 
something you know to be illegal. (Yes, I know there are questions of 
legality over the Western presence in Iraq, but until they are resolved, 
every serviceperson there is doing their duty.)

We should be thankful we have young men and women prepared to put their 
lives on the line for a way of life they believe in. I guess I'm one of 
those who may not support President Bush, but I certainly support the armed 
forces in that theatre. All of them. War is not a good way to settle 
disputes, but giving people security and protection so they can discuss 
their issues and vote on them seems to me to be a worthwhile use of force.

> He and such policies belong in the dark ages.

I beg to gently differ, Donald. It was people like that who brought us OUT 
of the Dark Ages. If someone didn't stand against the precept that 'Might is 
Right' we would still be in the Dark Ages.

>Unfortunately, we have
> not progressed much beyond them.
>
I disagree, but not entirely. You are right that in some ways Human Beings 
still try to get their way by force.  But we have come a long way since the 
8th Century.

Not all force is bad. There is a need to guarantee safety and security. 
There has always been that need. Some people have always accepted that 
challenge.

Whether you would give unquestioning loyalty or I would is not important.

We should appreciate the ones who stand on watch while we go about our 
lives, and be thankful that they do.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1e5hs$5ji$1@reader1.panix.com> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <4as8qfFuugarU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
<huge snippage to cut to the crux>
> 
> I disagree, but not entirely. You are right that in some ways Human Beings 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

yes, I agree with you.  With a caveat.  Unless that force is tempered
with law, and that law is universal, we will never have peace or
security. The unniversality is a key.

What I am objecting to is the contention that laws only apply to *some*
people, and that others are exempt.  The idea that someone can flout the
law, because they are part of the law-making team is something I will
fight to my dying day.

I do not want(or need) police that act outside and beyond the law, in
any shape or form.  The fact that they are police does not exempt them
from the law.  In fact, it makes it *more* important that they respect
the law.

The idea that the benefit of protective laws only apply to the select
citizenry of one country is as good a way as any to bring that country
into disrepute, IMO.

And the idea that an army can sell the constitution of a country by
going to another country and flouting every concept in that constitution
is extremely stupid, IMO. I really doubt it will bring about a similar
constitution in the country so invaded, and cannot comprehend how any
thinking person would believe so.

Donald
```

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:Wp72g.2829$DR6.211343@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> <huge snippage to cut to the crux>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Universal law? I guess there are some things that all societies could agree 
on, but any form of law has to be apposite to the local conditions, doesn't 
it?

Thinking about it, I guess the UN Human Rights are a step in that direction.

Sadly, without enforcement, there is no law.

> What I am objecting to is the contention that laws only apply to *some*
> people, and that others are exempt.  The idea that someone can flout the
> law, because they are part of the law-making team is something I will
> fight to my dying day.
>
Yep. Me too.

> I do not want(or need) police that act outside and beyond the law, in
> any shape or form.  The fact that they are police does not exempt them
> from the law.  In fact, it makes it *more* important that they respect
> the law.
>
Completely agree. We are pretty lucky here in that regard. For the most part 
our cops are good. (They don't have guns and I think that helps with 
attitude...). Of course, they have access to guns if required when dealing 
with armed offenders, but they don't carry guns as a matter of course.


> The idea that the benefit of protective laws only apply to the select
> citizenry of one country is as good a way as any to bring that country
> into disrepute, IMO.
>
Hmmm... not sure.

> And the idea that an army can sell the constitution of a country by
> going to another country and flouting every concept in that constitution
> is extremely stupid, IMO.

Yes. There have been too many horror stories coming out of Iraq. I think 
(hope) that lessons have been learned.

>I really doubt it will bring about a similar
> constitution in the country so invaded, and cannot comprehend how any
> thinking person would believe so.
>
I agree with you here. It think it will take at least 100 years before a 
stable democracy can be installed in Iraq. I don't think the West can or 
should be there that long.

Pete.
```

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
> Completely agree. We are pretty lucky here in that regard. For the most part 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I wish ours did not, I think we would be better off.  A pistol is a
pretty poor weapon anyway. I see nothing wrong with a police officer
carrying a shotgun or rifle when needed, but I think a proliferation of
pistols just makes the problem worse.

Being as close to the US as we are makes the problem even worse.  Most
of the illegal weapons in Canada come over from the states, and we still
get lots of Americans insisting they have a right to carry a pistol in
Canada.

> 
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hmmm... not sure.

Notice I said "protective laws". Like the right to a lawyer, and the
right to be charged with a crime before being jailed. I did not say they
should be immune from law. If they break laws, then they should be
charged like anybody else.  But arbitrary imprisonment?  For anybody the
government does not like? If they are illegal impigrants, or working
illegally, then they should be charged and prosecuted.  By due proccess,
not at the whim of an official.
> 
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
What really pisses me off is that Bush *could* have done it right.
Saddam Hussein was a tyrant, and the people were welcoming the
Americans. Then they decided to play political games, and became as
represive as he was.  They're body count per year is higher than his was.

If the kind of rights and laws that the Americans have at home had been
put in place, it is doubtfull we'd be where we are.

Donald
```

```
On Mon, 24 Apr 2006 16:49:33 -0400, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>> Does it mean that all laws apply equally to children and for adults?
>> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>They do in Canada, I do not know in the states.

In the United States, we have statutory rape laws.   We restrict
children from voting in national elections.   We give different
welfare benefits to the elderly.   We don't allow children to get
driver's licenses.   We don't allow children to buy liquor, porn, nor
cigarettes.   

It is interesting to discover that Canada is different.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Flexible standards in law and in CoBOL

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-28T10:18:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0cf452d448fpk9j9s95uhh380l97u12lg7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <4bejalF119no9U2@individual.net> <ydp4g.166$zn1.162@edtnps90> <4884525rvci89p75rugkj9cjkgpv93pe43@4ax.com> <e2te0e$qtt$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Fri, 28 Apr 2006 15:57:02 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>>I believe New Mexico has the age of consent of 14.   It varies from
>>state to state in the U.S.
>
>According to http://www.webistry.net/jan/consent.html (copyright 1999) New 
>Mexico is 17; Hawaii and Idaho are 14.

Well, shoot, I suppose I shall have to change my travel plans.   

(I knew a few years back New Mexico was thinking about changing their
law).

I met someone from Columbia a couple of decades ago who was too young
to get a drivers licence in the U.S.   He had had one in Columbia, but
was required to buy insurance which was very, very expensive for
someone of his age.     He could have gotten a license if he was a
farm-worker.    My nephew in Colorado and my niece in North Carolina
had very different laws about getting under-age permits - and for who
they can drive when they had licenses after they were 16.   I don't
know what applies when they visit each other.

Some states are becoming less anal about age of consent as well,
particularly with two kids who are month different in age - but one is
below the age of consent.   There have been cases where a boy will
have sex with a girl older than him - but he goes to jail for rape if
they are both underage.   In this country, that type of case is going
away.

Speaking of flexible standards, it is interesting to note what happens
when shops move to OO.    The testing standards cannot be the same
when someone modifies objects used ubiquitously.    I know of a case
where a shop considered OO CoBOL but went to C++ instead for some
shops because the guy making the decision did not want to go through
the fighting the status quo standards for CoBOL.   (There were no
standards yet for C++).
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:0cf452d448fpk9j9s95uhh380l97u12lg7@4ax.com...
>>According to http://www.webistry.net/jan/consent.html (copyright 1999) New
>>Mexico is 17; Hawaii and Idaho are 14.
>
> Well, shoot, I suppose I shall have to change my travel plans.

I found this just a bit alarming:  a closer examination of the list seems to 
indicate that the age of consent in Mississippi is 16 for virgins but 12 for 
nonvirgins.

Almost as alarming is that the age of consent in Kentucky seems to be 16 
*only if* the man is 21 or older.  It's not clear from the list what the age 
of consent for her is if he's younger than 21.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message 
news:kZr3g.1281$fx.203598@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Oliver Wong wrote:
>> "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> So is a police officer.

    I'm not a lawyer, so I don't know the exact wording of the law, but I 
had presumed it was something along the lines of "if you ignore the red 
light, you have to pay a fine, regardless of whether or not any harm 
actually came from you ignoring that red light". Also presumably, most of 
the time (e.g. when not in self defense), you're in trouble whenever you 
kill someone, regardless of whether red lights were involved or not. I 
figured you'd be charged with two crimes (manslaughter, and ignoring a red 
light), rather than being charged with one crime (manslaughter while 
ignoring a red light).

    I suppose a police officer could loophole their way out of this by 
having the police department pay the fine (to themselves) on behalf of the 
officer.

    - Oliver
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-04-25T13:08:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jIs3g.1163$1V4.81585@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<pls3g.343$aI4.80@edtnps89>`
- **References:** `<6ea53$443805cf$45491d7a$22496@KNOLOGY.NET> <8ec34$443b0a66$45491d7a$26947@KNOLOGY.NET> <e1gfst$9rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <483b$443c4d3b$45491d7a$19919@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144814111.437518.143050@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <4e31$443d9a61$45491d7a$9941@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144898716.226668.106050@t31g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <4a71s5Frudh8U1@individual.net> <ecqs32lgbvfiuo8ob51gife04kisvc4494@4ax.com> <4a83ckFs5nruU1@individual.net> <dj5042ldcp68l1sjp2lhgb11cb4eiqmu4a@4ax.com> <4aci6iFsde1cU1@individual.net> <4cf97$44418822$45491d7a$5404@KNOLOGY.NET> <1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <4as8qfFuugarU1@individual.net> <Wp72g.2829$DR6.211343@news20.bellglobal.com> <MNp3g.329$aI4.289@edtnps89> <kZr3g.1281$fx.203598@news20.bellglobal.com> <pls3g.343$aI4.80@edtnps89>`

```
Oliver Wong wrote:
> 
> "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>    - Oliver

I do not know the law in the United States, however in Canada, the
ability to break a non-criminal law is the same for everyone.  If you
were driving your child to the hospital, and it was life and death, so
you ran a light, for example, you can use the exact same provisions
available to an ambulance driver. You are also under a similar
obligation to use due care. I doubt, under those circumstances, that you
would be charged, actually. Precident law tends to apply in most of
those cases, and there is lots of it.  For example, in an emergency,
driving without a license can probably be gotten away with, or breaking
trespass laws, as was mentioned by Howard.

In the States, I believe that non-criminal equates to "not indictable".

It is criminal law that I was addressing, more than "rule" type
non-criminal law.  Treatment in court is seldom done on an "emergancy"
basis, so that type of arguement would seldom be usefull when it comes
to rights.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: US Presidents; an outside view WAS: Any comments? (Evolution - was Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 54)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-25T11:51:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<68os42p0m5bn78htihkq2fkj4obpdf6gp5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1145284864.785254.124850@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <aade7$44441a14$45491d7a$10780@KNOLOGY.NET> <ltV0g.2292$wK1.134911@news20.bellglobal.com> <4ak4agFtokfpU1@individual.net> <jz61g.3065$wK1.202881@news20.bellglobal.com> <4as8qfFuugarU1@individual.net> <Wp72g.2829$DR6.211343@news20.bellglobal.com> <MNp3g.329$aI4.289@edtnps89> <kZr3g.1281$fx.203598@news20.bellglobal.com> <pls3g.343$aI4.80@edtnps89> <jIs3g.1163$1V4.81585@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Tue, 25 Apr 2006 13:08:30 -0400, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>I do not know the law in the United States, however in Canada, the
>ability to break a non-criminal law is the same for everyone.  If you
>were driving your child to the hospital, and it was life and death, so
>you ran a light, for example, you can use the exact same provisions
>available to an ambulance driver.

There is a difference.    Certain people are entitled to drive with a
flashing red light on their vehicles which are designed for emergency
use.   When the vehicle is operating with these flashing for a
legitimate purpose, they have right-of-way despite the lights. (Right
of way doesn't give them carte blanche).

Most of us do not have the rights to use those lights.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
> This weekend in New Zealand we mark the Anniversary of the Anzac (Australia
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> aristocracy.

We do have some chinned wonders in the aristocracy as well. IIRC, not
that I was there, the problem was the failure to get off of the beaches
which allowed the Turks to mount an armed response.

> Men wrote letters to their wives and families knowing they were
> about to face certain death, then went 'over the top' into the Turkish
> machine guns and were mown down like ripe corn.

Clearly a failure to appreciate how the machine gun had changed
"modern" warfare.

>
> Daniel (LX-i) is a serving airman. He has chosen that profession and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> part in an overthrow of the government.

Er... the UK armed forces are loyal to the Crown (Her Majesty QE2).

> In a Democracy, the armed services
> are sworn to serve the people, through the duly and legally elected
> representatives of the people.
>
```

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-22T12:20:44+12:00

```
>

Don't start me on it, Alistair :-)

English officers were taking tea on the wrong beaches while men were being

happened. I just feel sorry for the pointless waste. And on this weekend we 
remember them.

>> Men wrote letters to their wives and families knowing they were
>> about to face certain death, then went 'over the top' into the Turkish
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No, they KNEW they would die. It was about duty and not letting your mates, 
or the folks back home, down.
>>
>> Daniel (LX-i) is a serving airman. He has chosen that profession and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Er... the UK armed forces are loyal to the Crown (Her Majesty QE2).

Yes. A valid point. However, she is a constitutional monarch and, as such, 
the head of a democratically elected government.

>
>> In a Democracy, the armed services
>> are sworn to serve the people, through the duly and legally elected
>> representatives of the people.
>>

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:4as8qfFuugarU1@individual.net...
[snip]
> In a Democracy, the armed services
> are sworn to serve the people, through the duly and legally elected
> representatives of the people.

Arguably, the United States is a republic and not a
democracy. Nonetheless, the obligation of those
entering [US] military service is, as I recall, "to protect
and defend the Constitution of the United States against
all enemies, foreign and domestic". Interestingly, those
'duly and legally elected representative' who "shall be
bound by oath or affirmation to support this [the US]
Constitution" and fail to do so are, by no stretch of
logic or imagination, domestic enemies of the
[US] Constitution. It took me 37 years to recognize
this connection as fundamental to the stress that led
to my disability.

[However, the US seems to take care of its ex-military.
I am now receiving health care through the Veteran's
Health Administration and, thus far, at no cost to me.
Furthermore, I was told that I am qualified to receive
a pension for "permanent and total disability", though
it will take about a year to complete the process.
Interestingly, soon after my first visit to the local
Veteran's Administration Community-Based Outpatient
Clinic, I had a mild heart attack, another a month later,
followed by a blood clot a month after that.]
```
