- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <e18g2p$9c4$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144938222.973033.158450@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <e1lnv7$e0h$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <1144938222.973033.158450@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>,
> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> least once, if it is solved then it doesn't appear to be insolvable.

Precisely. Please read the book, for me it was a life-changer. The idea
is that she puts forward an unsolvable problem. If a solution is found
then she gets loads of brownie points. If no solution is found she
still gets loads of brownie points (because she was proven to be right
first time).

>
> >Her (?) management do not appear to wish to be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> actively') in order to insure failure.

Yes. That is probably why she will fail. Certainly not for ineptitude
or lack of effort on her part.

>
> >Perhaps Holly should make a document listing each potential
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

No, but it does two things - provides an aide memoire and will show
sequence of actions. These can be very difficult to recall when needed.
I believe (but have no proof) that it would work in a British court
when accompanied by original documentation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-13T15:23:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144966998.249275.62560@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <e18g2p$9c4$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144938222.973033.158450@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <e1lnv7$e0h$1@reader1.panix.com> <1144948439.037681.111130@e56g2000cwe.googlegroups.com>`

```
  - I edited the original post I did to make this "neutral" with no key
words anybody could easily recognize.

Thank you Alistair.  I think I'll do that, list all of the
point/counterpoint type of examples.  This truly has been a learning
experience.
We do have a new development here today, I think the rumor is that we
are getting some outside consultants.  It's not like anyone asked us,
but hey, they didn't consult us on anything else so we aren't really
surprised.
We'll probably be demoted to data analysts or project managers or
something like that while these programmers fresh out of college
working for "insert cheesy company" convert our code, but since they
have no healthcare knowledge or understanding of mainframe systems
it is really going to be a joy to pick up the pieces once they've
finished.
Once all of the interesting code is written for us (gee, thanks),
we then have the opportunity of getting it to work with
this new Oracle database I'm having the pleasure of testing tonight.

I have until June to be fully vested in this sinkhole, so I think I'll
take the money and run when the time comes.
Then I won't have to be here to be liable for all of the potential
security
problems that will result from them asking us questions we aren't
qualified to answer (legal, etc), and from somebody else doing the work
that we are
actually somewhat qualified to do (programming).
Why they decided to grab programmers with mainframe experience in the
first place is totally beyond me, but it wasn't like we had a choice.
We came in to company A one day, and then heard - Congratulations you
are now working for "subsidiary" company B.  I don't think they thought
about
our background or even cared what we wanted to do.

Calgon - take me away!


I am also taking some satisfaction in writing this post at work. The
danger - what a rush!  Whoo Hoo!
I doubt they know about cobol or any other language enough to find it
 :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-04-14T00:39:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1mqvt$775$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <1144938222.973033.158450@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <e1lnv7$e0h$1@reader1.panix.com> <4a85f1Fs57dkU1@individual.net>`

```
In article <4a85f1Fs57dkU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e1lnv7$e0h$1@reader1.panix.com...

[snip]

>> The management does not (note the use of the singular, Amerispeak, for
>> 'management' as contrasted to the plural used in Britspeak) appear to be
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Perhaps 'ensure' was intended...? (or even entended...)

Hmmmm... perhaps another difference between Amerispeak and Britspeak?  
According to http://m-w.com/dictionary/insure , 'to make certain 
especially by taking necessary measures and precautions'... let's see what 
the OED has to say... funny thing, that OED... did you know it shows 
Shakespeare and Marlowe using 'dialog' as a verb?  Let's see... Vol I... 
page 1454... page 363... column 2... entry four... 'variant of ENSURE'... 
over to column 3... ahhhhhh.

5. trans To make certain, to secure, to guarantee (some thing, event, 
etc.): = ASSURE, v. 5, 7, a, ENSURE v. 8, 9.

Must be one of those American usages... there, first citing is '1681-6, J. 
Scott, Chr. Life, (1747) III 21. Thus Christ... hath taken the most 
effectual Care to insure the mutual Performance of this everlasting 
Covenant to both Parties...'

'Insure' was used, Mr Dashwood, in a manner which the OED states has been 
around for some three centuries and change... mustn't rush into these new 
fashions, I guess.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 25)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T10:55:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b1008$4437dccf$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Holly wrote:
> Thank you for helping out a hopeless cause-
> We discussed Microfocus and that was what we were going to do until our
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> they surprise us by changing the platform.

This is the first time I've ever heard of someone converting VB to 
COBOL.  :)  I've personally done it the other way around.  In fact, we 
have a process that we use for ad-hoc updates to the database, where 
they key changes daily (and is specific to the database partition 
they're updating).  It used to not be specific to the database - and, 
the algorithm was widely known.

What the customer wanted me to do was change it up to be 
database-specific, and set up a process to e-mail the control keys for 
"today" and "tomorrow" to the Major Command database managers; then, 
they would be in charge of allowing these ad-hoc updates.  (This keeps 
people from continually "fixing" the database instead of reporting the 
broken programs so we can fix them.)  The web server that sends e-mails 
ran ASP (which is VBScript).  So, I had to mimic the COBOL algorithm in 
VB - that was a challenge.  :)

> So now we have two options, option 1 is to use the VB apps that are
> currently running in production, that we know work, or option 2 to use
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> won't be able to get it to work by May 1st.

Personally, I'd stick with the known-good app over the unknown app.  :) 
  (especially with the time crunch you're working under)

> It's sort of out of my control, I feel more comfortable with our
> existing VB apps, using .NET and VBScripting.  But my team lead, I
> think, wants to go with Microfocus cobol., which is fine too but
> wouldn't be my choice.

If they end up targeting a .NET environment, then the language becomes 
somewhat less important.  Microsoft has tools that will convert a VB 6 
app to VB.NET.  And, if there are going to be more apps built, I'd start 
getting smart on C#.  C# and VB.NET interoperability is very good, and 
the C# is enough like C++ that you'll end up being able to write and 
maintain it as well.

> Eventually they will decide and then let me know a couple days before
> the deadline what it is.  But as a programmer, not a systems analyst or
> network analyst, I have a bit of a learning curve, especially when I'm
> asking all of these questions and my team lead and manager aren't.  It
> scares me a bit.

That's natural.  But, if you know the basic programming concepts (data 
manipulation, decision structures, etc.), given a manual, you could 
probably pick up whatever language they throw at you.

Also, if you're interested in doing some stuff on your own, Mono 
(http://www.mono-project.com) is an open-source implementation of C#, 
that runs in the Microsoft .NET environment.
```

- **References:** `<1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <b1008$4437dccf$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
Thank you Daniel!  I know I got a bit sidetracked on the religion and
the politics and all, but I really do respect your beliefs.  And
especially when you have been so helpful to me on this.  I'm a bit
sleep deprived and stressed out.  I appreciate the tips and
encouragement, that is very nice of you.  I wish I could convert my
team lead to VB right now 'cos that's what a couple of us agree would
be the easiest.  But I think he is one of the anti-microsoft
contingent, for some reason he refuses to believe that VB is how we
should go.  For our apps, we are simply receiving data feeds,
reformatting some of them, adding them to an Oracle database (currently
DB2 which is going away) and extracting data based on criteria.  It's
very simple really.  So to him, I think, Cobol was what he knew.  And
thinking we were going to aix, he probably thought it would be quickest
to redo the programs in cobol and use microfocus (which we do have but
only for aix not windows).  Now, we have this Windows server agreed on,
so we are trying to campaign to keep the existing VB code for the
implementation and then we wouldn't be so bugged out.

Maybe I could use some prayers - I guess I gotta go to church tomorrow.
 Thank you again - you are a good guy!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Holly" <anderschwan@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2006-04-10T10:13:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1144689212.732350.66220@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144489292.670943.151460@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <b1008$4437dccf$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET> <1144517273.147549.143130@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <enrk325dm81j3p8ogjkgffgvmfkc9rhslh@4ax.com>`

```
Hey Howard.  I respect anyone's beliefs and their right to have them. I
want them also to respect mine, which I know is hard for some people to
do on both sides.  I know a lot of very good people who don't believe
the same things I do, they are very conservative around here and I
don't fault people for that.  We were raised in a semi-liberal
household (liberal for Tennessee anyway) where we could think for
ourselves.  And maybe some of my ideas aren't for everyone.  We
travelled a lot as a family, my dad was a pilot, and I got to see the
world and meet all kinds of people.  Not everybody has had that same
kind of experience so they are going by what they know.  Helping people
to see that there are other valid beliefs and that it all really boils
down to being a good person, doing no harm to others, and loving your
neighbor should be what it is all about.  I think that is what Jesus
did which is why I think Christianity is a beautiful religion.  It's
only when people use it to control other people or as law that it
starts to become something else.  So if they have the right motivation
to help their fellow human beings I respect that.
I may not agree with how they go about doing it, but I can still
understand the motivation why.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<e1e4c503kb@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <vF6Zf.2723$pZ3.397479@news20.bellglobal.com> <1144383716.355143.229970@g10g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

In article <2263a$443707b3$45491d7a$6185@KNOLOGY.NET>, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> writes:
> anderschwan@hotmail.com wrote:
> > What generally is easier, from COBOL, would it be fujitsu cobol, cobol
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL.

We do.  I don't know about what Fujitsu has, but no doubt there is
some information about that on their website.  I do know that our
emulation of mainframe environments like CICS is less intrusive
than theirs, which requires source code changes.

> (I've never used them, but I've heard others discuss them here, 
> and I've had them advertised to me - I think it was Micro Focus and 
> their .NET Express product.)

Actually, confusingly, it's "Micro Focus Net Express", which was
eventually joined by "Micro Focus Net Express with .NET".  We were
using the "Net Express" name before Microsoft introduced .NET (which
is, IMO, as dumb a moniker as they've coined yet, but inexplicably
Microsoft has yet to put me in charge of their product branding).

In May, Net Express officially is replaced by Micro Focus Studio,
which (on Windows) contains Net Express 5 and other things.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(reply depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-08T02:56:34+12:00

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sure, we as people feel that way - but, then again, we aren't God.

    By this argument, it doesn't matter what answer anyone gives to the 
question coming up, as whoever answers it is (probably) not God, and so 
their answer is "wrong".

> If you are the absolute, one and only Creator of the Universe, aren't you 
> entitled to demand respect if your uppity creations don't give it on their 
> own?

    I have no idea how one determines whether God, or anyone else to that 
matter, is "entitled" to something or not. I raised a child from birth. Am I 
"entitled" to undying servitude from that child as they reach adulthood? I 
gave my friend a $1000 gift. Am I "entitled" to receiving a similarly valued 
gift in return? My pizza arrived later than 30 minutes. Am I "entitled" to 
receiving it for free? etc.

    Ignoring omnipotence for now, one cannot "demand" respect anyway. One 
could demand an act which is intended to be symbolic of respect (e.g. 
demanding that I stand up), but short of mind control, one cannot demand 
respect itself (e.g. I could stand up, but resent the person who forced me 
to do so).

    God, being omnipotent, certainly has the *power* to demand respect. I 
don't know if He is entitled to respect. And if he actually does demand 
respect itself (as opposed to merely demanding an act symbolic of respect), 
does that invalidate free-will?

    - Oliver
```

```
Bugger! Sorry about the blank post... I haven't done that for a while... 
Anyway, here's the real response...

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> own?

Well, that entirely depends on how important respect is to you. You might 
take the view that if your creations are getting uppity they have probably 
grown considerably, and this might be a Good Thing... Personally, I would 
expect that God requiring respect from Man is a bit like me requiring 
respect from a cockroach...and I didn't even create cockroaches :-)
>
>>> Back to Jesus - He was conceived by the Holy Spirit inside a virgin - 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
OK. I give you that one.

>>> I definitely agree on your last statement above.  Let me ask you this - 
>>> how is evangelical Christianity treated in New Zealand?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's the COBOL market like over there?  ;)

Almost dead.

I don't know of anybody here who is still writing COBOL, apart from Richard, 
and myself (occasionally, and purely for my own purposes now).

J2EE is red hot. Ironically enough, the place where I'm working is currently 
a Unisys (A series) site, that uses LINC to generate COBOL. It is likely 
that this will be moved to a server platform and the LINC will become ABS(?) 
and generate C#... There are a number of things under consideration at the 
moment, including implementing a major package written in J2EE and Web 
Based.

I am managing a team of developers and BAs who are using J2EE.
>
>>>  Are cities allowed to put up Christmas trees without the threat of 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> summer!)

Well, not for us. We often go to the beach on Xmas day, have a picnic at the 
beach, and eat Xmas dinner in the evening when things are a bit cooler. (Not 
everybody does this, of course, but there are quite a few who do.)
>
>>> Are Bible clubs allowed in public high schools, and afforded the same 
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sadly, reason does not often play a part in getting legislation changed... 
It comes down to the emotional responses of the electorate and what will get 
the most votes. I guess that's the downside of Democracy. It's still a 
better system than the alternatives. :-)

>> Believe it or not, but in a 'humane and civilized' society, these crimes 
>> simply would not occur.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't believe human nature is naturally depraved. Or even 'sinful'. I 
think we start with a blank sheet when we're born, and our life experiences 
determine to a large extent what we write on it. If you have loving parents 
and a loving family it is likely you will be a loving person. If you see 
your parents respecting each other, and their friends, you will learn to 
behave similarly.  (There is an expression in Liverpool which always amused 
me: "They don't lick it off the stones..." It's true; they don't.) Sadly, 
the converse is also true. The pressures and influences of modern living 
seduce people and screw their values up so they think that working overtime 
to have this year's model car is more important than spending time helping 
the kids with their homework. We get damaged by life. We learn the wrong 
lessons. People we trusted let us down. We find that others don't share our 
ideals and aspirations. All of this contributes towards aberrating our good 
sense, optimism, and decency. We start to behave badly.

And yet, there is a part of us that can rise above all of it. Even at rock 
bottom, people have turned their lives around. The human spirit transcends 
the bad stuff. I see people in every day life behaving with courage and 
gentility. People going the extra mile to see someone else gets a better 
deal,  people sacrificing their time and energy gladly to assist others, 
raising money for the many worthy causes that we can never hope to saturate, 
or just being a friendly shoulder for someone whose load has become too 
heavy.

People are basically good. Give them half a chance and they'll do a good act 
in preference to a bad one.

I'm not ashamed of being human, and if God actually exists, and chooses to 
judge me for using the intelligence He (presumably) gave me, to arrive at a 
world view that does not include Him, well, I'll cross that bridge when I 
come to it :-)

Pete.

<snipped remainder as we agree on it :-)>
```

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-04-09T19:59:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49rt7cFq3k2vU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net> <fa0a$443476d4$45491d7a$27195@KNOLOGY.NET> <49ovolFplfm9U6@individual.net> <1e67a$4437e0ad$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:1e67a$4437e0ad$45491d7a$18967@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
<snip>

>> J2EE is red hot. Ironically enough, the place where I'm working is 
>> currently a Unisys (A series) site, that uses LINC to generate COBOL. It 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I had to smile when I read this...

I have spent the last couple of weeks calming down some management who have 
a bee in their bonnet about J2EE performance. Just when I think I can move 
on, Daniel asks the same question... :-)

You have to understand that the question was prompted in the first place 
purely by politics. The people asking have no idea what J2EE even stands for 
and have never programmed a computer in their lives. However, it is good for 
one's image to be seen as asking the 'hard' questions and it is especially 
good when you are trying to stop something new anyway...

J2EE, provided you wite it properly (just like any other language) will 
perform well.  Our system is using entity beans to store things on a 
database. There is no easily accessible data model, and looking at the 
physical database really tells you nothing. You give the data to a bean and 
it puts it away for you. The contract is that it will retrieve it for you 
whenever you want it. Because these beans contain certain generalised code, 
there may be some initialization that takes longer than writing directly to 
the DB would. The idea is a separation of the data layer from the business 
layer, but it comes at a price. However, this price is not outrageous (or 
even really dear, when you consider the advantages of this approach) and the 
system is still being developed.

I see no point in optimizing data access while things are still under 
development, but, due to political pressure that was becoming tiresome, I 
finally relented and did some optimization. It far exceeds the existing 
system in efficiency and that is using a DMS database which I understand is 
hierarchical (anyone from Unisys please correct me if my understanding here 
is wrong). The beans are accessing a DB2 relational DB.

I reported the results to "Concerned" who noted this was just one process. 
Yes, it was. But a major one. Not persuaded. His sources told him J2EE data 
access was poor. I asked which processes he was referring to. Oh, not in our 
system, in another system developed by someone else 4000 miles away. But it 
was J2EE so it must be the same, right?

I finally bought my freedom by promising to have the IBM laboratories do 
some testing and optimization (argument from Authority...works every time 
with people who are uninformed :-)) I also agreed that we will make 
performance review part of our RAD iterations of processes. (For all the 
good it will do... it is running on a local development server that bears no 
relationship whatsoever to the deployment environment. Just by doing some DB 
buffer tuning we improved the local performance by around 30%...) What bugs 
me about all this is that I have to comply with people who have no idea what 
the Hell they are talking  about and are being fed bullets as part of a 
political agenda. Never mind, it comes with the territory.

Bottom line: There is no more reason to have poor performance from Java than 
there is to have poor performance from anything else. Yes, in our particular 
case there are some overheads in data storage, but these are acceptable and 
can easily be compensated for by other measures.

Can I go now...? :-)

Pete
```

make it the only server I know of that may run its slowest right after

- **References:** `<icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <976e9$44222d15$45491d7a$11915@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvuj8j$ram$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <48sufcFlm38nU1@individual.net> <45cf6$443060c9$45491d7a$8817@KNOLOGY.NET> <49f9mrFoe8urU1@individual.net> <52038$443325d5$45491d7a$8233@KNOLOGY.NET> <49i2lrFnupfrU2@individual.net>`

On Thu, 6 Apr 2006 02:28:44 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:452h621m8qhm9kfmq5tbk3p9fqd4b2dsti@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 6 Apr 2006 02:28:44 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> But not against men?

OK, against the weak and defenceless... (this isn't quite the same. There is 
an idea I had instilled in me that women and children should be protected 
and nurtured. Old-fashioned, I know, but I adhere to it...)
>
>>or murders innocent people by acts of terrorism:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Brave things" like leaving a parcel in a crowded pub and killing and 
maiming teenagers who are enjoying a quiet Sunday drink?  "Brave things" 
like flying aircraft into buildings, in the certain knowledge of going to 
Heaven? Or, machine gunning school children in the furtherance of a 
political cause?

Sorry Howard, I know who the terrorists are, and I don't need some American 
politician to decide for me.

I lived in London while it was under attack from such people; they call 
themselves 'soldiers' but it is an affront to anyone who has ever been a 
soldier.  Soldiers don't murder innocent civilians (or even enemy who have 
laid down their weapons).

I'd be perfectly happy to see such people put against a wall and shot like 
the 'soldiers' they claim to be.

> If someone murders innocent people by acts of greed, or acts of war,
> or acts of terrorism, the result is the same, so why make the
> distinction when picking punishments?
>

For the same reason that we make a distinction between someone stealing food 
from a supermarket and someone stealing cash from the same supermarket. 
Because the Law is required to consider the whole picture and temper justice 
with mercy.

Except for terrorism... :-)

Pete.
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:tn0h62psgjkilpf4il4mnnn63er513enab@4ax.com...

> But aren't all English language scriptures adjusted?

Yes.  Even the English text of the Book of Mormon has had some grammatical 
rough edges -- rectifying ambiguities in some cases -- cleaned up since its 
original publication in 1829!  ;-)

Seriously, there's a supportable argument that even the "original texts" 
(either Old or New Testament) have not survived without any shred of 
controversy as to their literal, or for that matter theological, content. 
There is no "the" Hebrew/Aramaic text for the Old Testament (though many use 
the Masoretic Text), nor is there a "the" text for the New (though some hold 
the Textus Receptus as the culmination of God's effort to rectify the 
problems in the Greek, and others hold with the most recent edition of the 
UBS/Nestle-Aland text as representing the best scholarship).

But in any event I try to refer back to what most (not all) scholars hold is 
most likely closest to "original text", in the original languages, with side 
glances at other witnesses to their contents (the Septuagint in Greek for 
the Old Testament, and primarily the Vulgate and other early Latin witnesses 
for the New).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-27T14:13:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e09o2t$1fhi$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <ZfpSf.152819$B94.42600@pd7tw3no> <8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
*"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET...

>> My understanding of the passage is that Jesus is indicating *His role* in 
>> *facilitating* such communications -- whether we as individuals recognize 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the point of the passage is pretty much the same.

No, that's *exactly* what I'm trying to say -- the point of the passage is 
*quite* different depending on how you understand the word "through".

In the one case, it's Jesus' actions -- whether we ask Him for them or not, 
whether we acknowledge his role in the communication or not, and indeed 
whether we are aware of his role or not, -- that provides for communication 
with the Father.

In the other case, what you end up with is God the Father playing a game of 
"Simon Says" -- Jesus saying God the Father won't listen to *anybody's* 
cries unless Jesus is specifically and explicitly invoked in the wording of 
those cries.  I do not believe in a petty God.

I know a number of people striving to recover from substance abuse who have 
gone from the premise "there is no God" to the premise "there is a God, and 
I ain't it".  I for one think that's a tremendous step, and when I hear 
someone come to that point, I personally rejoice in their progress, and I 
happen to think God rejoices as well.

On the other hand, I have seen Christians tell people who have just shared 
that particular piece of progress in their spiritual life in 
twelve-step-program meetings " ... but unless and until you accept Jesus as 
your personal Savior, the actual Son of God, the Second Person of the Holy 
Trinity and the promised Messiah to the Jews -- the world would be better 
off if you grabbed a gallon of Everclear and drank yourself to death 
tonight.  There is no point to any effort you might make to straighten your 
life out unless you *start* by accepting Jesus as Messiah, as Savior, as 
God.  What you're doing isn't good enough in God's eyes.  ..." and so forth. 
"You didn't do it right, you didn't do it good enough" isn't likely to be a 
helpful message, and it comes down to "You didn't do it the way *I* think 
you ought to have done it."   Do I think such attitudes and actions reflect 
well on Christians, on Christianity, or on God?  No, I don't.

What God deems as sufficient spiritual growth is ultimately up to God. 
I've known many people who did their level best to live in harmony and peace 
with their fellow man and with their understanding of the Creator, and among 
them I can number *some* Christians.   But the more self-proclaimed they 
are, the less likely I am to find it comfortable spending time with them, 
because they're more likely to be telling everyone around them how correct 
*their* views are and how wrong *everybody else's* views are.

Stated another way, the more someone takes the tack or exhibits the attitude 
"I know The Truth about God, whereas you, you poor schlub ..." the less 
likely I am to listen to them, and the less likely I am to encourage others 
to listen to them.  Even if their presentation of detail facts matches my 
understanding, they're presenting the message in a way that shows they're 
trying to make *themselves* look good.    Sets my teeth on edge.  At the 
very least.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Richard wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Instead of playing word games let's agree to disagree, but I'll try and 
make this real simple for you.

Firstly full marks to the Calgary Herald and Naomi Lakritz, to whom I've 
already referred. US born her father was a GP in the States - her 
political ideology grew from her childhood. She saw her father turn away 
patients because they couldn't pay. She, on a weekly basis writes an 
interesting, easily read and provocative column on various current 
topics - there's little of what she writes that I would disagree with.

The Herald and Naomi make a point of posting 'interesting' letters and 
they don't balk at 'interesting' rebuttals. (I don't know - but I'd put 
my money on the possibility that Naomi doesn't go near a synagogue. 
Surprisingly, she has just done a book review - in the title the word 
'Jesus' occurs).

Letters get posted on a variety of topics, possibly, environment, 
women's rights to control their own bodies, same sex-marriages, the 
legal system, (by no mean restricted to some of the controversials I 
have listed). The respondents are easy to identify - whether the 
original letter made inference, direct or indirect to *anything* 
associated with the word 'religion'. They are from the secular world, 
(people who may have been church/temple/mosque-goers as kids and have 
given up on religion, agnostics or atheists - sometimes identifying 
themselves as such).

In their definition of democracy the word 'religion' is an absolute 
No-No. "Practice what you want, but not religion". Responses for the 
most part are very often sarcastic/terse, a few have a vindictive tone. 
Sometimes I check their names to confirm the authors - the letters might 
just have easily been written by a Richard Plinston.

'Here endeth the lesson'. No more from me.

Jimmy
```

station shows South Park. The local chapter of the 'Cult of Mary'
started complaining that the episode 'Bloody Mary' was going to be
shown in a couple of months time, and it should be banned. C4 responded

Not only that but many said they stayed on that channel to watch the
Pink Floyd doco and will return to see the 'classic albums series' they
didn't know about before.

over what _I_ could watch.  Not that I wanted to see South Park, but I
object to 'book burning'.

else on and I can't be bothered doing anything constructive. I find both of 
these mildly amusing and sometimes actually funny.

I was interested in your point about the 'Classic Albums' series. I only 
found it by accident on C4 but was blown away by Fleetwood Mac 'Rumours' and 
'Bat out of Hell', which have always been two of my personal favourites.

seemed to run out of ideas a while back, but the latest ones have been

pertinent observations there :-)).

Pete.

On 17 Mar 2006 17:34:39 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>People are offended by many different things. The local Music TV
>station shows South Park. The local chapter of the 'Cult of Mary'
>started complaining that the episode 'Bloody Mary' was going to be
>shown in a couple of months time, and it should be banned.

You've read about Isaac Hayes quitting South Park.   He was quoted
saying:

"Religious beliefs are sacred to people, and at all times should be
respected and honored," he continued. "As a civil rights activist of
the past 40 years, I cannot support a show that disrespects those
beliefs and practices."

The producers of South Park responded:

"South Park" co-creator Matt Stone responded sharply in an interview
with The Associated Press Monday, saying, "This is 100 percent having
to do with his faith of Scientology... He has no problem ï¿½ and he's
cashed plenty of checks ï¿½ with our show making fun of Christians."

Stone told The AP he and co-creator Trey Parker "never heard a peep
out of Isaac in any way until we did Scientology. He wants a different
standard for religions other than his own, and to me, that is where
intolerance and bigotry begin."

LX-i wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
>> Firstly full marks to the Calgary Herald and Naomi Lakritz, to whom
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> for some...)

I'm almost done with "Misquoting Jesus." Nothing new there, but he does 
gather into one volume the problems with early transcriptions and 
translations. An observation I thought was interesting was the reverse of 
Occam's Razor.

One way of stating Occam's Razor is: "When presented with two explanations, 
the simpler one is usually correct." In comparing early biblical texts, 
reverse the rule: the more complex text is closer to the original. It seems 
as if early copyists tried to simplify complex arguments and changed the 
received text to match their understandings.

As for not being "real," well there are two answers for that: In the 
Catholic tradition, the New Testament is a mere artifact. The real authority 
belongs with the Church as an institution and dogma is Church determined. In 
the non-catholic tradition, "God would not have allowed a corruption of His 
word. Any 'changes' to the received text were done with his blessing. We 
have, today, exactly what He wants us to have" is the operative theory.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-21T13:44:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dvps4j$11b8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET> <jrgl12hrl2n7mveoktib2nq6u88g4i2r9s@4ax.com> <xHCSf.151691$sa3.67280@pd7tw1no> <1142625209.403821.79630@p10g2000cwp.googlegroups.com> <2fFSf.152115$sa3.138957@pd7tw1no> <1142637660.494121.272190@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <qvISf.152530$sa3.52881@pd7tw1no> <8e51$441c3875$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <121pkt9ac91gu89@news.supernews.com>`

```

"HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:121pkt9ac91gu89@news.supernews.com...

> As for not being "real," well there are two answers for that: In the 
> Catholic tradition, the New Testament is a mere artifact. The real 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> operative theory.

I'm afraid it's a lot more complicated than that.

The Roman Catholic Church does not dismiss Scripture, Old or New Testament, 
as a mere artifact; what it does is accept the tradition of the Church, as 
having equal value with it.  It also accepts the ability of the Pope and the 
body of Bishops to speak inerrantly in matters of doctrine and dogma.

The "received text" -- by which I presume you mean the "Textus Receptus" as 
originally collected by Erasmus in 1516 from the available Greek 
manuscripts, being the first Greek text published using movable type -- was 
so named in the publisher's foreword to the 1633 edition and is used to 
refer to that edition by Elzevir and to the predecessor 1550 edition by 
Stephanus.

It is *by no means* a universal perception among non-Catholics that the TR 
specifically, or the latest manuscripts in general, are more definitive than 
the earliest ones.  The various editions of the National Bible Society and 
the Nestle-Aland work are fundamentally non-Catholic exercises, and various 
Nestle-Aland editions underlie a number of the modern English translations. 
Acceptance of the Textus Receptus recension of the New Testament as 
"received from God" is a minority position among Protestants, and the 
Orthodox tradition tends to accept the Septuagint recension of the Old 
Testament, older than the Masoretic Text of the Old Testament accepted by 
Judaism and by most Protestants.

Many Catholic and non-Catholic scholars prefer working back toward the most 
likely *earliest* Greek text of the New Testament, sometimes using datable 
examples in other languages when those examples predate the extant Greek 
manuscripts.  If I recall correctly, the "shortest text" philosophy 
underlies the Jerusalem Bible, accepted by some Catholics and Protestants 
alike, which includes a number of cases in which *no* Greek text actually 
matches the translation.

And while Catholics and Protestants agree as to what books the New Testament 
includes and what it does not, Luther himself rejected several 
hitherto-accepted books, and some parts of the Orthodox tradition delete 
some books and add others that the West does not accept.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

I find it interesting that most Santa Clause movies teach the lesson
that, even though adults don't believe in Santa Clause - Belief is
very important.

Why is Belief in a fictional character important?

Our society says respects people with other religious beliefs.   Their
right to believe the wrong mystical beliefs is important.   If a Soux
says a hill is sacred, then it *is* sacred, by gum.    But an atheist
doesn't have any excuse to keep his hill pristine.
```

> I find it interesting that most Santa Clause movies teach the lesson

Interesting one. As per lot of old texts in Hindu religion this world
is 'maya' (illusion). Whatever mind thinks and believes, he sees the
world in that way, and it has real effect on this world. The story of
Santa distributing gifts to children, I think is to put hope and joy in
children so that they see the world as better place.
IMO, the world can be manipulated individually upto a certain extent(in
mind). To take it further, if human mind gets attached, then there are
reactions (cause and effect). Some of my (is it truly me) thoughts on
the subject...
PNR

PradeepR wrote:

> The story of
> Santa distributing gifts to children, I think is to put hope and joy in
> children so that they see the world as better place.

Actually, Santa and the tooth fairy, and in fact all religions, are
social control mechanisms:

"Don't cry about your tooth hurting, you will scare away the tooth
fairy, be good and you will be rewarded."

"Santa only brings presents to children who are good and he knows when
you are bad."

"Obey these rules, confess your sins, do as I (the church) tell you,
and you will be rewarded later (or at least not burned on a stake by
us)."

The mystical bit is that the deity, or helpers, can see you whereever
you are, these days CCTV is required.
```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:35e00$441a18b1$45491d7a$29873@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> Simon & Schuster Inc. plans to save $1 million a year on hardware costs 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> platform.

I happen to agree with you here!

> I believe they support up to 16 processors in one box (it may be 32 
> processors by now).

The first 16-processor ES7000 was shipped on May 15, 2000; the first 
32-processor system was shipped about a month later.   So far as I know, 
32-way systems have been orderable since the introduction of the product 
line.   They have evolved to incorporate the latest Intel processor 
offerings.

>
> They're very cool - and, I believe they also run Unisys's A-series (LX) 
> operating system.  For those folks, they can get cheaper hardware *and* no 
> conversion...  :)

These days, it's only the very top models of the MCP-based systems that use 
"A Series" hardware.  The smallest such systems run on "commodity" hardware 
(including laptops); the medium/large models run s on various sorts of 
ES7000 systems.   The upper end of the power range for the "medium/large" 
models overlaps the lower end of the "very top" models *significantly*.

> They haven't left us out in the cold, though.  Now, instead of paying for 
> the capability of the hardware, we pay for the MIPS we actually use. It's 
> significantly cheaper, while allowing us to have the power on demand if we 
> need it.

"Capacity on demand" is indeed a strong point of the latest ClearPath Plus 
servers.

> More than you ever wanted to know about Unisys, probably...  :)  I just 
> had to give props to the ES7000's - I wish I could get one for my house! 
> (although I have no idea what I'd do with it...  They run Linux too - 
> maybe that would be my terminal server!)

Well, if you can't afford a 32-way, maybe your local Unisys sales rep can 
talk you into a 4-way system?       ;-)

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-16T20:30:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1142569811.854034.268760@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<121kdnh713lvbeb@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <441A2D9E.3B3DE4FF@comcast.net> <121kdnh713lvbeb@news.supernews.com>`

```
> Micros~1 Windows is being booted thousands of times

Yeah, I find that I have to boot it thousands of times, too.      ;-)

In 2000 it was discovered that Win95, 98 and ME would lock up after
being up for exactly 39 days and some hours, being an integer roll over
problem. The joke was that it took 5 years before anyone had managed to
keep one up that long without having to reboot.
```

My home computer locks up more often than it reboots itself.   I start
it up by turning it off with the power button.

The computer shop has replaced the hard drive, replaced the video
card, and replaced the power supply.   I have removed the Iomega drive
and its drivers and replaced the monitor.    Microsoft gets the big
dumps (what do they do with all the aborts that get e-mailed to it?).

Spending lots of money without solving anything reminds me of
government.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Oliver Wong" <owong@castortech.com>
- **Date:** 2006-03-17T14:37:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2RzSf.939$me6.880@clgrps13>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <441A2D9E.3B3DE4FF@comcast.net> <121kdnh713lvbeb@news.supernews.com> <441A5A58.5B609BD3@comcast.net>`

```
"Charles Richmond" <richchas@comcast.net> wrote in message 
news:441A5A58.5B609BD3@comcast.net...
>> > Anyone who has *anything* important running on their computers...
>> > should *never* use a program from Mi$uck to do it. Instead, they
>> > should get a program from a *reliable* and *trustworthy* software
>> > company.
>> >
[snip]
> Yes, I was forced to work on Mi$uck Windows before. We had to reboot
> *several* times a day at my place of employment. So I guess that boosts
> the Mi$uck number of daily boots.  ;-)  One of the guys who was running
> Windows NT had to *re-install* his system a couple of times a month. It
> took him *all* afternoon to accomplish a re-install.
[snip]
> I'm *not* sure what you mean here...but I'm sticking with my Mac and
> Linux boxes thank you very much.

    If your goal is to promote the adoption of Linux and Mac over Windows, 
you should probably not do it by saying "*I* have had bad experiences with 
Windows, that's why *YOU* should switch".

    Imagine it from the perspective of the person you're preaching to. 
Perhaps they have a system which is running Windows XP just fine, which they 
haven't had to reinstall for maybe 2 or 3 years now, and when they did 
install, it was a quick 30 minute process, in which only say 7 of those 
minutes actually required user interaction. After hearing you talk about 
"number of daily boots", "re-install a couple of times a month", "all 
afternoon to accomplish a re-install", they might dismiss your situation as 
being completely alien to theirs, and thus your advice would not be 
applicable to them.

    Note that I'm not saying that you're lying about having problems with 
Windows; I'm saying just because YOU'VE had problems, why should that mean 
OTHER people have to change their OSes?

    - Oliver
```

```

Oliver Wong wrote:
>     If your goal is to promote the adoption of Linux and Mac over Windows,
> you should probably not do it by saying "*I* have had bad experiences with
> Windows, that's why *YOU* should switch".

I didn't see where he was promoting any particular syatem beyond
mentioning what he used. What he did say was that it is provvable that
millions have bad experiences with Windows systems which is why there
is such a thriving industry in anti-virus, anti-spyware and firewall
add-ons to cover over the poor design and implementation.

>     Imagine it from the perspective of the person you're preaching to.
> Perhaps they have a system which is running Windows XP just fine, which they
> haven't had to reinstall for maybe 2 or 3 years now,

Oh yeah ? name one    ;-)

> and when they did
> install, it was a quick 30 minute process, in which only say 7 of those
> minutes actually required user interaction.

Actually he said system. This would also include all the software that
he needed beyond the bare OS.  Also he said NT which does take longer
than XP. And then you have to apply all the patches and service packs
to try to make it less vulnerable to infection by malware, turn off all
the annoyances, configure the applications, restore the backed up
files.

> After hearing you talk about
> "number of daily boots", "re-install a couple of times a month", "all
> afternoon to accomplish a re-install", they might dismiss your situation as
> being completely alien to theirs,

And those that have spent half a day getting a system back to where
they want it will be just as dismissive of your 'quick 30 minutes'.

>     Note that I'm not saying that you're lying about having problems with
> Windows; I'm saying just because YOU'VE had problems, why should that mean
> OTHER people have to change their OSes?

I am sure that there are millions of people using Windows with no
problems at all, no security issues, no failures, no lock ups at all
ever.  There are, however, millions (well at least 10s of thousands) of
people running Windows who are unknowingly zombies in spam remailing
networks, and/or are infected with virusses and worms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 6)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2006-03-17T15:29:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<441B2A4A.1CD9439F@mts.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <441A2D9E.3B3DE4FF@comcast.net> <121kdnh713lvbeb@news.supernews.com> <441A5A58.5B609BD3@comcast.net> <2RzSf.939$me6.880@clgrps13> <1142626524.283856.319400@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

> 
> I am sure that there are millions of people using Windows with no
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> networks, and/or are infected with virusses and worms.

It may be interesting to note that there is a whole computing universe
out there in which many of these problems simply don't occur.  I'll
instance two machines that were absolutely bullet-proof when it came to
lockups or programs interfering with each other or memory leaks or
crashing the system.  None of these ever happened, so long as the
machine was not physically malfunctioning.  These were the IBM S/36, and
the various systems running PRIMOS.  IBM, of course, was once the
biggest computer company in the known universe, but Prime, although it
got into the top 500 for a while, has long since vanished and never had
resources comparable to IBM.  My point is that such operating systems
can and have been built.  Whether the difference is in the resources, or
the ability and span of control of the people in charge of the
development, or even the architecture of the machines, doesn't matter -
if it can be done at all, it ought to be achievable by all!  

I imagine that AS/400's are pretty tough, too.  How about IBM or Unisys
mainframes?

(A Prime systems engineer told me that he'd heard of a couple of virus
attempts.  But nothing ever came of them.  I'm not claiming immunity
from viruses is possible - any system can be cracked, I imagine, if the
necessary effort is taken).

(I do most of my work under WIN98 because that's what the PC came with
and I don't want the aggravation of upgrading and new problems (that's
to say, not before I have to!)  It is stable enough for what I need and
will run everything that I need.  Yet it locks up every couple of days,
sometimes when switching from one app to another, sometimes after trying
to to wake up from standby.  After standby, I can't print - although
that may be a driver problem, not a MS problem; still that can hardly be
a situation that wasn't anticipated.  And sometimes it won't shut down!)

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-03-18T15:13:23+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4818m5Fh69inU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <441A2D9E.3B3DE4FF@comcast.net> <121kdnh713lvbeb@news.supernews.com> <441A5A58.5B609BD3@comcast.net> <2RzSf.939$me6.880@clgrps13> <1142626524.283856.319400@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <441B2A4A.1CD9439F@mts.net>`

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:441B2A4A.1CD9439F@mts.net...
> Richard wrote:
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So, it ISN'T really "stable enough for what I need and will run everything 
that I need." is it?  Because it won't do it all of the time. If you need it 
to work at a time when it is locking up, it isn't meeting your needs.  It IS 
stable enough for you to get useful work from it, but it is far from perfect 
or even "pretty good".

What happens is that we all come to accept what our systems do and find 
workarounds for the problems.

I understand your hesitance to upgrade (been there myself...). I often build 
PCs for friends and charities where the user doesn't have much available 
cash, so I scrounge old boxes and bits and pieces. Win 98 (SE, NOT ME) was a 
very good OS but XP is much more stable. I used to immediately effect an 
upgrade to XP until I found that some of the old boxes simply couldn't 
support it. Win98 will run reasonably happily in 64MBs; XP won't. So then it 
becomes a memory upgrade.  (Sometimes with older boxes, the memory is very 
hard to get...). Then you find that a 5 year old HD is starting to throw bad 
sectors and getting nearer to its MTBF so you might want to replace that. 
Even if you can acquire a smaller (say 10 GB) hard drive for a nominal sum 
(I found a computer shop in Tauranga the other day who let me have one they 
had removed from an old machine for $15 (when they knew I was doing it for a 
worthy cause)), there are still risks.

The one I'm proudest of is a system I built for a disabled lady who wanted 
to do a remote study writing course. She has a fully functioning XP system 
with XP Office (all legal) that connects over broadband, and the whole 
system cost $NZ104. So far it has performed flawlessly for about 3 months; I 
have my fingers crossed... :-) All of the hardware was begged borrowed or 
recycled from other old systems that were non-functional. It took me 30 
hours to get the bits and build it. :-)

The bottom line is that if you have a machine running Win 98, you are 
PROBABLY better to bite the bullet and replace the whole thing, than attempt 
to upgrade.

Pete.
```

Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>The one I'm proudest of is a system I built for a disabled lady who wanted 
>to do a remote study writing course. She has a fully functioning XP system 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>hours to get the bits and build it. :-)

This reminds me of a put-it-together-yourself file cabinet I helped a 
physician I know assemble back in the early 1990s.  This stuff has 
improved since then and now it's more difficult to attach part B to part D 
instead of hooking it to part C, where it belongs... and we'd knock a few 
pieces together, realise we'd done the wrong ones, take it apart, set it 
aright...

... all in all it took us about three hours.  Sipping our Pilsner Urquels 
and gazing upon the finished product the doctor mused 'You know... between 
the cost of your time and mine that is, per cubic inch, probably the most 
expensive piece of furniture in this house.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments?

_(reply depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **Message-ID:** `<482ij3Fi1pp7U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <1142626524.283856.319400@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <441B2A4A.1CD9439F@mts.net> <4818m5Fh69inU1@individual.net> <dvgjem$aaq$1@reader2.panix.com>`

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:dvgjem$aaq$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <4818m5Fh69inU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
LOL! Very true... sometimes we have to donate our time and then it is beyond 
price :-)

I was very impressed to see you drank Pilsner Urquels :-) This could be 
considered the "original" lager beer. Pilsen used to be part of Germany and 
is where they invented what is referred to in Germany as 'Pils' and in 
England as 'lager'. (Although no English lagers that I know of are brewed 
according to the Reinheitsgebot (purity laws formulated in the 17th century 
in Germany, and forbidding the adulteration of beer with anything other than 
hops, malt, yeast and water. Sadly, 'lager' is simply chemical beer, and 
contains enzymes, rice, and fillers and they brew a batch of it in around 2 
days. In Germany it takes three weeks of natural fermentation.)

I have visited the town of Pilsen (now in the Czech Republic) and found it 
to be very interesting.

I drink Pilsner Urquels whenever I can get my hands on it, but, in NZ, that 
is not very frequently... :-(

Pete.
> DD
>
```

tasteless schlock). When I used to do home-brew, the better beers and
wines were the slow fermentation brews.

I drink lots of chemical beer when I can afford it (I am a lager-lout,
bitter-lout and a stout-lout) but prefer Czech Budweiser Budvar over
Urquell Pilsner.

    To me, that's more than merely "I run Linux/Mac/whatever, and

    My home machine is like that.

>
>> and when they did
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> files.

    The same is true of Linux. More than once have I waited 2 or 3 hours for 
Debian to download and install all the packages. I've played around with 
different distributions, and so far I like Ubuntu the best, but I'm not 
quite ready to "give up" Windows in exchange for it yet.

    There was one distribution, I think it was Mandrake but I'm not sure, 
where after installing, it said it was going to connect to the Internet and 
update itself. So I said fine. Then it told me I needed to create a 
"Mandrake Star Account" or something like that to get the updates. Okay, 
fine. Then the update agent crashed. Well, no big deal, so I go to the 
Mandrake website and fill in the registration, and it says it'll send an 
e-mail to my account containing my password. A few moments later, I get the 
password, type it in, and it says it can't find my account. I've double 
checked for typos, and I've asked it to reset my password half a dozen 
times, still no luck. Couldn't get my Mandrake account setup, and so I 
couldn't update my software. Spent several days on the problem before giving 
up.

    I also find Linux to be far less forgiving when "configuring 
everything". What kind of video drivers should I use? Well, my video card 
manufacturer isn't listed there, so I'll just choose one randomly. Oops, big 
mistake. Now I can never get a display on my monitor again, unless I 
completely reformat and restart the installation process (surely there was a 
keystroke combination I could have pressed upon boot-up to force linux to 
boot into text-only mode, but it wasn't apparent to me at the time what that 
key-combination would be, and yes, I did try googling for it).

    What's your DHCP server? I don't have one. Linux won't accept that 
answer. Er, how about 192.168.0.1? Fine. Linux proceeds, but my network 
stack is FUBAR. Gotta re-install. Again, there was probably some command I 
could have typed, but Google and the "man pages" and the find command was 
not particularly helpful to me.

    The are just random anecdotes, and certainly I do NOT make the claim 
that this is the "average" experience of Linux users, or that this is what 
kind of experiences one should expect. I'm just saying that for every 
software that exists, there also exists a user who is going to, or who 
already has had, problems with it.

>
>> After hearing you talk about
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> they want it will be just as dismissive of your 'quick 30 minutes'.

    Except I never claimed that people should, for example, switch from 
Linux to Windows, so there isn't anything for them to dismiss! I suppose the 
OP could dismiss my advice of "when advocating, think of perspective of the 
people you're advocating to", and that's fine by me. I have no desire to see 
Linux fail. In fact, I'd much rather have everyone switch to linux, as it 
would increase the likely hood of me finding help on google when I search 
for something, and increase the likelyhood that my favorite apps will get 
ported over to Linux, and that I could finally switch and not have to pay 
for my OS anymore. That'd be great for me! That's why I'm trying to give 
advice on converting people.

>
>>     Note that I'm not saying that you're lying about having problems with
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> networks, and/or are infected with virusses and worms.

    Yes, both statements can be true at the same time. No dispute here.

    - Oliver
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<S3GSf.88$nQ6.52@clgrps13>`
- **References:** `<47t5erFgvqllU1@individual.net> <121kdnh713lvbeb@news.supernews.com> <441A5A58.5B609BD3@comcast.net> <2RzSf.939$me6.880@clgrps13> <1142626524.283856.319400@z34g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <S3GSf.88$nQ6.52@clgrps13>`

```

Oliver Wong wrote:

> > I didn't see where he was promoting any particular syatem beyond
> > mentioning what he used. What he did say was that it is provvable that
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>     To me, that's more than merely "I run Linux/Mac/whatever, and

As I said, he was not promoting any particular system, even though he
was advocating  to not use Windows.

> incidentally, millions of people have problems with Windows."

It is _not_ 'incidental' that millions have actual and serious problems
with Windows.

> times, still no luck. Couldn't get my Mandrake account setup, and so I
> couldn't update my software. Spent several days on the problem before giving
> up.

Was that a problem with the OS or the web site ?

> (surely there was a
> keystroke combination I could have pressed upon boot-up to force linux to
> boot into text-only mode, but it wasn't apparent to me at the time what that
> key-combination would be, and yes, I did try googling for it).

Yes there is, but you don't even need to do that.  Usually there are 8
'consoles' available with only one started with the GUI, Use
Ctrl-Alt-Fn to switch to a text mode console and log in as root there.
The GUI is usually running on console 7 so Ctrl-Alt-F7 put you back to
KDE or Gnome or whatever.

>     What's your DHCP server? I don't have one. Linux won't accept that
> answer. Er, how about 192.168.0.1? Fine. Linux proceeds, but my network
> stack is FUBAR. Gotta re-install.

If you don't run a DHCP server then, just as with Windows, you specify
a manual address for the machine and its gateway and DNS server (eg
your ISP).

And, no you don't need to reinstall, just as with Windows, you
configure the network to take a fixed IP address and set the gateway
and DNS.  Unlike Windows there is no need to reboot to take the new
settings.

> Again, there was probably some command I
> could have typed, but Google and the "man pages" and the find command was
> not particularly helpful to me.

Most distros have GUI configuration for network and most other stuff,
but the command line is ifconfig if you need for scripted
reconfiguration (which I do).  cf Windows ipconfig which was copied
from BSD when they used the TCP/IP stack from that OS.

>     The are just random anecdotes, and certainly I do NOT make the claim
> that this is the "average" experience of Linux users, or that this is what
> kind of experiences one should expect. I'm just saying that for every
> software that exists, there also exists a user who is going to, or who
> already has had, problems with it.

Absolutely. Linux users have to know what they are doing, but Windows
users can specify the wrong video card driver too and screw up their
system. When XP came all the icons and menus changed, I had to relearn
where everything was (when I was forced to) and this was no different
from going to anther OS.

> > And those that have spent half a day getting a system back to where
> > they want it will be just as dismissive of your 'quick 30 minutes'.
>
>     Except I never claimed that people should, for example, switch from
> Linux to Windows, so there isn't anything for them to dismiss!

No, but you did dismiss his 'all afternoon', and I dismiss your claim
it could be done in 30 minutes.
```

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1142639359.645645.182640@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>
> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Was that a problem with the OS or the web site ?

    Probably the web site. I had initially used Mandrake's "proprietary 
client" to try to login. To try to isolate the problem, I then went back to 
my WinXP machine and tried resetting my password via the website, and then 
checking my e-mail for the "click here to reset your password" link, but it 
never arrived.

[random anecdotes snipped]

> Absolutely. Linux users have to know what they are doing, but Windows
> users can specify the wrong video card driver too and screw up their
> system.

    That's just the thing though: I think Windows user CANNOT specify the 
wrong video card. Nowhere in the install process for WindowsXP am I ever 
asked "What video card are you using"? Windows just "makes a guess", and so 
far, it has always guessed correctly on every system I've ever built 
(including those I've built for friends and family).

> When XP came all the icons and menus changed, I had to relearn
> where everything was (when I was forced to) and this was no different
> from going to anther OS.

    The learning curve from 3.1 to 98 was essentially zero for me. Similarly 
for 98 to 2000, and 2000 to XP. I wouldn't be surprised if the same thing 
happens when I go from XP to Vista.

    The curve from XP to MacOSX was a bit shaky, but not too bad. There's 
that moment of panic when you want to do something other than merely open 
(i.e. double click), select (i.e. single click) or move (i.e. drag) an icon, 
and realize that you don't have a right mouse button. But if google for it, 
you'll quickly find a chart with all the keyboard shortcuts to do whatever 
it is you were trying to do (e.g. create aliases, copy a file, create new 
folders, change the way icons are arranged, etc.)

    And for the most parts, things simply don't seem to "go wrong" in either 
Windows or Mac (in my experience, anyway). When I go into the control panel 
(and whatever the equivalent in OSX is called), and just randomly fiddle 
around with the settings that "seem safe", I don't end up seriously mucking 
up my system.

    With almost every Linux distribution I've ever worked with, I'm almost 
afraid to experiment, because it seems like the most innocent changes can 
have system wide repercussions. There's some sort of licensing issue which 
prevents mp3 players from being bundled with many linux distributions, for 
example. Well, I really want to be able to listen to mp3s. So I tell the 
package manager please do whatever it is you need to do to get mp3-playing 
working. It asks some vaguely dangerous sounding questions (something along 
the lines of "broken dependencies") but all the questions Linux asks me 
sound dangerous, so I say yes anyway. So it says it has to update my 
XWindow, so it's gonna switch to text mode now. Okay, fine. But now X is 
completely broken and I can't get back into the GUI anymore. So I tell the 
package manager to uninstall that mp3 thing it did. More warnings about 
broken dependencies. Yes, yes, fine, do whatever you want. Just get rid of 
that mp3 thing. Turns out I'm just digging myself into deeper and deeper 
trouble, breaking more and more components.

>> > And those that have spent half a day getting a system back to where
>> > they want it will be just as dismissive of your 'quick 30 minutes'.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> it could be done in 30 minutes.

    If I had a spare system, I'd try filming myself installing WinXP, 
setting up an internet connection, getting firefox, and installing Office, 
and optionally Apache HTTP server if there was time left, within 30 minutes.

    - Oliver
```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message

>
> Oliver Wong wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…

Please Sir!...Oh Sir!... me Sir! I have NEVER had to re-install XP Pro on my 
home system since buying the system with it installed (OEM) 4 years ago. I 
Have had ONE BSOD in that time, and that was remedied easily by downloading 
the latest video driver. This system is used every day and sometimes runs 
for a week without rebooting (it is on a laptop and sometimes I leave it in 
Tauranga switched on while I am away in Auckland.) I come back and do NOT 
have to reboot it. It simply wakes up and continues like it should.

In the workoplace, (We are running XP) I often go for several weeks without 
rebooting. My workstation has never crashed. XP Pro is a stable and useful 
Operating System and the FIRST from MicroSoft that I have ever been happy 
with.

The point about all this is that, as Oliver pointed out, "one swallow does 
not make a Summer."

We all have our preferences and in the end, whatever works for us is fine. I 
am currently looking to replace the laptop with a platform that can run 
Vista. That doesn't mean I'm uninterested in Linux... it just means I have 
no reason to leave MicroSoft.

(BTW, I loved your thousand boots joke, Richard... :-))
>
>> and when they did
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hmmm... and none of the above would apply to people using Macs and 
Unix/Linux...?

I'm one of the millions with no issues, but that doesn't mean I am a 
fanatical MS supporter (or detractor...). I go with what works. If Vista 
turns out to be a heapof crap I'll be on Linux or some other box faster than 
you can say "fickle user".:-) It is nice to have options...

Pete.

HeyBub wrote:

> > So it is the _users_ fault that Windows has security problems,
> > viruses, spyware, backdoors, design flaws, and general failures.
>
> Pretty much. Viruses, for example don't miracle themselves into a PC

Actually, they do.  Tests done with Windows XP (prior to SP2) showed
that a fresh install with all MS defaults connected to the internet
would be 'pwned' within 5 minutes or so. Granted SP2 set the firewall
on.

There were many faults in Outlook that would install viruses merely by
clicking on an email, and it was necessary to click to delete it.

Merely visiting sites would be enogh to become infected.

Servers with IIS with settings as per MS install, would propagate many
trojans and viruses.

Even playing a music CD from Sony would install a rootkit without the
user being even aware of it.

> If you mean your post alleging Windows will only run 39 days and, since no
> one had been able to keep Windows running that long, the bug was never
> found, I attributed that to hyperbole. I can find no Google reference to
> such a condition.

It was actually 49.6 days now that I check. This is when the timer tick
would overflow the size of an integer and the system would lockup. The
problem was fixed by a patch and was fixed in 98 SE.

> As for "courts of law" being unbiased arbiters of truth: bah!

Oh quite, much better to rely on Bush to tell us 'The Truth!!'.    ;-)

> >> On the other hand, I did get a $1,600.00 checky-poo from Micros~1
>
> Pretty smart of me, isn't it?

But then you have probably sent them more than that amount one way or
another.

"Joe Zitzelberger" <zberger@knology.net> wrote in message 
news:zberger-25DBFE.04433618032006@ispnews.usenetserver.com...
> In article <121m6i8ggoeng8b@news.supernews.com>,
> "HeyBub" <heybubNOSPAM@gmail.com> wrote:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> of "good experiences" for its users...

Oh, I dunno, Joe...:-)

what about:

1. The Edsel. (A monument to ugliness.)
2. The Pinto. (At least Windows doesn't blow your arse off when it 
crashes...)
3. The NCR 500.(First machine I ever programmed. I still occasionally dream 
about it... nightmares.)
4. Any trip with Magic Bus. (I nearly took a job driving this from Kathmandu 
to London, once. It should be called "Miracle Bus", not "Magic Bus"...:-))
5. Any trip with Air France.
6. The Pol Pot regime.
7. Consultancy from Arthur Andersen.
8. Insurance from Phoenix.
9. The Starfighter. (German joke, after many of them crashed in training... 
How do you get a Starfighter? Buy a field and wait...)
10. Matai beer. (This was invented by the Maori before Europeans arrived. It 
is made from the fermented berries of a native tree called the matai. I 
drank some once (and they weren't even holding a gun on me at the time...) 
in a part of NZ called Taranaki, where men are men and women are glad... It 
is certainly the foulest tasting stuff you could ever imagine. I reckon the 
haka was invented to try and shake off the taste of it. It's the kind of 
beer that is SO bad you don't even think: "Man, this beer is bad; I'll be 
glad when I've had enough..." Best avoided.
11. The PSION II organiser. (Batteries could be relied on to fail at 
absolutely the most inopportune time. You had around 20 seconds to get the 
old battery out and the new one in. The pressure was immense, resulting in 
dropped batteries, lost data, and, on one occasion I witnessed in Australia, 
the device being hurled across the room with a string of invective.)
12. A Frank Ifield concert.
13. Women's cricket.
14. Posting code to CLC...
15. MicroFocus VISOC. (Not the product; what they did with it...)
16. Anything that happens on ice, with the possible exception of hockey, and 
female 'ice dancing' solos.
17. The food in Sweden.
18. The wine in Tunisia.
19. Visiting France.
20. Any Lada car PRIOR to the takeover by  Volkswagen.

All of the above are pretty much unlikely to produce a 'good experience' for 
their users. Personally, I'd rather use Windows than have any of the above 
inflicted on me :-)

Pete.
```

"Clark Morris" <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message 
news:h7go12dh993g1au08btvkcgdd56hbdjls7@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 19 Mar 2006 03:01:09 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
